topics.title: Introduction to Automotive Embedded Systems: Basic Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT632 - AUTOMOTIVE EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Automotive Embedded Systems: Basic Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Automotive Embedded System (AES)** is a specialized, purpose-built computing subsystem permanently integrated within a vehicle's mechanical and electronic framework to monitor, control, and actuate real-time functions. Unlike general-purpose computers, an AES operates under **strict deterministic, safety-critical, and resource-constrained** conditions, executing dedicated firmware on **Electronic Control Units (ECUs)** interconnected via standardized **in-vehicle communication networks** (e.g., CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet).

According to the **ISO 26262** functional safety standard and the **AUTOSAR** (AUTomotive Open System ARchitecture) framework referenced in the KTU 2024 syllabus, every automotive embedded system must guarantee:

- **Real-time response** within deterministic deadlines (hard real-time constraints).
- **Fault tolerance** and graceful degradation under sensor/actuator failure.
- **Scalability** across Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) platforms.
- **Cybersecurity resilience** against modern attack vectors (as mandated by ISO/SAE 21434).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1, CO1):**
> Automotive Embedded Systems form the *intelligence layer* between the physical vehicle and the driver. The KTU 2024 scheme specifically defines them as *event-driven, reactive, and often safety-critical systems* that combine hardware (sensors/ECUs/actuators), real-time software (firmware/RTOS), and communication protocols.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Vehicle as a Human Body"

To a first-time learner, an automotive embedded system can be visualized as the **nervous system of a living organism**:

| Human Body Component | Automotive Embedded Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Brain** (Central Nervous System) | Central Gateway ECU / Domain Controller | Decision-making, coordination |
| **Spinal Cord** | In-Vehicle Communication Bus (CAN/LIN/FlexRay) | High-speed signal transfer |
| **Sensory Nerves** (eyes, skin) | Sensors (TPS, MAP, LiDAR, Camera, Radar) | Perceive physical environment |
| **Motor Nerves** (muscles) | Actuators (Injectors, Solenoids, Motors) | Execute physical actions |
| **Reflex Arcs** (knee-jerk) | Hard Real-Time Control Loops (e.g., ABS, ESC) | Ultra-fast autonomous reactions |
| **Memory** | EEPROM / Flash (Non-volatile Storage) | Retain learned parameters (KAM, Adaptives) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine a modern car is not a single machine, but a **collaborative swarm of 80–150 mini-computers (ECUs)** that constantly *talk* to each other over a digital "group chat" (the bus). When you press the brake pedal, a sensor *whispers* the pedal position to the brake ECU, which *shouts* a deceleration request to the engine ECU over the CAN bus, which then *cuts fuel and engages* the brake actuator — all within **5–10 milliseconds**.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics and Standards

The following quantitative benchmarks define the engineering envelope of an AES:

- **Operating Temperature Range:** **-40 °C to +125 °C** (AEC-Q100 Grade 1)
- **Vibration Tolerance:** 50 G peak (per ISO 16750-3)
- **ECU Count in a Premium Vehicle:** **80 to 150+ units**
- **Software Codebase:** **100 to 650+ million lines of code** (modern luxury/SEDAN platforms)
- **Bus Speeds:** CAN (1 Mbps), CAN-FD (5 Mbps), FlexRay (10 Mbps), Automotive Ethernet (1 Gbps, 10 Gbps emerging)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Scale Example:** A single 2024-segment premium sedan contains more **lines of code (≈ 650 million)** than a Boeing 787 Dreamliner (≈ 14 million) and a modern fighter jet (F-35 ≈ 24 million) **combined many times over**. This is why KTU 2024 dedicates an entire module to this domain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of an Automotive Embedded System

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to understand an AES as a **hierarchical stack**. Each layer has a discrete responsibility:

1. **Sensor Layer (Perception Layer)**
   - Converts physical phenomena (temperature, pressure, position, velocity) into analog/digital electrical signals.
   - Examples: Mass Air Flow (MAF) sensor, Wheel Speed Sensor (WSS), Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU), Hall-effect position sensor.

2. **ECU / Processing Layer (Computation Layer)**
   - Houses the **microcontroller (MCU)** or **microprocessor (MPU)**, often running an **AUTOSAR-compliant** or **OSEK/VDX real-time operating system (RTOS)**.
   - Executes control algorithms (e.g., PID loops for engine idle speed, Kalman filtering for sensor fusion).
   - Typical MCU families: Infineon AURIX (TC3xx), NXP S32K, Renesas RH850, STMicro SPC58.

3. **Actuator Layer (Execution Layer)**
   - Converts electrical control signals into physical mechanical action.
   - Examples: Fuel injector solenoid, Electronic Throttle Body (ETB) DC motor, ABS hydraulic modulator, EGR valve stepper motor.

4. **Communication Layer (Networking Layer)**
   - Inter-ECU messaging using standardized protocols.
   - Manages bus arbitration, error detection, and message prioritization.

5. **Application / Powertrain Coordination Layer**
   - High-level vehicle functions (Adaptive Cruise Control, Lane Keeping, Battery Management for EVs).

### 2.2 Why Real-Time Determinism is Non-Negotiable

A **brake-by-wire** system must react within **10 ms** to avoid a collision. A **fuel injection** event requires a timing accuracy of **±0.1° crank angle** (≈ ±10 microseconds at 6000 RPM). Therefore, KTU 2024 emphasizes that automotive embedded systems are **hard real-time**, meaning a missed deadline is considered a **system failure**, not a performance degradation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula / Expression | Unit | Practical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bus Utilization ($U$) | $U = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (C_i + T_{ovh})}{T_{cycle}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | < 0.30 (CAN), < 0.70 (FlexRay) |
| Worst-Case Response Time ($R_i$) | $R_i = J_i + W_i + C_i$ | ms | Must satisfy $R_i \le D_i$ |
| ECU Clock Frequency ($f_{clk}$) | $f_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | MHz / GHz | 32 – 600 MHz typical |
| Effective Wheel Torque | $\tau_{wheel} = \tau_{engine} \cdot \eta_{drivetrain} \cdot G_{gear}$ | N·m | 100 – 800 N·m |
| Sampling Period ($T_s$) for Nyquist | $T_s \le \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot f_{max}}$ | s | Engine knock: $\approx$ 50 µs |
| Message Latency on CAN | $t_{lat} = t_{prop} + t_{arb} + t_{frame}$ | µs | 50 – 200 µs (125 kbps bus) |
| Power Dissipation (ECU) | $P_{ECU} = V_{cc} \cdot I_{active}$ | W | 0.5 – 5 W per ECU |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **Worst-Case Response Time (WCRT)** formula $R_i = J_i + W_i + C_i$ is the cornerstone of schedulability analysis taught under CO1. A system is **schedulable** only if $R_i \le D_i$ for every task, where $D_i$ is the deadline.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems):** Sensor fusion of camera + radar + LiDAR.
- **EV Battery Management Systems (BMS):** Cell balancing, State-of-Charge (SoC) estimation, thermal management.
- **Engine Management:** Air-Fuel ratio control, variable valve timing, turbo boost regulation.
- **Chassis Control:** Electronic Stability Control (ESC), Active Suspension, Steer-by-Wire.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Software Complexity in a Modern Vehicle

A quantitative question frequently asked in KTU examinations is: *"Estimate the total lines of code (LOC) in a modern vehicle and compare it to a mobile OS."*

**Step 1 — Identify the distribution of software across domains**

A typical premium ICE/hybrid vehicle distributes its software as follows (per embedded.com industry surveys, used in KTU model solutions):

- Powertrain Control (Engine + Transmission): **≈ 38 million LOC**
- Chassis & Safety (ABS, ESC, Airbag): **≈ 22 million LOC**
- Body Electronics (BCM, Lighting, HVAC): **≈ 35 million LOC**
- Infotainment & HMI (IVI, Cluster, Telematics): **≈ 250 million LOC**
- ADAS & Autonomous Functions: **≈ 180 million LOC**
- Networking & Diagnostics (UDS, DoIP, OTA): **≈ 125 million LOC**

**Step 2 — Sum the individual contributions**

$$
L_{total} = L_{powertrain} + L_{chassis} + L_{body} + L_{infotainment} + L_{ADAS} + L_{network}
$$

$$
L_{total} = 38 + 22 + 35 + 250 + 180 + 125 \;\; \text{million LOC}
$$

$$
L_{total} = 650 \;\; \text{million LOC}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparative interpretation**

$$
\frac{L_{vehicle}}{L_{F-35}} = \frac{650}{24} \approx 27.1
$$

$$
\frac{L_{vehicle}}{L_{787}} = \frac{650}{14} \approx 46.4
$$

> **Conclusion:** A modern luxury vehicle contains **≈ 27× the code of an F-35 fighter jet** and **≈ 46× the code of a Boeing 787 Dreamliner**. This single derivation is a frequent **14-mark KTU question**.

### 3.2 Derivation: CAN Bus Utilization and Throughput Sizing

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A CAN bus at **500 kbps** carries 10 message IDs, each with an 8-byte data payload. If the average inter-message time is 5 ms, determine the **bus utilization**.

**Step 1 — Calculate the number of bits per CAN frame**

A standard CAN 2.0A frame contains:
- 1 Start-of-Frame bit
- 11-bit Arbitration field
- 6 Control bits (including IDE, RTR, DLC)
- 0–64 data bits (here 64 bits for 8 bytes)
- 15-bit CRC
- 3-bit Acknowledge
- 7-bit End-of-Frame

$$
N_{frame} = 1 + 11 + 6 + 64 + 15 + 3 + 7 = 107 \;\; \text{bits/frame}
$$

**Step 2 — Add inter-frame spacing (3 bits minimum per CAN ISO 11898)**

$$
N_{eff} = 107 + 3 = 110 \;\; \text{bits/frame}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the effective data rate per message**

$$
f_{msg} = \frac{1}{T_{msg}} = \frac{1}{5\,\text{ms}} = 200 \;\; \text{frames/sec}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the effective channel bit-rate requirement**

$$
B_{req} = f_{msg} \cdot N_{eff} = 200 \cdot 110 = 22{,}000 \;\; \text{bits/sec}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the utilization**

$$
U = \frac{B_{req}}{B_{bus}} = \frac{22{,}000}{500{,}000} = 0.044 \;\; (4.4\%)
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** KTU model answers expect students to comment that **CAN utilization should remain below 30%** to leave bandwidth for retransmissions, OBD-II diagnostics, and error frames. Our 4.4% indicates the bus has substantial headroom.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Protocol Selection by Domain

| Vehicle Domain | Typical Protocol | Bandwidth | Real-Time Guarantee | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Engine, Transmission | CAN-FD | 2 – 5 Mbps | Hard | Fuel injection, gear shift |
| Body Electronics (doors, mirrors) | LIN | 20 kbps | Soft | Window lift, seat adjust |
| Chassis Safety (X-by-Wire) | FlexRay / CAN-FD | 10 Mbps | Hard (deterministic) | Steer-by-Wire, Brake-by-Wire |
| Infotainment / Camera | Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1) | 100 Mbps | Soft | Rear-view camera, IVI |
| ADAS / Sensor Fusion | Automotive Ethernet (1000BASE-T1) | 1 Gbps | Mixed | LiDAR point cloud |
| Diagnostic Interface | CAN (UDS) / DoIP (Ethernet) | 500 kbps – 100 Mbps | Soft | Service tool, OTA updates |

### 3.4 Derivation: Sample Time for a Crank-Angle Sensor

A crankshaft turning at **6000 RPM** completes one revolution in:

$$
T_{rev} = \frac{60}{6000} = 0.01 \;\; \text{s} = 10 \;\; \text{ms}
$$

A single crank degree therefore corresponds to:

$$
T_{deg} = \frac{10 \;\text{ms}}{360} \approx 27.78 \;\; \mu\text{s/deg}
$$

To resolve crank position to **±0.1°**, the required sampling interval is:

$$
T_{sample} = 0.1 \cdot 27.78 = 2.78 \;\; \mu\text{s}
$$

The corresponding minimum sampling frequency:

$$
f_{sample} = \frac{1}{2.78\,\mu\text{s}} \approx 360 \;\; \text{kHz}
$$

> This is why **knock-detection and direct-injection controllers** in modern ECUs use dedicated high-speed ADC peripherals operating at **≥ 1 MSPS** (Mega-Samples Per Second).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level Block Architecture of an Automotive Embedded System

```mermaid
graph TD
    SENS1[Temperature Sensor]
    SENS2[Pressure Sensor]
    SENS3[Wheel Speed Sensor]
    SENS4[Crankshaft Position Sensor]
    ECU1[Engine ECU]
    ECU2[Transmission ECU]
    ECU3[Brake ECU ABS]
    ECU4[Body Control Module]
    ECU5[ADAS Domain Controller]
    ACT1[Fuel Injector]
    ACT2[Electronic Throttle Body]
    ACT3[Brake Hydraulic Modulator]
    ACT4[Headlamp Relay]
    BUS1[CAN Bus Powertrain]
    BUS2[CAN Bus Chassis]
    BUS3[LIN Bus Body]
    BUS4[Automotive Ethernet]
    GATE[Central Gateway]
    BAT[12V / 48V Battery]
    HMI[Driver HMI Cluster]
    DIAG[OBD-II Diagnostic Port]
    
    BAT --> ECU1
    BAT --> ECU2
    BAT --> ECU3
    BAT --> ECU4
    BAT --> ECU5
    
    SENS1 --> ECU1
    SENS2 --> ECU1
    SENS3 --> ECU3
    SENS4 --> ECU1
    
    ECU1 --> ACT1
    ECU1 --> ACT2
    ECU3 --> ACT3
    ECU4 --> ACT4
    
    ECU1 --> BUS1
    ECU2 --> BUS1
    ECU1 --> BUS2
    ECU3 --> BUS2
    ECU4 --> BUS3
    ECU5 --> BUS4
    
    BUS1 --> GATE
    BUS2 --> GATE
    BUS3 --> GATE
    BUS4 --> GATE
    
    GATE --> HMI
    GATE --> DIAG
```

### 4.2 Internal Architecture of a Single ECU (Block-Level)

```mermaid
graph LR
    SENSE[Analog / Digital Sensor Input] --> MUX[Input Multiplexer]
    MUX --> ADC[Analog-to-Digital Converter]
    ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller Core]
    DIO[Digital IO] --> MCU
    CANIF[CAN Transceiver] --> MCU
    MEM[Flash / EEPROM / RAM] --> MCU
    MCU --> DSP[Signal Processing Unit]
    DSP --> PID[PID Control Algorithm]
    PID --> PWM[PWM Generation Unit]
    PWM --> DRV[Power Driver Stage]
    DRV --> ACTF[Actuator]
    MCU --> WDT[Watchdog Timer]
    PSU[Voltage Regulator 5V / 3.3V] --> ADC
    PSU --> MCU
    PSU --> WDT
    WDT --> RST[System Reset]
```

### 4.3 V-Model Development Lifecycle (Per ISO 26262)

```mermaid
graph TD
    REQ1[Vehicle Level Requirements] --> REQ2[System Level Requirements]
    REQ2 --> REQ3[ECU Level Requirements]
    REQ3 --> REQ4[Software Module Requirements]
    REQ4 --> REQ5[Software Detailed Design]
    REQ5 --> CODE[Code Implementation]
    CODE --> SW_TEST[Software Unit Test]
    SW_TEST --> INT_TEST[Integration Test]
    INT_TEST --> SYS_TEST[System Validation]
    SYS_TEST --> VEH_TEST[Vehicle Acceptance Test]
    
    REQ4 -.verification.-> SW_TEST
    REQ3 -.verification.-> INT_TEST
    REQ2 -.verification.-> SYS_TEST
    REQ1 -.verification.-> VEH_TEST
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the V-Model:** The left descending arm represents *decomposition* (requirements → design → code). The right ascending arm represents *integration and verification* at each corresponding level. The dotted arrows indicate the **verification traceability matrix** that the KTU examiner expects students to identify in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define an automotive embedded system. List any four distinguishing characteristics that differentiate it from a general-purpose computer system.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An Automotive Embedded System is a computing subsystem permanently integrated within a vehicle to monitor, control, and actuate real-time functions in a deterministic and safety-critical manner (1 mark).

Distinguishing characteristics (½ mark each, any four):
1. **Hard real-time** deterministic response to events.
2. **Resource-constrained** — limited RAM, ROM, CPU cycles.
3. **Safety-critical** operation under ISO 26262.
4. **Harsh environmental tolerance** (−40 °C to +125 °C, vibration, EMI).
5. **Long lifecycle** (10–15 years of field deployment).
6. **Domain-specific communication** protocols (CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Series-1]**
*What is an Electronic Control Unit (ECU)? Mention the major functional blocks inside a typical ECU.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An **ECU (Electronic Control Unit)** is the embedded computing node inside a vehicle that processes sensor data, executes control algorithms, and drives actuators. It typically contains:

- **Microcontroller/Microprocessor core** (1 mark)
- **Memory** (Flash, EEPROM, RAM) (½ mark)
- **Input/output interfaces** — ADC, Digital I/O, PWM (½ mark)
- **Communication transceivers** (CAN, LIN, FlexRay) (½ mark)
- **Power supply and voltage regulation** block (½ mark)

---

### 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Series-2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **general architecture of an Automotive Embedded System**. Identify and briefly describe the roles of the **Sensor, ECU, Actuator, Bus, and Gateway** layers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **CAN, LIN, FlexRay, and Automotive Ethernet** protocols across the parameters of bandwidth, cost, real-time guarantee, and typical application domain. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture of an Automotive Embedded System (7 marks):**

The architecture consists of five interacting layers:

1. **Sensor Layer (1 mark):** Converts physical quantities (temperature, pressure, speed, position) into electrical signals. *Example:* MAP sensor, crankshaft position sensor, wheel speed sensor, camera, LiDAR.

2. **ECU / Processing Layer (1.5 marks):** The brain of the system. Contains an MCU/MPU running a real-time OS (AUTOSAR/OSECK), executes control algorithms (PID, lookup tables, state machines), and handles diagnostics via UDS.

3. **Actuator Layer (1 mark):** Converts electrical commands into physical action. *Examples:* fuel injector solenoid, electronic throttle body motor, ABS hydraulic modulator, DC motor for seat adjustment.

4. **Communication / Bus Layer (1.5 marks):** Standardized wiring (twisted pair, shielded) carrying serial messages between ECUs. Handles arbitration, error detection (CRC), and message prioritization.

5. **Gateway / Coordination Layer (1 mark):** A central ECU that bridges multiple buses (e.g., powertrain CAN ↔ chassis CAN ↔ Ethernet), performs message routing, firewalling, and OTA updates.

> A fully labeled block diagram is essential (refer to **Section 4.1** above). The KTU examiner typically awards 1 mark reserved exclusively for the diagram.

---

**(b) Comparative Study of Vehicle Communication Protocols (7 marks):**

| Parameter | CAN 2.0 | LIN | FlexRay | Auto. Ethernet |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth** | 1 Mbps | 20 kbps | 10 Mbps | 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps |
| **Topology** | Multi-master, bus | Single-master, bus | Dual-channel, star/bus | Star, switched |
| **Real-time** | Event-triggered (soft-hard) | Scheduled (soft) | Deterministic (hard) | TSN extensions (hard) |
| **Cost/Node** | Moderate | Very low | High | Moderate-high |
| **Cable** | Twisted pair | Single wire | Twisted pair (×2) | Single twisted pair (100/1000BASE-T1) |
| **Application** | Engine, brakes, body | Window, seat, mirror | Steer/Brake-by-wire, ESC | Camera, ADAS, IVI, OTA |
| **Error Detection** | CRC-15 | Checksum | CRC-24 | CRC-32 |

**Mark distribution (7 marks):**
- Tabular comparison: 4 marks (½ mark per cell × 8 cells)
- Selection rationale / example domain: 2 marks
- Conclusion on which protocol suits which subsystem: 1 mark

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Re-attempt Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **total lines of code in a modern vehicle**, and justify why this metric is used as a complexity indicator in automotive embedded development. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CAN bus operates at **250 kbps** and carries **6 message IDs** with an average payload of **4 bytes**. The inter-message gap is **2 ms** per message. Compute (i) the bus utilization, and (ii) the maximum number of additional 8-byte messages that can be added before utilization reaches **70%**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Vehicle Software Complexity (7 marks):**

Let $L_{total}$ be the aggregate lines of code across all software domains in a modern vehicle:

$$
L_{total} = L_{powertrain} + L_{chassis} + L_{body} + L_{infotainment} + L_{ADAS} + L_{network}
$$

Substituting the domain-wise contributions (1 mark each, with tabulation as in **Section 3.1**):

$$
L_{total} = 38 + 22 + 35 + 250 + 180 + 125 = 650 \;\; \text{million LOC}
$$

**Justification (3 marks):**
- **Complexity Indicator:** LOC correlates with defect density, testing effort, and memory footprint.
- **Comparative Benchmark:** Vehicles now contain more code than military aircraft, signaling the automotive industry has become a software-intensive sector.
- **Engineering Concern:** With 650 M+ LOC, **ISO 26262** mandates a structured V-Model and **MISRA-C** coding standards to manage risk.
- **Future Trend:** With autonomous driving, the LOC is projected to exceed **1 billion** within this decade.

---

**(b) Bus Utilization and Capacity Sizing (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Bits per CAN frame (1.5 marks):**

For 4-byte data payload, the frame length is:

$$
N_{frame} = 1 + 11 + 6 + 32 + 15 + 3 + 7 = 75 \;\; \text{bits/frame}
$$

Adding the 3-bit inter-frame space:

$$
N_{eff} = 75 + 3 = 78 \;\; \text{bits/frame}
$$

**Step 2 — Required bandwidth per message stream (1.5 marks):**

$$
f_{msg} = \frac{1}{2\,\text{ms}} = 500 \;\; \text{frames/sec}
$$

$$
B_{req} = 500 \cdot 78 = 39{,}000 \;\; \text{bits/sec}
$$

**Step 3 — Current bus utilization (1 mark):**

$$
U_{current} = \frac{39{,}000}{250{,}000} = 0.156 \;\; (15.6\%)
$$

**Step 4 — Available bandwidth for 8-byte messages (1.5 marks):**

Bits per 8-byte frame:

$$
N_{8B} = 1 + 11 + 6 + 64 + 15 + 3 + 7 + 3 = 110 \;\; \text{bits/frame}
$$

Maximum bits/sec permissible for 70% utilization:

$$
B_{70} = 0.70 \cdot 250{,}000 = 175{,}000 \;\; \text{bits/sec}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the maximum number of additional messages (1.5 marks):**

$$
B_{remaining} = 175{,}000 - 39{,}000 = 136{,}000 \;\; \text{bits/sec}
$$

$$
N_{msg,new} = \frac{136{,}000}{110} = 1236 \;\; \text{frames/sec}
$$

Since the additional message stream can run at 500 frames/sec (2 ms gap), the **number of additional independent 8-byte streams** is:

$$
N_{streams} = \left\lfloor \frac{1236}{500} \right\rfloor = 2 \;\; \text{streams}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Current utilization is **15.6%**; up to **2 additional 8-byte message streams (2 ms each)** can be added before the 70% utilization ceiling is reached.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the inter-frame space (IFS = 3 bits):** Many students calculate 75 bits/frame instead of 78 bits/frame. The IFS is mandatory per ISO 11898.
> 2. **Wrong bit-count for 8-byte payload:** Use 64 data bits, **not** $8 \times 8 + 4 = 68$. The frame overhead (SOF, CRC, EOF, ACK) is constant.
> 3. **Mixing up bit stuffing:** In an actual CAN bit-stream, stuffing adds ~10–20% extra bits. The KTU 2024 simplified model *excludes* stuffing unless explicitly stated.
> 4. **Drawing the block diagram without arrows:** Diagrams must show **directional data flow** from sensor → ECU → bus → actuator. Static boxes without arrows earn 0 marks for the figure.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention ISO 26262 / AUTOSAR:** These are *expected keyword mentions* in CO1 answers. Missing them = loss of 1 mark.
> 6. **Unit mistakes in LOC derivation:** Always specify "million LOC" — writing just "650" without units is penalized.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ An **Automotive Embedded System** is a deterministic, safety-critical, resource-constrained computing system inside a vehicle.
- ✅ A modern car uses **80–150+ ECUs** running **100–650+ million lines of code**.
- ✅ The architecture is **5-layered**: Sensor → ECU → Actuator → Communication Bus → Gateway.
- ✅ Communication protocols are **domain-specific**: CAN (powertrain/chassis), LIN (body), FlexRay (X-by-Wire), Automotive Ethernet (ADAS/IVI).
- ✅ **Hard real-time** is non-negotiable for safety functions; missed deadlines = system failure.
- ✅ Bus utilization should remain **< 30% (CAN) / < 70% (FlexRay)** to allow headroom for error frames and diagnostics.
- ✅ **AUTOSAR** is the standardized software architecture; **ISO 26262** is the functional safety standard; **AEC-Q100** is the IC qualification standard.
- ✅ CAN frame = 1 (SOF) + 11 (Arbitration) + 6 (Control) + Data + 15 (CRC) + 3 (ACK) + 7 (EOF) = 47 + Data bits; remember to add 3-bit IFS.
- ✅ ECU environmental envelope: **−40 °C to +125 °C**, 50 G vibration, 8–16 V supply (with load-dump protection to 40 V).
- ✅ The V-Model is the de-facto **development lifecycle** for automotive embedded software per ISO 26262.
- ✅ A **Gateway ECU** bridges heterogeneous buses, performs message routing, firewalling, and OTA updates.
- ✅ **Crank-angle resolution** at 6000 RPM requires **≈ 2.78 µs sampling** — this dictates high-speed ADC selection.

> **One-Line Takeaway for KTU Viva:**
> *An automotive embedded system is a network of safety-critical, real-time ECUs that sense, decide, and act — connected by deterministic buses, developed under the V-Model, and qualified by ISO 26262 and AUTOSAR.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded vs General Computing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT632 - AUTOMOTIVE EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Embedded vs General Computing Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Embedded System

An **Embedded System** is a specialized, purpose-built computing subsystem that is an integral part of a larger mechanical, electrical, or electromechanical product. It is designed to perform a **dedicated set of functions** under strict real-time, power, cost, and reliability constraints. The processor, memory, peripherals, and software are tightly co-engineered for **one specific application domain** (e.g., an Engine Control Unit in an automobile).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):** An embedded system is a microprocessor/microcontroller-based system that is *not* programmable by the end user, has a *fixed* and *predefined* set of capabilities, and is designed to operate as a component within a host device for a *specific function*.

### 1.2 Formal Definition — General Purpose Computing System

A **General Purpose Computing System (GPCS)** — typified by a desktop PC, laptop, or server — is a programmable machine whose **hardware architecture is intentionally decoupled** from any single application. Its CPU, memory hierarchy, and I/O subsystems are engineered to execute a virtually **unbounded range of user-defined software tasks**, with flexibility and user programmability prioritized over power, cost, or form-factor minimization.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Swiss Army Knife vs. The Scalpel Analogy**
>
> - **General Purpose Computer = Swiss Army Knife** 🇨🇭 — One handle (CPU) hosts dozens of interchangeable blades (MS Word, Chrome, MATLAB, Games, Compilers). It is *flexible* but *bloated* and *power-hungry*.
> - **Embedded System = Surgical Scalpel** 🔪 — Engineered to do **one job** (cut cleanly). It is *blazing fast at that one task*, uses *minimal power*, costs *pennies*, and fits in the surgeon's palm. In a car, this scalpel might be the **ABS brake controller** — it must react in under 5 ms; a Swiss Army Knife cannot be trusted with that.
>
> In an **automotive context**, the Engine Control Unit (ECU) is an embedded system that *only* calculates fuel injection timing and ignition advance — nothing else. The infotainment head unit, while more capable, still qualifies as embedded because it is dedicated to the vehicle's HMI and telematics.

### 1.4 Key Distinguishing Attributes

| # | Attribute | Embedded System | General Purpose System |
|---|-----------|----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Primary Goal** | Single / dedicated function | Multi-purpose flexibility |
| 2 | **User Programmability** | Not user-programmable | Fully user-programmable |
| 3 | **OS Dependency** | Often RTOS or bare-metal | Full-fledged OS (Windows/Linux/macOS) |
| 4 | **Power Budget** | µW to a few Watts | 65 W – 1500 W (workstation/server) |
| 5 | **Unit Cost Target** | ₹50 – ₹5,000 | ₹30,000 – ₹5,00,000+ |
| 6 | **Real-Time Behavior** | Often **hard real-time** | Soft real-time (best effort) |
| 7 | **Time-to-Market** | Long (1–3 yrs hardware+SW co-design) | Short (incremental SW updates) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Differentiation

Every comparison between an embedded system and a general-purpose system can be reduced to **three engineering axes** that KTU examiners love to test:

#### Pillar 1 — Functional Specialization
- **Embedded:** The system's *entire software–hardware stack* is co-optimized for **one application class**. Removing or adding software features is meaningless because the hardware was *physically stripped* of unnecessary blocks.
- **General Purpose:** The hardware contains *every conceivable peripheral* (USB, PCIe, SATA, Wi-Fi, GPU, sound codec) so it can run *any* future software without hardware revision.

#### Pillar 2 — Resource Constraint Enforcement
- **Embedded:** Designer enforces strict caps on:
  - **ROM/Flash:** typically **32 KB – 16 MB**
  - **RAM:** typically **2 KB – 512 KB**
  - **CPU Clock:** typically **1 MHz – 600 MHz** (e.g., NXP S32K144 at 80 MHz)
  - **Power Dissipation:** **< 1 W** (often 10–500 mW)
- **General Purpose:** Resource limits are dictated by *performance scaling*, not by the application. A modern server CPU may draw **150–300 W** with **128 GB+ RAM**.

#### Pillar 3 — Determinism & Real-Time Guarantees
- **Embedded (especially Automotive Hard Real-Time):** The Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) of the control loop **must** be bounded and provably less than the deadline.
- **General Purpose:** No such guarantee — the OS may preempt, swap, or stall user code for arbitrary intervals.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield "Cheat Sheet" — Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this **9-row table** verbatim — it is the single most frequently asked question in Module 1 across past KTU university examinations.

| Sl. | Parameter | Embedded System | General Purpose System |
|-----|-----------|-----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Definition** | Specialized computing element inside a host product | Universal computational platform |
| 2 | **Hardware** | Microcontroller / SoC with minimal peripherals | CPU + chipset + discrete GPUs/storage |
| 3 | **Clock Speed** | Low to moderate (≤ 1 GHz typical) | Very high (3 – 5.8 GHz turbo) |
| 4 | **Memory (RAM)** | Tiny (KB scale) | Large (GB scale) |
| 5 | **Operating System** | RTOS (FreeRTOS, AUTOSAR OS) or none | Windows / Linux / macOS |
| 6 | **Power Consumption** | Very low (mW – few W) | High (tens to hundreds of W) |
| 7 | **Cost** | Very low (₹100 – ₹10,000) | High (₹30k – lakhs) |
| 8 | **Response Time** | Hard / firm real-time (deterministic) | Soft real-time / non-deterministic |
| 9 | **Examples** | ECU, ABS, Airbag controller, Smart sensor | Desktop, Laptop, Server, Smartphone (used as computer) |

### 2.3 Classification of Embedded Systems (Context-Building for Later Modules)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Module 1 expects students to know the **four classes** that any embedded system may fall into — this often appears as a 3-mark question.

1. **Stand-alone embedded systems** — operate independently (e.g., MP3 player, digital camera).
2. **Real-time embedded systems** — produce output within a strict deadline.
   - *Hard real-time:* missing deadline = **system failure** (airbag deployment).
   - *Soft real-time:* missing deadline = **degraded quality** (video streaming in infotainment).
3. **Networked embedded systems** — connected to a wired/wireless network (V2X modules, telematics control units).
4. **Mobile embedded systems** — portable, battery-powered, GUI-centric (modern automotive instrument clusters).

### 2.4 Real-World Automotive Utility

| Vehicle Subsystem | System Class | Deadline / Constraint |
|-------------------|--------------|------------------------|
| Airbag ECU | Hard Real-Time Embedded | Deploy within **< 10 ms** of crash detection |
| Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) | Hard Real-Time Embedded | Wheel slip correction every **5 ms** |
| Infotainment (Android Automotive) | Soft Real-Time Embedded | Audio/video latency < **100 ms** |
| ADAS (Lane Keep Assist) | Hard Real-Time Embedded | Steering torque update at **20–50 Hz** |
| Diagnostic Scanner Tool | General Purpose | No real-time constraint |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — Designing the Distinction

### 3.1 The Decision Metric — How an Engineer Chooses

A real engineer rarely *starts* by picking "embedded vs general purpose." Instead, the **application constraints** drive the choice. Below is the formal analytical flow that KTU examiners may ask you to derive (typically a 7-mark sub-question).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Derivation of the Design Choice**

**Step 1 — Enumerate Hard Constraints**

Let the set of application constraints be denoted:

$$ C = \{ C_1, C_2, C_3, \dots, C_n \} $$

Common automotive constraints include:

$$ C = \{ \text{deadline} \le T_d,\; \text{power} \le P_{max},\; \text{cost} \le B,\; \text{size} \le V_{max} \} $$

**Step 2 — Quantify the Real-Time Pressure Index (RTPI)**

Define the *Real-Time Pressure Index* of a task as:

$$ \text{RTPI} = \frac{\text{WCET of the task}}{\text{Deadline of the task}} $$

This is dimensionless and lies in the interval $[0, 1]$. When RTPI is **close to 1**, the system is at the *brink* of deadline violation — demanding **hard real-time embedded** architecture.

**Step 3 — Apply the Decision Rule**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{System Class} &= \begin{cases}
\text{Hard Real-Time Embedded}, & \text{if } \text{RTPI} \ge 0.7 \text{ and any missing deadline is safety-critical} \\[4pt]
\text{Soft Real-Time Embedded}, & \text{if } 0.3 \le \text{RTPI} < 0.7 \text{ and only quality degrades} \\[4pt]
\text{General Purpose}, & \text{if } \text{RTPI} < 0.3 \text{ AND (user programmability required OR multi-app)} \\[4pt]
\text{Stand-alone Embedded}, & \text{if } \text{RTPI} < 0.3 \text{ AND single fixed function required}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify Against Cost & Power Budgets**

After choosing the class, the engineer must satisfy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Unit Cost} &= C_{\mu} + C_{\text{mem}} + C_{\text{pwr}} + C_{\text{PCB}} \le B \\[4pt]
\text{Steady-State Power} &= V_{DD} \cdot I_{active} \cdot D + V_{DD} \cdot I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D) \le P_{max}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $D$ = duty cycle (fraction of time the CPU is active),
- $I_{active}$, $I_{sleep}$ = active and sleep-mode currents of the MCU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** If the *steady-state power equation* cannot be satisfied with an embedded MCU class, you may be forced to use a low-power SoC — which still counts as *embedded*, *not* general purpose.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Choosing Between Embedded and General Purpose

**Problem Statement:** A Tier-1 automotive supplier must build a **Tyre Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS)** sensor. The sensor is battery-powered (CR2032, 220 mAh), must run for **7 years**, sample pressure every **3 seconds**, and transmit a 64-bit packet wirelessly within **10 ms** of pressure-change detection. Should the design use an embedded or general-purpose processor?

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad &\text{WCET}_{\text{sense}} \approx 0.5 \text{ ms (sensor read + ADC)} \\
&\text{WCET}_{\text{transmit}} \approx 1.5 \text{ ms (radio packet formation)} \\
&\text{WCET}_{\text{total}} \approx 2.0 \text{ ms} \\[4pt]
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad &\text{Deadline} = 10 \text{ ms} \\
&\text{RTPI} = \frac{2.0 \text{ ms}}{10 \text{ ms}} = 0.20 \\[4pt]
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad &\text{Since RTPI} = 0.20 < 0.3, \text{ and the function is single (pressure sense + TX)}, \\
&\text{design falls into the Stand-alone Embedded category.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 4 (Power Budget Verification):} \quad \\
\text{Avg current budget} &= \frac{220 \text{ mAh}}{7 \times 365 \times 24 \text{ h}} = \frac{220}{61320} \text{ mA} \approx 3.59 \; \mu\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

A typical low-power MCU like the NXP KW36 draws **< 1 µA in deep sleep** and **3.5 mA during active TX** for ~3 ms — easily meeting the **3.59 µA average** budget. A general-purpose processor drawing **> 30 mA even at idle** would *drain the battery in days*. ✅ **Conclusion: Embedded system is the only viable choice.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architectural Block Diagram — Embedded vs General Purpose

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EMB["EMBEDDED SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE"]
        e1[Sensor Input] --> e2[Microcontroller SoC]
        e2 --> e3[Actuator Output]
        e2 --> e4[Fixed Firmware in ROM]
        subgraph eCORE["Tightly Integrated Core"]
            e5[CPU Core] --- e6[On-chip Flash] --- e7[On-chip RAM] --- e8[Timers ADC PWM UART]
        end
        e2 -.uses.-> eCORE
    end

    subgraph GEN["GENERAL PURPOSE SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE"]
        g1[Keyboard Mouse Network] --> g2[CPU Socketed on Motherboard]
        g2 --> g3[Discrete GPU]
        g2 --> g4[Discrete RAM DIMMs]
        g2 --> g5[SSD NVMe Storage]
        g4 --> g3
        g4 --> g5
        g2 --> g6[User-installable Apps]
    end

    classDef emb fill:#e6f4ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000
    classDef gen fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#7a3d00,color:#000
    class e1,e2,e3,e4,e5,e6,e7,e8 emb
    class g1,g2,g3,g4,g5,g6 gen
```

### 4.2 Design-Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Requirements] --> B{Real-Time Critical?}
    B -- YES --> C[Hard Real-Time Embedded]
    B -- NO --> D{Multiple Apps by User?}
    D -- YES --> E[General Purpose Computer]
    D -- NO --> F[Stand-alone Embedded]

    C --> G[MCU + RTOS + Fixed Firmware]
    F --> H[MCU + Bare-metal Code]
    E --> I[CPU + Full OS + User Apps]

    G --> J[Automotive ECU Airbag ABS]
    H --> K[MP3 Player Digital Watch TPMS]
    I --> L[Desktop PC Engineering Workstation]

    classDef crit fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef emb fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1a6600,color:#000
    classDef gen fill:#d6e6ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000
    class C,G crit
    class F,H,K emb
    class E,I,L gen
    class A,B,D,J gen
```

### 4.3 Application-to-Architecture Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AUTO["Automotive Embedded Spectrum"]
        s1[Airbag ECU] -->|Hard RT| a1[ASIL-D MCU]
        s2[ABS Module] -->|Hard RT| a2[ASIL-C MCU]
        s3[Infotainment] -->|Soft RT| a3[Application SoC]
        s4[Instrument Cluster] -->|Soft RT| a4[MPU with GPU]
        s5[Diagnostic Tool] -->|None| a5[Laptop or Tablet]
    end
    classDef hard fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000
    classDef soft fill:#fff0b3,stroke:#996600
    classDef gen fill:#d6e6ff,stroke:#003a75
    class s1,s2,a1,a2 hard
    class s3,s4,a3,a4 soft
    class s5,a5 gen
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Differentiate between a general-purpose computer and an embedded system. List any four points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. | Embedded System | General Purpose System |
|-----|-----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Designed for a *specific, dedicated* function | Designed for *multiple general* tasks |
| 2 | Microcontroller-based with on-chip peripherals | CPU + chipset + discrete peripherals |
| 3 | Tight real-time constraints (often hard RT) | Soft / no real-time constraints |
| 4 | Low power (mW – few W) and low cost (₹100 – ₹10k) | High power (50 – 500 W) and high cost |
| 5 | OS is RTOS or none | Full-fledged OS (Windows/Linux) |

*(Any four points, 1 mark each, with example → +0.5 mark. Total 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]`
**With a suitable example, explain what is meant by a *hard real-time* embedded system in the automotive domain.**

**Model Answer:**
A *hard real-time* embedded system is one in which **missing a deadline results in catastrophic system failure**, endangering life or causing irreversible damage. The timeliness constraint is **absolute and non-negotiable**.

> **Automotive Example — Airbag ECU:**
> When the crash sensor detects a deceleration > 5 g, the ECU *must* fire the inflator within **10 ms**. A delay of even **15 ms** could mean the occupant's head has already contacted the steering wheel — leading to fatal injury.
>
> Hence the airbag ECU is built on an **ASIL-D** rated MCU (e.g., Infineon AURIX TC39x) running an **AUTOSAR OS** that pre-empts every other task to meet this 10 ms deadline — guaranteed deterministically.

*(Definition 1 mark + Example 1 mark + Justification 1 mark = 3 marks.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In comparison questions, students *only list* differences without explaining the *reason* behind them. The 2024 scheme marks understanding of the **'why'** at Bloom's *Analyze* level. For each point, write: **(i) the difference** *(1 mark)* and **(ii) one-line reason** *(1 mark)*. Failing to provide the reason costs 50% of the marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** List and explain **any seven** major differences between an embedded system and a general-purpose computing system. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Identify the class (embedded hard-RT / embedded soft-RT / general purpose) and **justify with quantitative reasoning** for the following automotive systems: *(7 marks)*
    (i) Electronic Stability Control (ESC)
    (ii) In-vehicle Infotainment with Android Automotive OS
    (iii) Dealer-side Diagnostic Scan Tool used in service bays

---

#### Model Solution — Q-A(a)

| # | Parameter | Embedded System | General Purpose System | Reason |
|---|-----------|-----------------|------------------------|--------|
| 1 | **Purpose** | Single dedicated task | Multi-purpose | Hardware is co-designed for one job |
| 2 | **Hardware** | MCU + on-chip peripherals | CPU + chipset + discrete I/O | Minimizes BOM cost and size |
| 3 | **Memory** | KB-scale RAM/Flash | GB-scale RAM, TB storage | Application code is small & fixed |
| 4 | **OS** | RTOS or bare-metal | Windows/Linux/macOS | Determinism is critical |
| 5 | **Power** | mW – few W | Tens to hundreds of W | Battery/thermal constraints |
| 6 | **Cost** | ₹100 – ₹10,000 | ₹30k – several lakhs | Mass-produced, single-purpose |
| 7 | **Response Time** | Hard-deterministic | Soft / non-deterministic | Safety-critical loops |

*(7 points × 1 mark = 7 marks. Explanation column adds rigor — examiner awards full marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q-A(b)

**(i) Electronic Stability Control (ESC)**
- **Class:** *Hard Real-Time Embedded System*
- **Reasoning:**
  - ESC must sample wheel speed, yaw rate, and steering angle every **5 ms**.
  - WCET of the control algorithm ≈ **2 ms**, deadline = **5 ms**.
  - Therefore, RTPI = 2/5 = **0.40**, but missing a deadline can cause vehicle skid → *safety-critical*.
  - Implemented on an **ASIL-D MCU** running **AUTOSAR Classic** with priority-based preemption. *(3 marks)*

**(ii) In-vehicle Infotainment with Android Automotive OS**
- **Class:** *Soft Real-Time Embedded System*
- **Reasoning:**
  - Touch response must be < **100 ms**, audio buffer under-run threshold = **20 ms**.
  - Missing an audio deadline causes a glitch, not a crash → *quality degrades but no safety risk*.
  - Runs on a high-performance application SoC (e.g., Qualcomm Snapdragon SA8155P) with a **Linux kernel** + **Android Automotive** stack. *(2 marks)*

**(iii) Dealer-side Diagnostic Scan Tool**
- **Class:** *General Purpose Computing System*
- **Reasoning:**
  - No real-time deadline — technician initiates a scan, waits minutes for results.
  - Must run *many* different diagnostic apps (engine, ABS, airbag, transmission) → *multi-application*.
  - Built on a **ruggedized laptop/tablet** running Windows or Linux. *(2 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a typical embedded system** and explain the role of each block. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A TPMS sensor must run for **10 years** on a **220 mAh** coin cell, sample pressure every **5 s**, and transmit a packet within **8 ms** of detection. Compute the average current budget and justify whether an embedded MCU or general-purpose CPU should be used. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q-B(a)

**Typical Embedded System Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Sensor / Input] --> M[Microcontroller / SoC]
    U[User Interface: LED Button] --> M
    M --> A[Actuator / Output]
    M --> P[Power Supply + Battery]
    M --> C[Clock Oscillator]
    M --> T[Communication: CAN LIN UART]
    subgraph CORE["Microcontroller Core"]
        CPU[CPU] --- MEM[ROM Flash for Program] --- RAM[RAM for Data] --- PER[On-chip Peripherals: ADC Timer PWM GPIO]
    end
    classDef block fill:#e6f4ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000
    class S,U,M,A,P,C,T,CPU,MEM,RAM,PER block
```

**Role of each block:**
- **Sensor / Input:** Captures physical signals (temperature, pressure, speed) and converts them to electrical form.
- **User Interface:** Buttons, LEDs, LCDs for local user interaction.
- **Microcontroller:** The *brain* — integrates CPU, memory, and peripherals on a single die.
- **Actuator / Output:** Converts electrical decisions back to physical action (motor, relay, buzzer).
- **Power Supply:** Battery or regulated supply; often includes sleep-mode management.
- **Clock Oscillator:** Provides timing reference; crystal or internal RC oscillator.
- **Communication Block:** Interfaces to other ECUs via CAN, LIN, FlexRay, or Ethernet.

*(1 mark for diagram, 6 × 1 mark for explanation = 7 marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q-B(b)

**Step 1 — Compute Average Current Budget**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Energy Available} &= 220 \text{ mAh} \\[4pt]
\text{Total Lifetime} &= 10 \times 365 \times 24 = 87600 \text{ hours} \\[4pt]
\text{Average Current Budget} &= \frac{220 \text{ mAh}}{87600 \text{ h}} \\[4pt]
&= 2.511 \;\mu\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with Processor Classes**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Typical low-power MCU (sleep)} &\approx 1 \; \mu\text{A} \\
\text{Typical low-power MCU (active 3 ms @ 5 mA)} &\Rightarrow \text{avg over 5 s} = \frac{5 \text{ mA} \times 3 \text{ ms}}{5000 \text{ ms}} = 3 \; \mu\text{A} \\
\text{Total estimated avg} &\approx 1 \; \mu\text{A (sleep)} + 3 \; \mu\text{A (active TX)} = 4 \; \mu\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

But since the TX happens **only when pressure changes** (rare), the *actual* average is dominated by sleep and is well below **2.51 µA**.

**Step 3 — Compare with General Purpose CPU**

$$
\text{A general-purpose CPU idle current} \ge 30 \text{ mA} \gg 2.51 \; \mu\text{A}
$$

A general-purpose CPU would drain the 220 mAh cell in:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lifetime with GPCPU} &= \frac{220 \text{ mAh}}{30 \text{ mA}} = 7.33 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A **general-purpose CPU is infeasible** — the battery would die within a single day. An **embedded MCU** (e.g., NXP KW36, TI CC2640) with deep-sleep modes is the only viable choice. ✅

*(Step 1: 2 marks, Step 2: 3 marks, Step 3 + conclusion: 2 marks = 7 marks.)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Embedded vs General Purpose**

- ☐ An **Embedded System** is *specialized*; a **General Purpose System** is *versatile*.
- ☐ Embedded systems use **microcontrollers**; GPCs use **microprocessors** with chipsets.
- ☐ Embedded = **hard/soft real-time, deterministic**; GPC = **best-effort, non-deterministic**.
- ☐ Embedded = **low power, low cost, small size**; GPC = opposite on all three.
- ☐ OS in embedded: **RTOS** (FreeRTOS, AUTOSAR OS, VxWorks) or **bare-metal**; GPC: **GPOS** (Windows, Linux).
- ☐ **Real-Time Pressure Index** = $\text{WCET} / \text{Deadline}$. Above 0.7 → hard real-time territory.
- ☐ **Hard real-time** failure = catastrophic (Airbag, ABS, ESC). **Soft real-time** failure = quality drop (audio glitch).
- ☐ Automotive examples: ECU, ABS, Airbag, TPMS → **embedded**; Dealer diagnostic PC → **general purpose**.
- ☐ **MISRA-C** and **ASIL** compliance applies *only* to automotive embedded systems, never to GPCs.
- ☐ Memory: Embedded uses **KB**; GPC uses **GB** — a difference of ~$10^6$.
- ☐ User programmability: **None** in embedded (firmware is locked); **Full** in GPC.
- ☐ Always end a 14-mark answer with a **justification backed by a number** (deadline, RTPI, current, cost) — KTU 2024 examiners reward *quantitative reasoning* explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components and Applications of Automotive Embedded Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT632 - AUTOMOTIVE EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Components and Applications of Automotive Embedded Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Automotive Embedded System (AES)** is a microprocessor- or microcontroller-based computing subsystem permanently integrated into a vehicle to perform dedicated, real-time, safety-critical, and resource-constrained functions such as engine management, braking, steering, powertrain control, body electronics, infotainment, and advanced driver-assistance. The system is composed of tightly coupled **hardware (sensors, actuators, ECUs, communication buses, power supplies)** and **software (RTOS, AUTOSAR layers, application algorithms, diagnostic stacks)** that operate under strict automotive quality, reliability, and environmental standards (ISO 26262, AEC-Q100, ISO 14229 / UDS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT632, Module 1):**
> Automotive Embedded Systems are characterized by **real-time deterministic behaviour**, **distributed intelligence across multiple ECUs**, **fault tolerance**, and **bounded worst-case latency** — distinguishing them from generic embedded systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vehicle as an Orchestra"

Imagine a modern car as a **70-piece orchestra** travelling at 120 km/h on a highway:

| Orchestra Element | Automotive Embedded Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Conductor | Central Gateway / CAN Bus | Synchronizes all musicians, controls timing |
| Musicians (strings, brass, percussion) | Individual ECUs (Engine ECU, ABS ECU, BCM) | Each performs a specialised role |
| Sheet music (notes) | Embedded Software & Firmware | Tells each ECU *what* and *when* to act |
| Musical instruments (violin, trumpet) | Actuators (fuel injector, brake caliper motor) | Produce the real physical effect |
| Microphones picking up sound | Sensors (MAP, wheel-speed, oxygen, accelerometer) | Convert physical reality → electrical signals |
| Audience safety & rules | ISO 26262 functional safety, OBD-II diagnostics | Ensure the "performance" is safe and verifiable |

**Intuition**: When you press the brake pedal, the system does *not* directly move the brake pads. Instead, a *sensor* (pedal position) tells an *ECU* your intent, the ECU computes optimal braking, *communicates* this over a CAN bus to the ABS ECU, which then commands an *actuator* (hydraulic modulator) to apply braking force — all within **~10–20 ms** total latency.

## 1.3 Why Automotive Embedded Systems Are Special

> [!NOTE]
> A modern mid-segment car contains **~70–100 ECUs**, **~2 km of wiring**, **~100 million lines of code**, and generates up to **~25 GB of data per hour** from in-vehicle sensors.

Key differentiating factors vs. general-purpose embedded systems:

1. **Hard Real-Time Constraints** — missed deadlines may cause loss of life (e.g., airbag deployment must occur in **< 10 ms** after crash detection).
2. **Extended Temperature Range** — AEC-Q100 grade-1 mandates operation from **−40 °C to +125 °C**.
3. **EMI / EMC Resilience** — must survive harsh electrical noise from ignition coils, motors, alternators.
4. **Functional Safety Compliance** — ISO 26262 mandates ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level) ratings from **A to D**.
5. **Long Product Life-Cycle** — vehicles stay in service for **15–20 years**; software must remain supportable.
6. **Deterministic Communication** — bus protocols (CAN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet) guarantee bounded latency.

## 1.4 Application Domain Spectrum

Automotive embedded systems are typically classified into **five functional domains**:

| Domain | Typical ECUs | Criticality |
|---|---|---|
| **Powertrain** | Engine ECU, Transmission ECU, BMS (EV) | ASIL C/D |
| **Chassis & Safety** | ABS, ESC, EPS, Airbag | ASIL D |
| **Body & Comfort** | BCM, HVAC, Seat Control, Lighting | QM → ASIL B |
| **Infotainment & Telematics** | Head Unit, Instrument Cluster, TCU | QM |
| **ADAS / Autonomous** | Radar ECU, Camera ECU, Domain Controller | ASIL B/D |

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an Automotive Embedded System — The Functional Layers

An automotive embedded system is best understood as a **stack of four interacting layers**:

### Layer 1 — Physical / Sensor Layer
Converts real-world physical quantities into electrical signals.

| Sensor Class | Physical Quantity | Example Sensor | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Position | Angular / Linear displacement | Throttle Position Sensor (TPS), Crankshaft position | Analog 0–5 V, PWM, or digital (SENT) |
| Pressure | Manifold, Tire, Oil | MAP, TMPS | Analog ratiometric |
| Temperature | Coolant, Air, Oil | NTC thermistor, PT200 | Resistance / Analog |
| Inertial | Acceleration, Yaw rate | MEMS Accelerometer (e.g., ADXL345) | SPI / I²C digital |
| Chemical | O₂ concentration | Lambda / UEGO sensor | 0–1 V narrow-band |
| Speed | Wheel rotation | Hall-effect wheel-speed sensor | Square-wave digital |

### Layer 2 — Processing / ECU Layer
The **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** is the brain. A typical ECU contains:

1. **Microcontroller Unit (MCU)** — e.g., NXP S32K3, Infineon Aurix TC3xx, Renesas RH850, TI TMS570.
   - Core: ARM Cortex-M/R, TriCore, RH850G3MH (often **dual-core lockstep** for ASIL-D).
   - Clock: **40–300 MHz**, with deterministic interrupt latency.
2. **Memory Subsystem** — Flash (program), SRAM (runtime), EEPROM (calibration), often with ECC.
3. **Power Management IC (PMIC)** — Buck regulators, LDOs, watchdog.
4. **Communication Transceivers** — CAN FD, LIN, FlexRay, 100BASE-T1 PHY.
5. **Analog Front-End (AFE)** — Op-amps, ADC drivers, signal conditioning.
6. **Digital I/O & Driver Stages** — Low-side/high-side switches (e.g., BTS50010), H-bridges.

### Layer 3 — Communication / Network Layer
Enables **distributed** intelligence across the vehicle. The five key protocols:

| Protocol | Bandwidth | Topology | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CAN 2.0** | 1 Mbit/s max | Multi-master, differential bus | Powertrain, Body |
| **CAN FD** | up to 5 Mbit/s | Multi-master | Modern powertrain, ADAS |
| **LIN** | 19.2 kbit/s | Single-master, single-wire | Window, mirror, seat |
| **FlexRay** | 10 Mbit/s | Star / bus | X-by-wire (legacy) |
| **Automotive Ethernet (100/1000BASE-T1)** | 100 Mbit/s – 1 Gbit/s | Point-to-point, switched | ADAS, backbone, infotainment |

### Layer 4 — Software / Application Layer
Stacked software model following **AUTOSAR Classic** or **Adaptive**:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│   Application Software Layer (ASW)      │  ← Vehicle functions
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Runtime Environment (RTE)             │  ← Virtual Function Bus
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Basic Software (BSW)                  │  ← MCAL, ECU Abstraction,
│       ├── Microcontroller Abstraction   │     Services, COM stack,
│       ├── ECU Abstraction Layer         │     Memory, Diagnostic
│       ├── Services Layer                │
│       └── Complex Drivers               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Microcontroller Hardware              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Mapping**: This four-layer decomposition is the standard answer framework expected for any "Explain the architecture of an automotive embedded system" question (14-mark module question).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **deterministic, exam-relevant** expressions for Module 1 calculations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CAN Bit Time | $t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{f_{baud}}$ | $f_{baud}$ in bit/s | CAN bus timing analysis |
| 2 | CAN Bus Throughput | $\eta = \dfrac{N \cdot L_{data}}{N \cdot L_{frame} + \tau_{inter}}$ | N = frames/sec, L = bits, $\tau_{inter}$ = inter-frame gap | Payload efficiency |
| 3 | CAN FD Max Frame Time | $T_{frame} = t_{arb} + t_{data} + t_{ack}$ | Arbitration + data + ack phases | Worst-case latency |
| 4 | ADC Resolution (LSB) | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}}$ | n = ADC bits, V in Volts | Sensor quantization error |
| 5 | Quantization Noise | $V_{q,rms} = \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}}$ | RMS quantization voltage | Sensor accuracy budget |
| 6 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_{s} \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | $f_{max}$ = highest signal freq. | Sensor sampling design |
| 7 | PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$ (avg.) | Duty ratio | Actuator / motor control |
| 8 | Power Consumption | $P = V_{cc} \cdot I_{active} + V_{cc} \cdot I_{sleep}$ | V in V, I in A | ECU thermal design |
| 9 | Worst-Case Response Time | $t_{resp} = t_{sense} + t_{compute} + t_{comm} + t_{actuate}$ | All in seconds | Real-time schedulability |
| 10 | CAN Arbitration Latency | $t_{arb} = N \cdot t_{bit}$ | N = transmitting nodes, $t_{bit}$ = bit time | Worst-case bus access |
| 11 | Sensor Transfer (Ratiometric) | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{x}{x_{max}}$ | x = physical measurand | Calibration of MAP / TPS |
| 12 | ECU Clock Cycle Budget | $t_{cyc} = \dfrac{1}{f_{CLK}}$ | $f_{CLK}$ in Hz | Instruction execution timing |

**Boundary Conditions & Important Notes:**

- CAN bit stuffing can add up to **+20 %** to nominal frame time.
- ADC resolution $n$ is typically **10, 12, or 16 bits** in automotive MCUs.
- PWM switching frequencies for motor control: **5–20 kHz** (above audible range).
- FlexRay static segment gives deterministic latency; dynamic segment is event-driven.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are used daily by **OEMs (Bosch, Continental, ZF)** and **Tier-1 suppliers** for:

- **Sizing MCUs** — ensuring instruction cycles fit within control loop period.
- **Thermal management** — predicting ECU board temperature.
- **Bus load analysis** — CAN buses must operate below **~70 % bus load** to remain responsive.
- **Sensor fusion** — combining IMU + GPS + radar using Kalman filters (the math relies on the ADC quantization formulas above).

# SECTION 3 — System Architecture Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Worst-Case Response Time of an Automotive Control Loop

This is the **single most important derivation** for understanding why automotive embedded systems are designed the way they are (timing, scheduling, ECU count, bus selection).

### Step 1 — Define the Control Loop

Consider a typical brake-by-wire loop. The chain of events from driver intent to physical action:

$$
\text{Driver Pedal} \rightarrow \text{Pedal Sensor} \rightarrow \text{ECU}_{\text{brake}} \rightarrow \text{CAN Bus} \rightarrow \text{ABS ECU} \rightarrow \text{Hydraulic Actuator}
$$

### Step 2 — Decompose Total Latency into Four Segments

$$
t_{resp} = t_{sense} + t_{compute} + t_{comm} + t_{actuate}
$$

Each segment is derived below.

### Step 3 — Derive $t_{sense}$ (Sensor Acquisition Time)

The sensor must be sampled, conditioned, and digitized. With an ADC of $n$ bits and a sample-and-hold acquisition time $\tau_{SH}$, plus signal conditioning settling time $\tau_{sett}$:

$$
t_{sense} = \tau_{SH} + \tau_{sett} + \frac{n}{f_{ADC\,clk}}
$$

where $\frac{n}{f_{ADC\,clk}}$ is the time to clock out $n$ bits of conversion result.

### Step 4 — Derive $t_{compute}$ (ECU Processing Time)

If the control algorithm requires $C$ clock cycles and the MCU runs at $f_{CLK}$:

$$
t_{compute} = \frac{C}{f_{CLK}}
$$

For example, a typical PID + signal-processing block in ABS requires $C \approx 4000$ cycles on an Aurix TC3xx at $f_{CLK} = 300\,\text{MHz}$:

$$
t_{compute} = \frac{4000}{300 \times 10^{6}} = 13.33\,\mu s
$$

### Step 5 — Derive $t_{comm}$ (Bus Communication Time)

For a CAN frame containing $L$ data bytes at baud rate $f_{baud}$, accounting for arbitration (11 bits), control (2 bits), CRC (15 bits), ACK (2 bits), EOF (7 bits), and **worst-case bit stuffing** (factor of 1.2):

$$
t_{comm} = \frac{(47 + 8L)}{f_{baud}} \times 1.2
$$

**Numerical example** for $L = 8$ bytes, $f_{baud} = 500\,\text{kbit/s}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{comm} &= \frac{(47 + 8 \times 8)}{500 \times 10^{3}} \times 1.2 \\
&= \frac{(47 + 64)}{500 \times 10^{3}} \times 1.2 \\
&= \frac{111}{500000} \times 1.2 \\
&= 222 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.2 \\
&= 266.4\,\mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Derive $t_{actuate}$ (Actuator Response Time)

For a hydraulic brake modulator with solenoid energize time $\tau_{sol}$ and fluid-fill time $\tau_{hyd}$:

$$
t_{actuate} = \tau_{sol} + \tau_{hyd}
$$

Typical values: $\tau_{sol} \approx 1\,\text{ms}$, $\tau_{hyd} \approx 3\,\text{ms}$, so $t_{actuate} \approx 4\,\text{ms}$.

### Step 7 — Sum Total Response Time

Using representative numerical values from above:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{resp} &= t_{sense} + t_{compute} + t_{comm} + t_{actuate} \\
&= 5\,\mu s + 13.33\,\mu s + 266.4\,\mu s + 4000\,\mu s \\
&\approx 4.28\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

This satisfies the **ISO 26262 ASIL-D** requirement of brake-system end-to-end latency $<\mathbf{10\,ms}$.

### Step 8 — Design Implication

If $t_{resp}$ exceeds the budget, the engineer must:

| Lever | Action | Effect on $t_{resp}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Faster MCU | Increase $f_{CLK}$ | $\downarrow t_{compute}$ |
| Higher bus speed | Migrate to CAN FD or FlexRay | $\downarrow t_{comm}$ |
| Pre-processing | Sensor pre-conditioning in AFE | $\downarrow t_{sense}$ |
| Faster actuator | Use electro-mechanical instead of hydraulic | $\downarrow t_{actuate}$ |
| Reduce data bytes | Compress or change protocol | $\downarrow t_{comm}$ |

## 3.2 ADC Resolution Derivation (Sensor Quantization)

Starting from the transfer function of an ideal ADC:

$$
V_{out} = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}}\right) \cdot V_{LSB}
$$

where

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}}
$$

For a 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5\,V$:

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{5}{2^{12}} = \frac{5}{4096} = 1.2207\,\text{mV}
$$

The **RMS quantization noise** (assuming uniform distribution of error between $\pm \tfrac{V_{LSB}}{2}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{q,rms} &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{V_{LSB}} \int_{0}^{V_{LSB}} \!\left(x - \tfrac{V_{LSB}}{2}\right)^{2}\, dx} \\
&= \frac{V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{1.2207\,\text{mV}}{\sqrt{12}} \\
&= 0.3524\,\text{mV (RMS)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This determines the **minimum detectable sensor signal change** — a critical parameter for engine MAP and oxygen sensor calibration.

## 3.3 KTU Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Automotive Communication Protocols

This matrix is the standard "compare & contrast" content for 7-mark sub-questions.

| Parameter | CAN 2.0 | CAN FD | LIN | FlexRay | Automotive Ethernet |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Max Bit Rate** | 1 Mbit/s | 5 Mbit/s | 19.2 kbit/s | 10 Mbit/s | 1 Gbit/s |
| **Wiring** | 2-wire differential | 2-wire differential | 1-wire | 2- or 4-wire | 1-pair twisted |
| **Topology** | Bus | Bus | Bus | Bus / Star | Switched point-to-point |
| **Determinism** | Best-effort CSMA/BA | Best-effort CSMA/BA | Time-triggered | TDMA | TSN / best-effort |
| **Max Payload** | 8 bytes | 64 bytes | 8 bytes | 254 bytes | 1500 bytes |
| **Cost per Node** | Low | Low | Very low | High | Medium |
| **Typical Use** | Powertrain, body | Modern powertrain | Mirrors, seats | X-by-wire | ADAS, backbone |
| **Standard** | ISO 11898-1 | ISO 11898-1:2015 | ISO 17987 | ISO 17458 | IEEE 100BASE-T1 / 1000BASE-T1 |
| **Real-Time Guarantee** | Soft | Soft | Soft | Hard | TSN-aware |

| Engine Control | MAP, MAF, Crank, Cam, O₂, Knock | ECM | Fuel injector, Ignition coil, EGR valve | CAN FD |
| Automatic Transmission | Vehicle speed, Turbine speed, Gear position | TCU | Solenoid valves, Clutch actuator | CAN FD |
