# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define an automotive embedded system and list any four of its distinguishing characteristics.**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
An automotive embedded system is a dedicated, real-time, microcontroller-based computing subsystem permanently embedded in a vehicle to perform specific functions such as engine control, braking, steering, body electronics, or infotainment.

**Four distinguishing characteristics:**

1. **Hard real-time constraints** — deterministic response within bounded latency (e.g., airbag deployment < 10 ms).
2. **Functional safety compliance** — must satisfy ISO 26262 ASIL ratings (A to D).
3. **Harsh operating environment** — temperature range **−40 °C to +125 °C** (AEC-Q100), high EMI/EMC.
4. **Distributed architecture** — multiple ECUs interconnected via standardized automotive communication buses (CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet).
5. (Optional) Long product life-cycle of **15–20 years**.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between CAN, LIN, and FlexRay communication protocols used in vehicles.**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | CAN 2.0 | LIN | FlexRay |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bit Rate** | Up to 1 Mbit/s | 19.2 kbit/s | 10 Mbit/s |
| **Wiring** | 2-wire differential | 1-wire (single-ended) | 2- or 4-wire |
| **Topology** | Multi-master bus | Single-master bus | Bus or star |
| **Determinism** | Best-effort (CSMA/BA) | Time-triggered master | Hard real-time TDMA |
| **Application** | Powertrain, chassis | Body (mirrors, seats) | X-by-wire, chassis |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an automotive embedded system. Describe the four functional layers in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major components of a modern Electronic Control Unit (ECU). Explain the role of the microcontroller, power management IC, communication transceivers, and memory subsystem. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### **(a) Architecture of an Automotive Embedded System**

A modern automotive embedded system is organized as a **four-layer architecture**:

**Layer 1 — Physical / Sensor Layer:**
Sensors convert physical quantities (temperature, pressure, speed, position) into electrical signals. Examples: MAP sensor, crankshaft position sensor, wheel-speed sensor, oxygen (lambda) sensor, MEMS accelerometer.

**Layer 2 — Processing / ECU Layer:**
The ECU receives sensor signals, processes them using a microcontroller, and generates actuator commands. ECUs are distributed across the vehicle — a modern car has **70–100 ECUs**.

**Layer 3 — Communication / Network Layer:**
ECUs are interconnected using automotive bus protocols:
- **CAN 2.0 / CAN FD** for powertrain and chassis.
- **LIN** for low-cost body electronics.
- **FlexRay** for high-bandwidth deterministic chassis.
- **Automotive Ethernet** for ADAS and infotainment backbone.

**Layer 4 — Software / Application Layer:**
Standardized software architecture (AUTOSAR Classic):
- **Application Software (ASW)** — vehicle functions.
- **Runtime Environment (RTE)** — middleware / virtual function bus.
- **Basic Software (BSW)** — MCAL, ECU Abstraction, Services (COM, MEM, OS), Diagnostic stack.
- **Microcontroller Hardware.**

*(Refer to Section 4.5 Mermaid diagram for the layered block-level functional flow.)*

#### **(b) Major Components of a Modern ECU**

An ECU is a sealed aluminium die-cast housing containing:

1. **Microcontroller (MCU)** — the brain. Automotive-grade MCUs (e.g., **NXP S32K3**, **Infineon Aurix TC3xx**, **Renesas RH850**) operate at **40–300 MHz**, often using **dual-core lockstep** for ASIL-D safety. Contains CPU cores, on-chip Flash, SRAM, ADCs, PWM timers, CAN/LIN/Ethernet peripherals.

2. **Memory Subsystem** — Flash (program code, calibration), SRAM (runtime data), EEPROM or emulated EEPROM (non-volatile parameters), often with **ECC (Error Correction Code)** for reliability.

3. **Power Management IC (PMIC)** — converts battery voltage (12 V or 24 V) to regulated 5 V, 3.3 V, 1.2 V rails using buck converters and LDOs. Includes **windowed watchdog**, voltage supervisors, and reset management.

4. **Communication Transceivers** — physical-layer chips that interface the digital MCU to the bus:
   - **TJA1044 / TJA1057** for CAN FD.
   - **TJA1027** for LIN.
   - **100BASE-T1 PHY** (e.g., NXP TJA1101) for Automotive Ethernet.

5. **Analog Front-End (AFE)** — operational amplifiers for signal conditioning and active filters.

6. **Output Driver Stages** — high-side/low-side switches (e.g., Infineon BTS50010), H-bridges, and gate drivers to drive actuators.

7. **PCB & Connectors** — multi-layer FR4 PCB, automotive-grade sealed connectors (e.g., USCAR, MX150).

**ECU Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** List and explain the major applications of automotive embedded systems across the powertrain, chassis, body, infotainment, and ADAS domains. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a numerical example, derive the worst-case response time of a brake-by-wire control loop and discuss the design levers an engineer can use to reduce it. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### **(a) Major Applications of Automotive Embedded Systems**

| Domain | Application | Function | Critical ECUs |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Powertrain** | Engine Management | Controls fuel injection, ignition timing, air-fuel ratio, emissions | ECM (ASIL C/D) |
| | Transmission Control | Automatic gear shifting, torque converter lock-up | TCU |
| | Battery Management (EV) | Cell balancing, SoC/SoH estimation, thermal control | BMS |
| **Chassis & Safety** | Anti-lock Braking (ABS) | Prevents wheel lock during hard braking | ABS ECU (ASIL D) |
| | Electronic Stability Control (ESC) | Prevents skidding by selective braking | ESC ECU |
| | Electric Power Steering (EPS) | Assists steering using electric motor | EPS ECU |
| | Airbag System | Deploys airbag within 10 ms of crash detection | ACU |
| **Body & Comfort** | Body Control Module (BCM) | Centralized control of lights, locks, wipers | BCM |
| | HVAC | Climate control | HVAC ECU |
| | Seat / Mirror / Window | Memory positions, anti-pinch | LIN nodes |
| **Infotainment** | Head Unit | Audio, video, navigation, smartphone integration | HU |
| | Instrument Cluster | Digital/analog driver display | IC |
| | Telematics | Remote diagnostics, eCall, OTA updates | TCU |
| **ADAS / Autonomous** | Adaptive Cruise Control | Radar + camera + actuators | Radar ECU + Domain Controller (ASIL B/D) |
| | Lane Keep Assist | Camera-based steering correction | Camera ECU |
| | Automatic Emergency Braking | Forward-collision mitigation | Fusion ECU |

#### **(b) Worst-Case Response Time Derivation**

The brake-by-wire loop response time:

$$
t_{resp} = t_{sense} + t_{compute} + t_{comm} + t_{actuate}
$$

**Step 1 — Sensor time** $t_{sense}$:

$$
t_{sense} = \tau_{SH} + \tau_{sett} + \frac{n}{f_{ADC}} = 2 + 2 + \frac{12}{40\,\text{MHz}} = 4.3\,\mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Compute time** $t_{compute}$:

$$
t_{compute} = \frac{C}{f_{CLK}} = \frac{4000}{300\,\text{MHz}} = 13.33\,\mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Communication time** $t_{comm}$ (CAN FD, 8 bytes, 2 Mbit/s):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{comm} &= \frac{(47 + 8L)}{f_{baud}} \times 1.2 \\
&= \frac{(47 + 64)}{2 \times 10^{6}} \times 1.2 \\
&= 55.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.2 \\
&= 66.6\,\mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Actuator time** $t_{actuate}$ (electro-mechanical brake):

$$
t_{actuate} = \tau_{sol} + \tau_{hyd} = 1\,\text{ms} + 2\,\text{ms} = 3\,\text{ms}
$$

**Total response time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{resp} &= 4.3\,\mu s + 13.33\,\mu s + 66.6\,\mu s + 3000\,\mu s \\
&\approx 3.08\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

This comfortably meets the **ISO 26262 ASIL-D** brake latency requirement of **< 10 ms**.

**Design levers to reduce $t_{resp}$:**

1. **Faster MCU** — increasing $f_{CLK}$ from 300 MHz to 600 MHz halves $t_{compute}$.
2. **Higher bus speed** — CAN FD at 5 Mbit/s reduces $t_{comm}$ by ~2.5×.
3. **Pre-conditioning** in AFE reduces $t_{sense}$ by ~50 %.
4. **Electro-mechanical brake** (eliminate hydraulic fluid) reduces $t_{actuate}$ from ~4 ms to ~1 ms.
5. **Reduce data payload** — compress or use 2-byte CAN signals instead of 8-byte where possible.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Do NOT skip the bit-stuffing factor (×1.2)** in CAN communication time — failure to apply this is the #1 reason students lose 2 marks in timing questions.
> 2. **Always specify ASIL rating and standard** (ISO 26262, AEC-Q100) when mentioning safety / reliability — vague statements like "it is safe" earn zero marks.
> 3. **In comparison tables**, do not just list features — explicitly **contrast** them (e.g., "CAN is event-triggered whereas FlexRay uses TDMA").
> 4. **Do not confuse LIN with CAN** — LIN is single-master, single-wire, 19.2 kbit/s, used only for low-speed body electronics.
> 5. **For derivation questions**, label every variable in your equation AND substitute numerical values with units — equations without units are treated as incomplete by KTU examiners.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Components and Applications of Automotive Embedded Systems**

**📌 Core Definitions**
- ☐ Automotive Embedded System = dedicated, real-time, distributed MCU-based subsystem in a vehicle.
- ☐ ECU = Electronic Control Unit — the "brain" executing a control function.
- ☐ RTOS = Real-Time Operating System (e.g., OSEK/VDX, AUTOSAR OS) — guarantees bounded task latency.
- ☐ AUTOSAR = AUTomotive Open System ARchitecture — standardized software architecture for ECUs.

**📌 Key Components (Hardware)**
- ☐ Sensors: MAP, MAF, TPS, O₂, crank/cam position, wheel speed, accelerometer, radar/lidar.
- ☐ Microcontrollers: NXP S32K3, Infineon Aurix TC3xx, Renesas RH850, TI TMS570.
- ☐ Memory: Flash (program), SRAM (runtime), EEPROM (calibration), often with ECC.
- ☐ PMIC: Buck/LDO regulators + windowed watchdog + voltage supervisor.
- ☐ Transceivers: TJA1044 (CAN), TJA1027 (LIN), 100BASE-T1 PHY (Ethernet).
- ☐ Driver ICs: BTS50010 (high-side), H-bridges for motors.

**📌 Key Components (Software)**
- ☐ AUTOSAR layers: ASW → RTE → BSW (MCAL, ECU Abstraction, Services) → MCU.
- ☐ Diagnostics: UDS (ISO 14229), OBD-II (SAE J1979).
- ☐ Security: Secure Boot, HSM (Hardware Security Module), SecOC.

**📌 Communication Buses (Must Memorize)**
- ☐ **CAN 2.0** — 1 Mbit/s, differential, multi-master, 8-byte payload, CSMA/BA.
- ☐ **CAN FD** — 5 Mbit/s, 64-byte payload.
- ☐ **LIN** — 19.2 kbit/s, single-wire, single-master, low cost.
- ☐ **FlexRay** — 10 Mbit/s, TDMA, hard real-time.
- ☐ **Automotive Ethernet** — 100/1000BASE-T1, switched, for ADAS and backbone.

**📌 Key Standards**
- ☐ ISO 26262 — Functional Safety (ASIL A to D).
- ☐ AEC-Q100 — IC stress test qualification.
- ☐ ISO 11898 — CAN standard.
- ☐ ISO 14229 — Unified Diagnostic Services (UDS).
- ☐ ISO 17987 — LIN.
- ☐ ISO 17458 — FlexRay.

**📌 Key Formulas (Always derive, never just quote)**
- ☐ $t_{bit} = 1/f_{baud}$
- ☐ $V_{LSB} = V_{ref}/2^{n}$
- ☐ $t_{resp} = t_{sense} + t_{compute} + t_{comm} + t_{actuate}$
- ☐ $t_{comm} = \frac{(47 + 8L)}{f_{baud}} \times 1.2$
- ☐ $D = T_{on}/T_{period}$ (PWM duty cycle)
- ☐ $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist)

**📌 Top Applications (Categorize in answers)**
- ☐ Powertrain → Engine, Transmission, BMS.
- ☐ Chassis & Safety → ABS, ESC, EPS, Airbag.
- ☐ Body & Comfort → BCM, HVAC, Lighting, Seats.
- ☐ Infotainment → Head Unit, Cluster, Telematics.
- ☐ ADAS → Radar, Camera, Domain Controller.

**📌 Sample KTU One-Liners (Use in answers for easy marks)**
- ☐ "A modern car contains ~70–100 ECUs and ~100 million lines of code."
- ☐ "Airbag deployment must occur within 10 ms — an ASIL-D timing requirement."
- ☐ "AEC-Q100 grade-1 mandates operation from −40 °C to +125 °C."
- ☐ "Automotive Ethernet uses a single twisted pair with full-duplex 1 Gbit/s."
- ☐ "LIN is a single-master, 19.2 kbit/s, single-wire sub-bus used as a slave to CAN."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microcontroller Based Embedded System Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT632 - AUTOMOTIVE EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Microcontroller Based Embedded System Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Microcontroller-Based Embedded System Architecture** refers to the structured, hierarchical organization of hardware and software components within a single-chip computing unit (the microcontroller) that is dedicated to performing a specific, predefined set of tasks inside a larger mechanical or electronic system, most notably an **Automotive Electronic Control Unit (ECU)**.

In strict KTU 2024 terminology, this architecture is defined by the integration of three primary subsystems onto a single Integrated Circuit (IC) die:

1. **Central Processing Unit (CPU) Core** – The arithmetic-logic decision-making engine (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V, Infineon TriCore).
2. **On-Chip Memory Subsystem** – Comprising Read-Only Memory (ROM/Flash) for program storage and Random-Access Memory (RAM) for volatile data.
3. **Programmable Peripheral Set** – Timers, Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADC), Digital-to-Analog Converters (DAC), communication controllers (CAN, LIN, SPI, UART, I²C), and General-Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) ports.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike a general-purpose computer (e.g., a desktop PC), an embedded microcontroller is *resource-constrained* — meaning it has limited processing power, memory, and energy budget — but it is **real-time deterministic**, meaning it must respond to external events within strict, bounded time deadlines (e.g., an airbag ECU must deploy within **10 ms** of crash detection).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Factory Supervisor"

Imagine a **factory supervisor** standing on a shop floor:

| Human Factory Supervisor | Microcontroller in an Automotive ECU |
|---|---|
| The supervisor's **brain** (decision-making) | The **CPU Core** (fetches, decodes, executes instructions) |
| The supervisor's **clipboard & rulebook** (standing instructions) | **Flash/ROM memory** (stores the compiled program code permanently) |
| The supervisor's **notepad** (temporary calculations, sensor readings) | **RAM memory** (volatile working memory for variables and stack) |
| The supervisor's **wristwatch & stopwatch** (timing) | **Hardware Timers & PWM modules** |
| The supervisor's **ears & eyes** (sensors) | **ADC, GPIO, Encoder interfaces** |
| The supervisor's **mouth & walkie-talkie** (outputs) | **DAC, GPIO, CAN Transceiver** |
| The supervisor's **power source** (energy to work) | **Voltage regulator & clock oscillator** |

Just as a factory supervisor does not need a smartphone or a web browser — they only need the specific tools to oversee their plant — an automotive microcontroller is **stripped down** to contain *only* the circuitry necessary to read sensors, make decisions, and drive actuators (fuel injectors, solenoids, motors) in a vehicle.

---

## 1.3 The "Automotive" Context — Why This Architecture Matters in PEMUT632

In an automobile, dozens of these "supervisors" (ECUs) coexist on a high-speed network bus. The famous ones are:

- **ECM (Engine Control Module)** – Manages fuel injection, ignition timing, and idle speed. Typical cycle time: **5 ms to 10 ms**.
- **TCU (Transmission Control Unit)** – Controls automatic gear shifting.
- **ABS ECU (Anti-lock Braking System)** – Prevents wheel lockup. Cycle time: **2 ms to 5 ms** (extremely critical).
- **BCM (Body Control Module)** – Operates lights, wipers, power windows. Cycle time: **20 ms to 100 ms** (less critical).
- **ADAS ECU** – Adaptive Cruise Control, Lane Keep Assist. Uses higher-end 32-bit microcontrollers (e.g., NXP S32K3, Renesas RH850, Infineon AURIX TC3xx).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Automotive MCU Families You MUST Know (KTU 2024):**
> - **8-bit legacy:** Freescale (NXP) HC08/HC11, Atmel AVR (used in older body ECUs).
> - **16-bit legacy:** Freescale HC12/S12, Renesas M16C.
> - **32-bit modern:** ARM Cortex-M0/M3/M4/M7, ARM Cortex-R5 (for safety-critical), Infineon TriCore (AURIX), Renesas RH850, NXP S32K3, Texas Instruments TMS570.
> - **System-on-Chip (SoC) for ADAS:** NXP i.MX8, Renesas R-Car, Qualcomm Snapdragon Ride.

---

## 1.4 Core Architectural Distinctions (Foundational for KTU Exams)

A KTU examiner will almost always test the difference between the two dominant processor-memory connection models. Memorize this:

| Feature | Von Neumann Architecture | Harvard Architecture |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory structure** | Single unified memory for program + data | Two separate memories: one for program (ROM/Flash), one for data (RAM) |
| **Bus structure** | Single shared bus (one address + one data bus) | Separate buses for instruction fetch and data access |
| **Throughput** | Lower (bottleneck — *von Neumann bottleneck*) | Higher (parallel fetch + execute) |
| **Determinism** | Lower — execution time varies with mix of instructions | Higher — deterministic instruction fetch time (vital for **real-time** ECU tasks) |
| **Examples in automotive** | x86 PCs (not typical automotive) | ARM Cortex-M (modified Harvard), PIC, AVR, 8051, Infineon TriCore |
| **KTU exam relevance** | "What is the von Neumann bottleneck?" | "Why is Harvard preferred for real-time automotive ECUs?" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for KTU 2024:** Most modern automotive microcontrollers (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4) use a **Modified Harvard Architecture** — they have separate caches/buses internally for speed, but expose a unified linear address space to the programmer (von Neumann-style programming model).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven-Layer Functional Architecture of an Automotive Microcontroller System

An embedded system architecture is best understood as a stack of interacting layers. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to be able to **draw and label** the full block diagram and explain the role of each block.

### Layer 1 — The Application Layer (Software)
- The high-level **C/C++ algorithm** that runs the control loop (e.g., a Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller for engine RPM).
- Compiled and stored in Flash memory.

### Layer 2 — The Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) Layer (Optional but Common in Modern ECUs)
- **AUTOSAR OS** (Automotive Open System Architecture) is the industry standard RTOS for KTU-era automotive systems.
- Schedules multiple tasks (e.g., "read crank sensor" every 2 ms, "read coolant temp" every 100 ms) using **preemptive priority scheduling**.

### Layer 3 — The Firmware / Device Driver Layer
- Software routines that directly manipulate hardware registers (e.g., `ADC0->SC1[0] = 0x41;` to start an ADC conversion on channel 1).

### Layer 4 — The CPU Core Layer
- The actual microprocessor architecture (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4 with **Thumb-2 instruction set**).
- Contains the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, **Register Bank**, and **Program Counter (PC)**.

### Layer 5 — The Memory Layer
- **Flash (ROM):** 256 KB to 16 MB — non-volatile program storage.
- **SRAM:** 16 KB to 2 MB — volatile data memory.
- **EEPROM/EFlash:** 4 KB to 64 KB — for small non-volatile parameters (e.g., odometer, learned idle speed).
- **Cache:** 0 to 32 KB — L1 instruction + data cache (in higher-end Cortex-M7).

### Layer 6 — The Peripheral Layer
- Timers, PWM, ADC, CAN, SPI, I²C, UART, GPIO, DMA controllers.

### Layer 7 — The Physical / Hardware Interface Layer
- Voltage regulators, crystal oscillators, ECU connectors, PCB traces, ESD protection diodes.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Execution Model (The "How" Behind the Architecture)

When a microcontroller runs a program, the following sequence repeats billions of times per second:

1. **Clock Generation:** A crystal oscillator (e.g., **8 MHz**) feeds the Phase-Locked Loop (PLL), which multiplies the frequency up to the system clock (e.g., **168 MHz** on a Cortex-M4).
2. **Fetch:** The CPU reads the next instruction from Flash memory using the **I-Bus** (Instruction Bus). The instruction address comes from the **Program Counter (PC)**.
3. **Decode:** The instruction decoder figures out *what* the instruction does (e.g., ADD, MOV, BNE).
4. **Operand Fetch:** If the instruction needs data, the CPU reads it from SRAM via the **D-Bus** (Data Bus).
5. **Execute:** The ALU performs the operation.
6. **Write-Back:** The result is stored in a register or back to memory.
7. **Interrupt Check:** The CPU checks if any interrupt is pending (e.g., a CAN message arrived, a timer overflowed). If yes, it saves context, jumps to the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR), and returns afterward.

This cycle is called the **Fetch-Decode-Execute (FDE) cycle** or **Instruction Cycle**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table. These formulas appear in almost every KTU 2024 exam paper for this module.**

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Typical Unit | Notes for KTU |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Instruction Cycle Time (T-cycle)** | $T_{cyc}$ | $T_{cyc} = \dfrac{1}{f_{CLK}}$ | seconds (s) or microseconds (µs) | Inverse of clock frequency. |
| **Machine Cycle Time** | $T_{MC}$ | $T_{MC} = k \cdot T_{cyc}$ | s, µs | $k$ = clock cycles per instruction (typically $k=1, 2, 4,$ or $12$ depending on architecture). |
| **Instruction Throughput** | $IPS$ | $IPS = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{k}$ | Instructions Per Second (IPS) | For 8051: $k=12$; for ARM Cortex-M: $k=1$ (pipelined). |
| **Time to execute N instructions** | $T_N$ | $T_N = N \cdot T_{MC}$ | s, µs, ms | Multiply by number of instructions. |
| **ADC Conversion Time** | $T_{ADC}$ | $T_{ADC} = (N+2) \cdot T_{ADCLK}$ | µs | $N$ = ADC resolution in bits (e.g., 10 or 12); $T_{ADCLK}$ = ADC clock period. |
| **Timer Tick Frequency** | $f_{tick}$ | $f_{tick} = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{Prescaler}$ | Hz | Timer counts every $1/f_{tick}$ seconds. |
| **PWM Frequency** | $f_{PWM}$ | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{Prescaler \cdot (Period+1)}$ | Hz | Common for motor/LED control. |
| **PWM Duty Cycle** | $D$ | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{PWM}} \times 100$ | % | $D = \dfrac{Compare}{Period} \times 100$ in register terms. |
| **CAN Bus Bit Rate** | $f_{CAN}$ | $f_{CAN} = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{Prescaler \cdot (TSEG1 + TSEG2 + 1)}$ | bps | E.g., 500 kbps standard in CAN. |
| **Power Consumption (CMOS)** | $P$ | $P = C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$ | Watts (W) | $C$ = switching capacitance, $V$ = supply voltage, $f$ = clock frequency. |
| **Memory Addressable Size** | $M$ | $M = 2^n$ | bytes | $n$ = number of address bus lines. A 16-bit address bus $\rightarrow 2^{16} = 64$ KB. |

> **Critical Reminder for the Formula Table:** Since KTU papers often ask you to "calculate the time to execute a 1000-instruction loop," memorize the **8051 ($k=12$)** and **ARM Cortex-M ($k=1$)** constants. Mixing them up is a **3-mark deduction** in exams.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility — Why an Engineer Must Know This

- **Engine Control:** The CPU must finish all fuel-injection calculations before the next crankshaft tooth (typically every **6 degrees of rotation = ~1 ms at 6000 RPM**). If the math is slow, the engine misfires.
- **Braking (ABS):** The CPU has ~2 ms to read 4 wheel-speed sensors, decide if any wheel is locking, and command a hydraulic valve. Insufficient architecture performance = brake failure.
- **Battery Management (BMS):** A Battery Management System in an EV uses an MCU to monitor hundreds of cell voltages. Low-power architecture (e.g., ARM Cortex-M0+) is chosen to minimize quiescent current drain to **microamperes**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

In this section, we will derive the four most commonly asked numerical answers in KTU 2024 paper Part-B questions on this topic. **Do not skip steps** — KTU examiners award partial marks for *every* correct intermediate step.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Instruction Execution Time for an 8051-Based ECU

> **Problem Statement (KTU-type):** A legacy automotive ECU uses an **8-bit 8051 microcontroller** running at an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. The 8051 architecture uses **12 clock cycles per machine cycle ($k=12$)**. Calculate:
> (a) The duration of one clock cycle.
> (b) The duration of one machine cycle.
> (c) The time taken to execute a fuel-injection ISR that contains **450 machine cycles**.
> (d) Verify whether this fits inside the **5 ms** crankshaft event deadline.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**(a) Clock cycle period:**

The clock period is the reciprocal of the clock frequency. Substitute $f_{CLK} = 11.0592$ MHz $= 11.0592 \times 10^6$ Hz.

$$
T_{cyc} = \frac{1}{f_{CLK}} = \frac{1}{11.0592 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}}
$$

$$
T_{cyc} = 0.09042 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 90.42 \text{ ns}
$$

**(b) Machine cycle duration:**

The 8051 requires 12 oscillator periods to complete one machine cycle (i.e., to fetch, decode, and execute a single 1-byte instruction). Multiply the clock period by 12.

$$
T_{MC} = k \cdot T_{cyc} = 12 \times 90.42 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{MC} = 1085.07 \text{ ns} \approx 1.085 \text{ µs}
$$

**(c) Total time for 450 machine cycles:**

Multiply the duration of one machine cycle by the number of machine cycles required by the ISR.

$$
T_{ISR} = N \cdot T_{MC} = 450 \times 1.085 \text{ µs}
$$

$$
T_{ISR} = 488.33 \text{ µs} \approx 0.488 \text{ ms}
$$

**(d) Real-time deadline check:**

We must confirm that $T_{ISR}$ is strictly less than the available deadline of 5 ms.

$$
T_{ISR} = 0.488 \text{ ms} \quad < \quad T_{deadline} = 5 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Result:** The ISR uses approximately **9.77 %** of the available time budget. This is well within the deadline, leaving CPU headroom for other tasks like sensor reading and CAN transmission.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ADC Conversion Time for a Crank Position Sensor

> **Problem Statement:** A 32-bit ARM Cortex-M4 ECU is reading a crankshaft position sensor through its **12-bit Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC**. The peripheral bus clock is **84 MHz**, and the ADC is configured with a prescaler of **4** and a sampling time of **15 ADC clock cycles** plus **12.5 conversion cycles** (standard for STM32). Calculate:
> (a) The ADC clock frequency.
> (b) The total ADC conversion time for one sample.
> (c) The maximum ADC sampling rate (samples per second).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**(a) ADC peripheral clock:**

The ADC clock is derived from the system peripheral clock divided by the prescaler. Substitute $f_{PCLK} = 84$ MHz and $Prescaler = 4$.

$$
f_{ADCLK} = \frac{f_{PCLK}}{Prescaler} = \frac{84 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}}{4}
$$

$$
f_{ADCLK} = 21 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 21 \text{ MHz}
$$

**(b) Total conversion time per sample:**

A STM32 SAR ADC requires 15 sampling cycles plus 12.5 conversion cycles — a total of 27.5 ADC clock cycles per sample. Multiply by the period of the ADC clock.

$$
T_{ADC} = 27.5 \cdot T_{ADCLK} = 27.5 \times \frac{1}{21 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_{ADC} = 1.3095 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \approx 1.31 \text{ µs}
$$

**(c) Maximum sampling rate:**

The sampling rate is the reciprocal of the conversion time.

$$
f_{sample} = \frac{1}{T_{ADC}} = \frac{1}{1.3095 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
f_{sample} \approx 763,600 \text{ samples/second} \approx 763.6 \text{ kS/s}
$$

> **Result:** At ~764 kS/s, the ADC is fast enough to sample the crank position at the **highest engine speeds (e.g., 10,000 RPM = 167 Hz mechanical; tooth-by-tooth at 60-2 = 36 teeth/rev = 6 kHz)** with massive oversampling for noise rejection.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PWM Duty Cycle for an Electronic Throttle Body

> **Problem Statement:** An electronic throttle body uses a DC servo motor driven by a **20 kHz PWM signal** from a timer with a **84 MHz clock**, prescaler of **84**, and auto-reload register (**ARR**) value of **49**. The throttle is currently at **30 % opening**, meaning the Compare Register (CCR) value is set to 15. Calculate:
> (a) The PWM period.
> (b) The actual PWM frequency achieved.
> (c) The achieved duty cycle in percent.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**(a) Theoretical PWM period:**

The PWM period equals (ARR + 1) timer ticks, each of duration $1 / f_{tick}$. First compute the tick frequency.

$$
f_{tick} = \frac{f_{CLK}}{Prescaler} = \frac{84 \times 10^6}{84} = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ MHz}
$$

$$
T_{tick} = 1 \text{ µs}
$$

$$
T_{PWM} = (ARR + 1) \cdot T_{tick} = (49 + 1) \times 1 \text{ µs} = 50 \text{ µs}
$$

**(b) Achieved PWM frequency:**

The frequency is the reciprocal of the period.

$$
f_{PWM} = \frac{1}{T_{PWM}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 20{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 20 \text{ kHz} \quad \checkmark
$$

**(c) Achieved duty cycle:**

Duty cycle equals the ratio of the compare register to (ARR + 1), expressed as a percentage.

$$
D = \frac{CCR}{ARR + 1} \times 100 = \frac{15}{50} \times 100 = 30 \% \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Result:** The configuration produces an exact 20 kHz, 30 % duty cycle — perfect for the throttle servo.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — CAN Bus Bit Timing

> **Problem Statement:** A CAN bus in a car operates at **500 kbps**. The CAN peripheral clock is **42 MHz**, prescaler = 6, $TSEG1 = 13$, $TSEG2 = 2$, and $SJW = 1$. Verify the configuration.

### Step-by-Step Solution

The CAN bit time consists of the Sync Segment (1 fixed time quantum, $T_q$) + TSEG1 + TSEG2.

$$
T_q = \frac{Prescaler}{f_{CLK}} = \frac{6}{42 \times 10^6} = 142.86 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
N_{tq} = 1 + TSEG1 + TSEG2 = 1 + 13 + 2 = 16
$$

$$
T_{bit} = N_{tq} \cdot T_q = 16 \times 142.86 \text{ ns} = 2285.7 \text{ ns} \approx 2.286 \text{ µs}
$$

$$
f_{CAN} = \frac{1}{T_{bit}} = \frac{1}{2.286 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 437.5 \text{ kbps}
$$

> **Result:** This gives 437.5 kbps, **not 500 kbps**. To achieve exactly 500 kbps, the student must reconfigure the prescaler. This is a common KTU pitfall.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Common Automotive Microcontrollers

| Feature | **Infineon AURIX TC3xx** | **NXP S32K3** | **Renesas RH850** | **STM32 (Cortex-M4)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bit-width** | 32-bit (TriCore) | 32-bit (Cortex-M7) | 32-bit (RH850 G3/G4) | 32-bit (Cortex-M4) |
| **Clock Speed** | 300 MHz (multicore) | 240 MHz | 240 MHz | 180 MHz |
| **Flash** | 16 MB | 8 MB | 12 MB | 2 MB |
| **ASIL-D Ready** | Yes (built-in lockstep cores) | Yes | Yes | No (ASIL-B only) |
| **Typical Use** | Powertrain, ADAS, EV inverter | Body, gateway, BMS | Engine, transmission | Low-end body, sensors |
| **No. of CAN-FD** | Up to 12 | Up to 8 | Up to 12 | 2 |
| **Cost** | $$$$ | $$$ | $$$$ | $$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> **Note on diagrams:** Mermaid `flowchart` syntax is used because it gives the cleanest, most academically faithful output for hierarchical block diagrams. All node IDs are alphanumeric per the safeguard rules.

---

## 4.1 Generic Microcontroller Architecture (Top-Level Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXT["EXTERNAL WORLD (Automotive Sensors and Actuators)"]
        SENSOR["Sensors: Crank, Cam, MAP, MAF, Lambda, Wheel Speed"]
        ACTUATOR["Actuators: Injector, Ignition Coil, Solenoid, Motor"]
    end

    subgraph MCU["MICROCONTROLLER CHIP"]
        CPU["CPU CORE: Arithmetic Logic Unit + Register Bank + Program Counter + Stack Pointer"]
        MEM["MEMORY: Flash Program Memory + SRAM Data Memory + EEPROM"]
        PERI["PERIPHERALS: Timer PWM + ADC + CAN + SPI + I2C + UART + GPIO + DMA"]
        CLOCK["CLOCK SUBSYSTEM: Crystal Oscillator + PLL + Clock Dividers"]
        POWER["POWER SUBSYSTEM: Voltage Regulator + Brown-out Detector"]
    end

    SENSOR -- "Analog or digital signals" --> PERI
    PERI -- "Processed values to CPU" --> CPU
    CPU -- "Reads instructions and data" --> MEM
    CPU -- "Control commands" --> PERI
    PERI -- "PWM and logic outputs" --> ACTUATOR
    CLOCK -- "Synchronization ticks" --> CPU
    CLOCK -- "Peripheral clock" --> PERI
    POWER -- "Stable Vcc rails: 5V, 3.3V, 1.2V" --> MCU
```

---

## 4.2 Von Neumann vs Harvard Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VN["VON NEUMANN ARCHITECTURE"]
        VN_CPU["CPU"]
        VN_MEM["Unified Memory: Holds Both Program and Data"]
        VN_BUS["Single Shared Bus: Address + Data + Control"]
        VN_CPU <--> VN_BUS
        VN_BUS <--> VN_MEM
    end

    subgraph HV["HARVARD ARCHITECTURE"]
        HV_CPU["CPU"]
        HV_PMEM["Program Memory: Flash or ROM"]
        HV_DMEM["Data Memory: SRAM"]
        HV_IBUS["Instruction Bus: Separate"]
        HV_DBUS["Data Bus: Separate"]
        HV_CPU <--> HV_IBUS
        HV_IBUS <--> HV_PMEM
        HV_CPU <--> HV_DBUS
        HV_DBUS <--> HV_DMEM
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Automotive ECU Functional Architecture (How an Engine ECU is Built)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ECU["ENGINE CONTROL UNIT ECU"]
        subgraph INPUT["INPUT SIGNAL CONDITIONING"]
            I1["Crank Position Sensor Signal Conditioning"]
            I2["Cam Position Sensor Signal Conditioning"]
            I3["Manifold Air Pressure MAP Amplifier and Filter"]
            I4["Lambda Oxygen Sensor Bias Circuit"]
        end

        subgraph MCUCORE["MICROCONTROLLER CORE"]
            MCPU["CPU Core ARM Cortex M4"]
            MRAM["SRAM 256 KB"]
            MFLASH["Flash 2 MB"]
            MTIMER["Timers and PWM Generators"]
            MADC["12 bit SAR ADC 24 channels"]
            MCAN["CAN 2.0 and CAN FD Controller"]
            MDMA["DMA Controller 8 channels"]
        end

        subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT DRIVER STAGE"]
            O1["Fuel Injector Driver: Low side N-channel MOSFET"]
            O2["Ignition Coil IGBT Driver"]
            O3["Idle Air Control Stepper Motor Driver"]
            O4["Variable Valve Timing VVT Solenoid Driver: PWM at 100 Hz"]
        end

        subgraph COMMS["VEHICLE NETWORK TRANSCEIVERS"]
            C1["CAN Transceiver TJA1051"]
            C2["LIN Transceiver for Auxiliary"]
        end
    end

    I1 --> MADC
    I2 --> MADC
    I3 --> MADC
    I4 --> MADC
    MCPU --> MRAM
    MCPU --> MFLASH
    MCPU --> MTIMER
    MTIMER --> O1
    MTIMER --> O2
    MTIMER --> O3
    MTIMER --> O4
    MCPU --> MCAN
    MCAN --> C1
    C1 --> VEH["VEHICLE CAN BUS: 500 kbps"]
    C2 --> VEH
```

---

## 4.4 Memory Map of a Typical 32-bit Automotive Microcontroller

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAP["LINEAR ADDRESS MAP 32 bit 4 GB Total"]
        A["0x0000 0000 to 0x1FFF FFFF: Code Flash 512 MB"]
        B["0x2000 0000 to 0x200F FFFF: SRAM 1 MB"]
        C["0x4000 0000 to 0x5FFF FFFF: Peripheral Register Space 512 MB"]
        D["0x6000 0000 to 0x7FFF FFFF: External Memory Controller 512 MB"]
        E["0x8000 0000 to 0x9FFF FFFF: QSPI External Flash 512 MB"]
        F["0xE000 0000 to 0xE00F FFFF: System Control Space NVIC, MPU, SysTick 1 MB"]
    end

    A --- B --- C --- D --- E --- F
```

---

## 4.5 CPU Execution Pipeline (ARM Cortex-M3/M4)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Stage 1: FETCH Instruction from Flash"]
    S2["Stage 2: DECODE Instruction and Read Registers"]
    S3["Stage 3: EXecute ALU Operation"]
    S4["Stage 4: WRITEBack Result to Register File"]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4
    S4 -.->|"Next Instruction"| S1
```

> **Explanation:** This 3-stage (or 4-stage for M4 with branch prediction) pipeline allows the CPU to overlap the execution of multiple instructions, achieving an effective **1 instruction per clock cycle** for most code, in stark contrast to the 8051's **12 clocks per instruction**.

---

## 4.6 Interrupt Handling Flow (How the ECU Reacts to a Crank Tooth)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Engine Crankshaft Tooth Detected by Hall Sensor"] --> B["GPIO Pin Edge Triggered Interrupt"]
    B --> C["CPU Finishes Current Instruction"]
    C --> D["Pushes Registers to Stack: R0-R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR"]
    D --> E["Loads ISR Address from Vector Table"]
    E --> F["Executes Interrupt Service Routine: Read Time Stamp, Update Crank Angle"]
    F --> G["Clear Interrupt Flag"]
    G --> H["Pop Registers from Stack"]
    H --> I["Return to Main Loop"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2-Mark Conceptual / 3-Mark Definitions)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]
**Q: Differentiate between Von Neumann and Harvard architectures. Why is the Harvard architecture preferred for real-time automotive embedded systems? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Single unified memory | Separate program and data memories |
| Bus | Single shared bus | Separate instruction and data buses |
| Bottleneck | Yes (Von Neumann bottleneck) | No (parallel fetch and execute) |
| Determinism | Lower | Higher (predictable timing) |
| Examples | Desktop PCs | 8051, AVR, ARM Cortex-M, PIC, Infineon TriCore |

> **Why Harvard for automotive:** Real-time ECUs (e.g., ABS, airbag) require **deterministic worst-case execution time (WCET)** for safety. Harvard's separate buses allow the CPU to fetch the next instruction *in parallel* with reading data from RAM, eliminating the bus contention that would otherwise make timing unpredictable. In safety-critical ECUs certified to **ASIL-D** under **ISO 26262**, deterministic timing is mandatory, which is why almost all automotive microcontrollers use Harvard or Modified Harvard architectures.

*(3 marks: 1 for table differentiation, 2 for real-time reasoning)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]
**Q: List any three on-chip peripherals commonly found in an automotive microcontroller, and state one automotive application for each. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **CAN Controller** — Used for communication between ECUs on the vehicle's CAN bus (e.g., ECM ↔ TCM). Application: Engine-to-transmission torque coordination.
2. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — Used to read analog sensor voltages. Application: Reading the MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure) sensor output.
3. **Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM) Timer** — Used to generate variable-duty-cycle control signals. Application: Driving an electronic throttle body motor or fuel-injection solenoid.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per peripheral with correct application)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** Each Part-B question is worth **14 marks** and has sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth **7 marks**. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B (full internal choice). For each sub-part, a **complete step-by-step model solution** is given.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 MARKS) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a typical automotive embedded system** based on a 32-bit microcontroller. Clearly identify the CPU core, memory, and at least **four peripherals** relevant to automotive applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An automotive ECU uses an **ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller** running at **168 MHz**. The processor uses a **pipelined architecture with 1 clock cycle per instruction ($k=1$)**. The engine control algorithm contains a critical inner loop of **2500 machine instructions** that must complete before the next crankshaft tooth at **6000 RPM with a 60-2 tooth wheel**.

(i) Calculate the time taken to execute one full loop. **(3 marks)**
(ii) Calculate the available time between two consecutive teeth. **(2 marks)**
(iii) Determine the **CPU utilization percentage** and comment on whether the design meets the real-time deadline. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution for A(a)

**Block Diagram:** (Draw the diagram from **Section 4.1** above — the 7-block generic microcontroller architecture.) Label and explain each block:

- **CPU Core (ARM Cortex-M4):** 32-bit RISC processor with Thumb-2 instruction set, hardware single-precision FPU, NVIC for interrupts.
- **Memory:** Flash (1–2 MB) for program, SRAM (256 KB) for data, EEPROM emulation for odometer.
- **Peripherals (4 relevant ones):**
  1. **12-bit SAR ADC** (≥16 channels) — for MAP, MAF, lambda, temperature sensors.
  2. **CAN 2.0B / CAN-FD controller** — for vehicle network communication.
  3. **16-bit Timers with PWM** — for injector and ignition timing control.
  4. **DMA Controller** — to offload ADC and SPI transfers from the CPU.

**Brief explanation (2 marks):** The CPU fetches instructions from Flash via the I-Bus, reads sensor data via the ADC, performs control calculations, and drives actuators via PWM outputs or CAN messages. The architecture is **Modified Harvard** for deterministic real-time performance.

#### Model Solution for A(b)

**(i) Time per loop:** Substitute $N = 2500$ instructions, $f_{CLK} = 168$ MHz, $k = 1$ into the instruction-execution formula.

$$
T_{cyc} = \frac{1}{168 \times 10^6} = 5.952 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{MC} = k \cdot T_{cyc} = 1 \times 5.952 \text{ ns} = 5.952 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{loop} = N \cdot T_{MC} = 2500 \times 5.952 \text{ ns} = 14{,}881 \text{ ns} \approx 14.88 \text{ µs}
$$

**(ii) Time between teeth:** At 6000 RPM, the crankshaft rotates at 100 revolutions per second. A 60-2 wheel has **58 usable teeth** per revolution (60 minus 2 missing). The tooth event frequency is:

$$
f_{tooth} = \frac{6000}{60} \times 58 = 100 \times 58 = 5800 \text{ events/s}
$$

$$
T_{tooth} = \frac{1}{5800} = 172.41 \text{ µs}
$$

**(iii) CPU utilization and verdict:**

$$
U = \frac{T_{loop}}{T_{tooth}} \times 100 = \frac{14.88}{172.41} \times 100 = 8.63\%
$$

> **Verdict:** The loop uses only **8.63 %** of the available time budget. The design is **safe and meets the deadline** with over 91 % CPU headroom for other tasks (CAN handling, diagnostics, other ISRs).

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 MARKS) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **seven-layer architecture of an automotive embedded system**, from the application software down to the physical hardware. For each layer, give one example in the context of an Engine Control Module. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A body-control ECU uses a **timer to generate a 100 Hz PWM signal** for the HVAC blower motor. The timer clock is **8 MHz**, the prescaler is **8**, and the auto-reload (period) register is **9999**.

(i) Calculate the **timer tick frequency** and **PWM frequency achieved**. **(3 marks)**
(ii) If the desired blower speed is 45 %, what value must be loaded into the **Compare Register (CCR)**? **(2 marks)**
(iii) The ECU's CMOS power consumption is modeled as $P = C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$, with $C = 10$ nF, $V = 3.3$ V, and $f = 168$ MHz. Calculate the dynamic power dissipation. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution for B(a)

| Layer | Role | ECM Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Application** | Highest-level algorithm | PID controller maintaining target idle RPM of 800 rpm |
| **2. RTOS (AUTOSAR)** | Task scheduler | AUTOSAR OS scheduling the "fuel calc" task every 5 ms |
| **3. Firmware / Drivers** | Hardware register manipulation | ADC driver reading coolant temperature |
| **4. CPU Core** | Executes instructions | ARM Cortex-M4 fetching from Flash |
| **5. Memory** | Stores code and data | 2 MB Flash holds the compiled control algorithm |
| **6. Peripherals** | Interfaces to physical world | 12-bit ADC reading the throttle position sensor |
| **7. Physical / Hardware** | Power, clock, connectors | 5 V LDO regulator + 16 MHz crystal + 121-pin ECU connector |

*(7 marks: 1 mark per layer for correctly identifying the role and giving a valid example)*

#### Model Solution for B(b)

**(i) Timer tick and PWM frequencies:**

$$
f_{tick} = \frac{f_{CLK}}{Prescaler} = \frac{8 \times 10^6}{8} = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ MHz}
$$

$$
f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{tick}}{ARR + 1} = \frac{1 \times 10^6}{9999 + 1} = \frac{1 \times 10^6}{10000} = 100 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark
$$

**(ii) Compare Register value for 45 % duty cycle:**

$$
D = \frac{CCR}{ARR + 1} \times 100 \implies CCR = \frac{D \times (ARR + 1)}{100}
$$

$$
CCR = \frac{45 \times 10000}{100} = 4500
$$

**(iii) Dynamic power dissipation:**

$$
P = C \cdot V^2 \cdot f = 10 \times 10^{-9} \times (3.3)^2 \times 168 \times 10^6
$$

$$
P = 10 \times 10^{-9} \times 10.89 \times 168 \times 10^6
$$

$$
P = 10 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.8295 \times 10^9 = 18.295 \text{ W}
$$

> **Result:** The dynamic power is approximately **18.3 W**. This is why automotive ECUs use heat sinks, thermal vias, and low-power sleep modes (where the clock $f$ is reduced to kHz range, cutting power by orders of magnitude).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $k$ values:** Mixing up $k=12$ (8051) and $k=1$ (ARM Cortex-M) costs **3 to 4 marks** instantly. Always state $k$ explicitly.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors:** Writing $11.0592 \times 10^6$ instead of $11.0592$ MHz, or forgetting to convert ns to µs. KTU examiners explicitly look for **correct units in the final answer**.
> 3. **Forgetting the "+1" in PWM period formula:** The PWM period is $(ARR+1) \cdot T_{tick}$, **not** $ARR \cdot T_{tick}$. This is a **classic 2-mark trap**.
> 4. **No block diagram on architecture questions:** A 7-mark question that asks "explain the architecture" *requires* a labeled block diagram. Text-only answer = maximum **4 marks**.
> 5. **Ignoring real-time constraints:** When asked "will the design meet the deadline?", you **must** explicitly state the comparison (e.g., "$T_{loop} = 14.88$ µs $< 172.41$ µs, therefore **meets the deadline**"). Just calculating the loop time without comparing to the deadline loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Writing Harvard instead of Modified Harvard:** Modern automotive MCUs (ARM Cortex-M) are technically **Modified Harvard** internally. If the question specifically asks for the architecture of an ARM-based ECU, writing "Harvard" without the "Modified" prefix is a half-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Pin this list to your study wall before the exam.**

- [x] A **microcontroller** = CPU + Memory + Peripherals **on a single chip**.
- [x] An **embedded system** = a microcontroller (or microprocessor) embedded inside a larger product to perform a dedicated function.
- [x] An **automotive ECU** is the canonical real-world example of a microcontroller-based embedded system.
- [x] **Von Neumann** = single bus, single memory → bottleneck → NOT preferred for real-time.
- [x] **Harvard** = separate instruction + data buses → deterministic → preferred for automotive.
- [x] **Modified Harvard** = modern automotive standard (e.g., ARM Cortex-M).
- [x] **Instruction cycle time:** $T_{cyc} = 1/f_{CLK}$. **Machine cycle:** $T_{MC} = k \cdot T_{cyc}$.
- [x] **8051** uses $k = 12$. **ARM Cortex-M** uses $k = 1$ (pipelined).
- [x] **PWM period** = $(ARR + 1) / f_{tick}$ — never forget the **+1**.
- [x] **PWM duty cycle** $D = (CCR / (ARR+1)) \times 100$ %.
- [x] **ADC conversion time** $T_{ADC} = (\text{sampling cycles} + \text{conversion cycles}) / f_{ADCLK}$.
- [x] **Power dissipation** in CMOS scales as $P \propto C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$ — reducing $V$ and $f$ saves the most power.
- [x] **Real-time safety-critical ECUs** (ABS, airbag, EPS) require **ASIL-D** (ISO 26262) certification → use **lockstep cores** like AURIX TC3xx or RH850.
- [x] **Automotive bus protocols to know:** CAN, CAN-FD, LIN (sub-bus), FlexRay (legacy), Automotive Ethernet (modern).
- [x] **Key MCU families for 2024 vehicles:** Infineon AURIX TC3xx, NXP S32K3, Renesas RH850, STM32 (low-end).
- [x] **AUTOSAR** is the industry-standard RTOS and software architecture framework for modern vehicles.
- [x] **Typical cycle times:** Engine ~5–10 ms, ABS ~2–5 ms, BCM ~20–100 ms, ADAS ~10–50 ms.
- [x] Always **draw a labeled block diagram** for any "explain the architecture" question — never answer with text alone.

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** Module 1 is the foundation. If you can confidently sketch the block diagram from memory, state the $k$-value for both 8051 and ARM, and solve a PWM / ADC / Instruction-cycle numerical problem in under 8 minutes, you are exam-ready. Good luck! 🚗⚙️
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microcontrollers Used in Automotives: Types, Features, and Selection Criteria
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT632 - AUTOMOTIVE EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Microcontrollers Used in Automotives: Types, Features, and Selection Criteria

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Automotive Microcontroller (Automotive MCU)** is a highly integrated, ruggedized, single-chip computing element specifically designed, qualified, and validated to operate reliably within the harsh electrical, thermal, mechanical, and electromagnetic environment of a road vehicle. It functions as the **logical and computational heart of an Electronic Control Unit (ECU)**, executing deterministic firmware to sense, process, and actuate vehicle subsystems such as the engine, brakes, steering, body electronics, and infotainment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An automotive microcontroller is a specialized, real-time, safety-critical embedded processor that meets the **AEC-Q100** (Automotive Electronics Council) stress-test qualification standard and supports in-vehicle networking protocols such as **CAN, LIN, FlexRay, and Automotive Ethernet**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a modern car as a small city. Inside that city, hundreds of decisions need to be made every second:
- *How much fuel should be injected?*
- *Should the airbag deploy in this crash?*
- *How bright should the headlight be on a foggy road?*

The **microcontroller is like the city hall computer** — but a *super-rugged* one. It is built to:
- Survive **-40°C to +125°C** (unlike a normal room-temperature computer),
- Shake violently (engine vibrations),
- Withstand electrical noise (from spark plugs, alternators, motors),
- Make a decision in **microseconds** (real-time response).

So while your laptop's processor is built for *speed and graphics*, an automotive MCU is built for **safety, determinism, and survival**.

## 1.3 Why Automobiles Need Special Microcontrollers

A standard commercial microcontroller (like the Arduino ATmega328) would fail in a car within minutes. The automobile demands:

| Stress Factor | Commercial MCU | Automotive MCU |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Temperature | 0°C to +70°C | **-40°C to +125°C (AEC-Q100 Grade 0)** |
| Vibration Tolerance | Low | High (engine bay mounting) |
| EMI/EMC Immunity | Basic | **ISO 11452 / CISPR 25 compliant** |
| Functional Safety | Not certified | **ISO 26262 ASIL-D ready** |
| Lifetime | 3–5 years | **15+ years / 200,000 km** |
| Real-Time Determinism | Best-effort | **Hard real-time guaranteed** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Functional Safety (ISO 26262):** A risk-based standard that classifies automotive systems into **ASIL-A (lowest) to ASIL-D (highest)** safety integrity levels. An airbag ECU must operate at **ASIL-D**, while a window lifter may operate at **ASIL-A**.

## 1.4 The ECU–MCU Relationship

An **ECU (Electronic Control Unit)** is the entire black box mounted in the car (housing, PCB, connectors, power supply, MCU, sensors/actuator drivers). The **MCU is the silicon "brain" inside the ECU**. Multiple ECUs (often **70–100** in a premium car) communicate over a vehicle network (CAN bus), and the MCU is what runs the software on each ECU.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Automotive Microcontrollers (By Bit-Width)

The bit-width refers to the **datapath / ALU size** — i.e., how many bits the processor manipulates per instruction.

### 2.1.1 8-bit Automotive MCUs
- **Examples:** Freescale (NXP) S08 family, Infineon XC800, Renesas 78K0.
- **Architecture:** CISC, Von Neumann or modified Harvard.
- **Clock:** 8–40 MHz.
- **Typical Use Cases:**
  - Body Control Modules (BCM) – lighting, wipers.
  - Window lifters, mirror adjusters.
  - Simple sensor nodes on LIN slave side.
- **Why used:** Lowest cost, lowest power, deterministic behavior, sufficient for slow mechatronic loops.

### 2.1.2 16-bit Automotive MCUs
- **Examples:** Infineon XC164, TI MSP430F5xx (automotive), Renesas RL78.
- **Architecture:** CISC/RISC, often Harvard.
- **Clock:** 16–80 MHz.
- **Typical Use Cases:**
  - Electric power steering (EPS) entry-level.
  - Transmission control – gear-shift logic.
  - HVAC control modules.

### 2.1.3 32-bit Automotive MCUs (Dominant Today)
- **Examples:** Infineon AURIX (TC3xx), NXP S32K3, Renesas RH850, ST SPC58, Texas Instruments TDA4x.
- **Architecture:** RISC, Harvard (modified), often **multi-core lockstep** for safety.
- **Clock:** 100–600 MHz; some up to 1 GHz.
- **Typical Use Cases:**
  - Engine Control Units (ECU / EMS) – fuel injection, ignition.
  - ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems) – radar, vision.
  - Battery Management Systems (BMS) in EVs.
  - VCU (Vehicle Control Unit) in electric vehicles.
  - Domain controllers (zonal architecture).

### 2.1.4 64-bit / Multi-core SoCs (Emerging)
- **Examples:** NXP S32G, Qualcomm Snapdragon Ride, NVIDIA Orin, Mobileye EyeQ.
- **Used in:** Centralized compute, autonomous driving (L3+), infotainment head units.

## 2.2 Classification by Architecture

### 2.2.1 Von Neumann vs Harvard

| Feature | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Memory for Code & Data | **Single bus, shared** | **Separate buses** |
| Fetch & Execute | Serial (one at a time) | Parallel (overlap) |
| Determinism | Lower (bus contention) | **Higher (deterministic timing)** |
| Usage in Auto | Older 8-bit | **Modern 32-bit automotive** |

### 2.2.2 CISC vs RISC

- **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer):** Fewer instructions but each does more work (e.g., multiply-and-accumulate in one opcode). Used in older Freescale HC08/S12.
- **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer):** Simpler, fixed-length instructions → faster pipelining → higher **MIPS/MHz**. All modern 32-bit automotive MCUs are RISC (ARM Cortex-R, Power Architecture, RH850).

## 2.3 Critical Features of Modern Automotive MCUs

### 2.3.1 On-Chip Peripherals (The "Automotive Specials")

1. **CAN (Controller Area Network) Module**
   - Supports **CAN 2.0A/B** and **CAN-FD** (Flexible Data-rate, up to 5 Mbps).
   - Hardware acceptance filters, hardware timestamping.

2. **LIN (Local Interconnect Network) Module**
   - Single-wire, up to **19.2 kbps**, used for low-cost sub-bus.

3. **FlexRay Module**
   - **10 Mbps**, deterministic TDMA, used in chassis and x-by-wire (legacy, being replaced).

4. **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1 / 1000BASE-T1) MAC**
   - **100 Mbps / 1 Gbps** over a single twisted pair (OABR), backbone of modern E/E architecture.

5. **SENT (Single Edge Nibble Transmission) / PSI5**
   - For sensor interfacing (pressure, position).

6. **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) Timers**
   - For motor control (e.g., 3-phase BLDC for electric pumps).

7. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)**
   - 12-bit, 1 MSPS, often with **hardware-triggered group conversion** synchronized to PWM for deterministic sampling.

8. **eFlash (Embedded Flash)**
   - **OTA-capable** (Over-The-Air firmware update), A/B bank architecture for rollback safety.

### 2.3.2 Safety & Security Features

- **Lockstep Core:** Two cores execute the same code; mismatch triggers a fault (for ASIL-D).
- **ECC (Error-Correcting Code) on Flash & SRAM:** Single-bit error correction, double-bit detection.
- **MPU (Memory Protection Unit):** Prevents code from writing into forbidden regions.
- **HSM (Hardware Security Module):** Secure boot, key storage, cryptographic accelerators (AES, SHA, RSA, ECC) for V2X and OTA security.
- **Watchdog Timers (WDT):** Independent window watchdog — if firmware fails to "pet" the watchdog, ECU resets.

## 2.4 Selection Criteria for Automotive Microcontrollers

A KTU question frequently asks to *justify the choice of MCU for a given application*. The choice is driven by **six pillars**:

1. **Performance (MIPS, Dhrystone DMIPS, CoreMark)**
2. **Memory Size (Flash, RAM, EEPROM/Emulated EEPROM)**
3. **Peripherals (CAN, LIN, Ethernet, PWM channels, ADC)**
4. **Power Consumption (active mA, sleep µA)**
5. **Safety & Security (ISO 26262 ASIL level, HSM presence)**
6. **Cost, Toolchain, Automotive Grade (AEC-Q100), Supply Longevity (15+ years)**

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are the **most-asked** derivations and numerical problems in KTU Module-1 examinations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Instruction Cycle Time (T)** | $T_{cyc} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | Time for one clock pulse. |
| 2 | **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** | $CPI = \dfrac{\text{Total Clock Cycles}}{\text{Instruction Count}}$ | Average cycles per instruction. |
| 3 | **Execution Time of a Program** | $T_{exec} = N \times CPI \times T_{cyc}$ | For $N$ instructions. |
| 4 | **MIPS Rating** | $MIPS = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | Million Instructions Per Second. |
| 5 | **DMIPS / MHz** | $DMIPS/MHz = \dfrac{MIPS}{f_{clk\,in\,MHz}}$ | Normalized Dhrystone performance. |
| 6 | **CAN Bit Time** | $t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{Bit\,Rate}$ | Time per CAN bit. |
| 7 | **CAN Bit Segments** | $t_{bit} = t_{Sync} + t_{Prop} + t_{Phase1} + t_{Phase2}$ | Total nominal bit time. |
| 8 | **Time Quantum (CAN)** | $t_q = \dfrac{BRP}{f_{clk}}$ | Base unit; $BRP$ = Baud-Rate Prescaler. |
| 9 | **Nominum Count of $t_q$ per bit** | $N_{bit} = Sync + Prop + Phase1 + Phase2$ | Integer count, 8–25 typically. |
| 10 | **Bit Rate Formula (CAN)** | $Bit\,Rate = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{BRP \times N_{bit}}$ | Direct bit-rate computation. |
| 11 | **PWM Frequency** | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{(PS+1) \times(PERIOD+1)}$ | Period register based. |
| 12 | **PWM Duty Cycle (%)** | $D = \dfrac{COMPARE}{PERIOD} \times 100$ | Compare-to-period ratio. |
| 13 | **ADC Sampling Rate** | $f_{s} = \dfrac{f_{ADC}}{N_{conv\,cyles}}$ | Channels-per-scan factor $N$. |
| 14 | **Power Dissipation (Dynamic)** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | Activity-factor $\alpha$, capacitance $C$. |
| 15 | **Throughput (Coremark)** | $CM/\text{MHz} = \dfrac{\text{Iterations} \times \text{Score}}{t \times f}$ | Normalized benchmark. |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine ECU:** Needs 32-bit RISC at ~200 MHz, ≥ 4 MB Flash, multiple CAN-FD, hardware crypto (HSM), ASIL-D. → **AURIX TC3xx**.
- **Body Control (BCM):** 16-bit/32-bit, low cost, LIN + CAN, low sleep current. → **NXP S32K1, RL78**.
- **EV Battery Management (BMS):** High-resolution ADC (16-bit sigma-delta), galvanic-isolated CAN, ASIL-C/D. → **AURIX, SPC58**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive, board-mark-worthy derivations** that examiners love to set. Every algebraic step is shown, with reasoning alongside.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Instruction Execution Time

### 3.1.1 Setup
A 32-bit automotive MCU (e.g., AURIX TC377) runs at a clock frequency $f_{clk}$. We are to derive the time taken to execute $N$ instructions.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } &\text{Definition — one clock period (instruction clock cycle)} \\
& T_{cyc} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}} \\
\\
\text{Step 2: } &\text{If CPU is RISC (1 instruction per cycle, CPI = 1):} \\
& T_{per\_instr} = 1 \cdot T_{cyc} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}} \\
\\
\text{Step 3: } &\text{If pipeline has stalls (CPI = 1.5 due to branches/loads):} \\
& T_{per\_instr} = CPI \cdot T_{cyc} \\
\\
\text{Step 4: } &\text{For N instructions total:} \\
& T_{exec} = N \cdot CPI \cdot T_{cyc} = \dfrac{N \cdot CPI}{f_{clk}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Doubling the clock halves execution time, *only* if CPI remains constant (true for RISC pipelines that scale linearly with frequency).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: MIPS Rating

### 3.2.1 Setup
For a CPU with a clock of $f_{clk}$ Hz and CPI = 1, derive MIPS.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } &\text{Instructions per second} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI} \\
\\
\text{Step 2: } &\text{Million Instructions Per Second (MIPS):} \\
& MIPS = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}} \\
\\
\text{Step 3: } &\text{For } f_{clk} = 200\,\text{MHz},\; CPI = 1: \\
& MIPS = \dfrac{200 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 10^{6}} = 200\;\text{MIPS} \\
\\
\text{Step 4: } &\text{DMIPS/MHz (normalized):} \\
& \dfrac{DMIPS}{MHz} = \dfrac{MIPS}{f_{clk}\text{ in MHz}} = \dfrac{200}{200} = 1.0\;\text{DMIPS/MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> AURIX TC3xx delivers ~1.7 DMIPS/MHz (Cortex-R5), so at 300 MHz ≈ 510 DMIPS. This is a *typical KTU numerical*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: CAN Bit-Rate from Time Quanta

### 3.3.1 Setup
The CAN bit time is built from **Time Quanta ($t_q$)** derived from the peripheral clock. Given $f_{clk}$ and prescaler $BRP$, find the achievable bit rate.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } &\text{Time Quantum (smallest unit of CAN bit time):} \\
& t_q = \dfrac{BRP}{f_{clk}} \\
\\
\text{Step 2: } &\text{Nominal bit time is split into 4 segments:} \\
& t_{bit} = (Sync + Prop + Phase1 + Phase2) \cdot t_q \\
& \text{Let } N_{bit} = \text{Sync} + \text{Prop} + Phase1 + \text{Phase2} \\
& \Rightarrow t_{bit} = N_{bit} \cdot t_q = N_{bit} \cdot \dfrac{BRP}{f_{clk}} \\
\\
\text{Step 3: } &\text{Bit Rate is the reciprocal of bit time:} \\
& \text{Bit Rate} = \dfrac{1}{t_{bit}} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{BRP \times N_{bit}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.3 Worked Numerical (KTU-typical)

**Problem:** A CAN module of an MCU is clocked at $f_{clk} = 80\,\text{MHz}$. The prescaler is $BRP = 4$, and the segments are configured as $Sync = 1$, $Prop = 3$, $Phase1 = 4$, $Phase2 = 4$. Find the CAN bit rate.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{bit} &= 1 + 3 + 4 + 4 = 12 \\
\\
\text{Bit Rate} &= \dfrac{80 \times 10^{6}}{4 \times 12} \\
&= \dfrac{80\,000\,000}{48} \\
&= 1\,666\,666.67\;\text{bps} \\
&\approx \mathbf{1.667\;\text{Mbps}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **CAN-FD**, the data phase uses a separate, often higher, prescaler to allow up to 5 Mbps.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: PWM Frequency and Duty Cycle

### 3.4.1 Setup
A timer module of an automotive MCU has $f_{clk} = 100\,\text{MHz}$. Prescaler $PS = 0$, period register $= 999$, compare register $= 250$. Find PWM frequency and duty cycle.

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: PWM Frequency} & \\
f_{PWM} &= \dfrac{f_{clk}}{(PS+1) \cdot (PERIOD+1)} \\
&= \dfrac{100 \times 10^{6}}{(0+1)(999+1)} \\
&= \dfrac{100 \times 10^{6}}{1000} \\
&= 100\,\text{kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Duty Cycle} & \\
D &= \dfrac{COMPARE}{PERIOD} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{250}{999} \times 100 \\
&\approx 25.03\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Use Case in Automotive:** 25% duty at 100 kHz is typical for valve-control solenoids; for a 3-phase BLDC motor driver, the PWM frequency is 10–20 kHz (above audible band).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Performance vs Cost vs Safety

The selection of an automotive MCU is best understood as a **trade-off triangle**. KTU expects students to *map an application to the right point* on this triangle.

| Application Domain | Bit-Width | Architecture | Key Peripherals | ASIL Level | Example MCU |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wiper / Window Lifter | 8 / 16-bit | CISC or low-end RISC | LIN, GPIO, basic timer | QM or ASIL-A | NXP S08, RL78 |
| HVAC Blower Control | 16-bit | RISC | PWM (motor), ADC, LIN | ASIL-A | Infineon XC164 |
| Body Control Module | 32-bit | Cortex-M / R | Multiple CAN-FD, LIN, GPIO | ASIL-B | NXP S32K3, RH850/F1L |
| Engine ECU (EMS) | 32-bit | Cortex-R5 / TriCore | 6× CAN-FD, FlexRay, HSM, 12-bit ADC, eGTM | **ASIL-D** | AURIX TC3xx |
| EPS (Electric Power Steering) | 32-bit | Lockstep RISC | CAN-FD, FlexRay, dual ADC, resolver interface | **ASIL-D** | RH850/F1K, AURIX |
| BMS (EV Battery) | 32-bit | Cortex-R5 | Isolated CAN, 16-bit Σ-Δ ADC, HSM | ASIL-C/D | AURIX, SPC58 |
| ADAS Radar / Vision | 32-bit / 64-bit | Cortex-A72, custom NPU | Ethernet 1 Gbps, MIPI-CSI, PCIe | ASIL-B (perception) | TDA4, Orin, S32V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on ASIL-D:** Only achieved via **lockstep dual-core** (e.g., two Cortex-R5 cores running the same instruction and clock; a comparator checks parity per cycle).

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Why Lockstep Catches Faults

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } & C_1(t), C_2(t) \text{ be the two core outputs at time } t. \\
\text{Comparison: } & \Delta(t) = C_1(t) \oplus C_2(t) \\
\text{If } \Delta(t) &= 0 \Rightarrow \text{outputs agree} \rightarrow \text{continue execution.} \\
\text{If } \Delta(t) &\neq 0 \Rightarrow \text{mismatch detected} \rightarrow \text{enter Safe State (ISO 26262).}
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives **diagnostic coverage > 99%** for single-point faults, which is the basis for ASIL-D.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Since a physical chip drawing is not feasible in Mermaid, the following diagrams represent the **block-level functional architecture and decision flows** of the automotive MCU system.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Modern 32-bit Automotive MCU Internals

```mermaid
graph TD
    clk[Clock and Reset Unit] --> coreA
    clk --> coreB

    coreA[Core 1 Lockstep RISC Cortex R5] --> mpu
    coreB[Core 2 Lockstep RISC Cortex R5] --> mpu
    mpu[MPU Memory Protection Unit] --> busM[System Bus Matrix AHB AXI]

    busM --> flash[Embedded Flash ECC 4 MB]
    busM --> sram[SRAM ECC 512 KB]
    busM --> dmu[DMU Direct Memory Access]

    busM --> pwm[PWM Timer eGTM 6 channels]
    busM --> adc[12 bit ADC 1 MSPS]
    busM --> can1[CAN FD Module 1]
    busM --> can2[CAN FD Module 2]
    busM --> lin[LIN UART Module]
    busM --> eth[100BASE T1 Ethernet MAC]
    busM --> hsm[HSM Security Module AES SHA]
    busM --> wdt[Window Watchdog WDT]

    pwm --> motor[BLDC Motor Driver]
    adc --> sensor[Crankshaft Sensor]
    can1 --> vnet[Vehicle CAN Bus]
    can2 --> vnet
    eth --> adasnet[ADAS Ethernet Backbone]
    lin --> bcn[Body Control Nodes]
    hsm --> ota[OTA Firmware Update Channel]
    wdt --> safe[Safe State if Timeout]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — ECU Architecture (MCU within ECU)

```mermaid
graph TD
    psu[Vehicle Battery 12V] --> smps[SMPS 5V and 3.3V]
    smps --> mcu[Automotive MCU Core]

    sens1[Crankshaft Sensor] --> cond[Signal Conditioning]
    cond --> adc[MCU ADC Input]
    sens2[Coolant Temp Sensor] --> adc
    sens3[Throttle Position Sensor] --> adc

    mcu --> canphy[CAN Transceiver]
    canphy --> canbus[CAN Bus to Other ECUs]

    mcu --> injdrv[Injector Driver Stage]
    injdrv --> inj[Fuel Injector]
```
