```mermaid
    igndrv --> coil[Ignition Coil]
```

## 4.3 Flowchart — MCU Selection Methodology for an Automotive Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Application Requirement] --> B{Real Time Deadline Hard or Soft}
    B -- Hard --> C[Need 32 bit RISC Lockstep]
    B -- Soft --> D[8 or 16 bit Sufficient]

    C --> E{Safety Goal ISO 26262}
    E -- ASIL D --> F[Need Lockstep HSM ECC]
    E -- ASIL B --> G[Single Core HSM Optional]
    E -- QM --> H[Minimal Safety Features]

    F --> I{Network Protocol Needed}
    G --> I
    H --> I

    I -- CAN FD --> J[Select MCU with CAN FD MAC]
    I -- Ethernet --> K[Select MCU with 100BASE T1 MAC]
    I -- LIN --> L[Select MCU with LIN UART]

    J --> M[Check AEC Q100 Grade 0 and Temperature Range]
    K --> M
    L --> M

    M --> N[Evaluate Toolchain Compiler Debugger]
    N --> O[Verify Supply Longevity 15 Years]
    O --> P[Final MCU Selection]
```

## 4.4 Layer Diagram — In-Vehicle Network and MCU Position

```mermaid
graph TD
    cloud[Cloud OTA Server] --> tcu[Telematics TCU with MCU]
    tcu --> cen[Central Gateway ECU MCU]
    cen --> do1[Domain Controller 1 ADAS MCU]
    cen --> do2[Domain Controller 2 Powertrain MCU]
    cen --> do3[Domain Controller 3 Body MCU]

    do1 --> cam[Camera Sensor]
    do1 --> rad[Radar Sensor]
    do2 --> engu[Engine ECU MCU]
    do2 --> bmsu[Battery Management MCU]
    do3 --> bcm[Body Control Module MCU]
    do3 --> hvac[HVAC Module MCU]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
**List any three differences between a commercial-grade microcontroller and an automotive-grade microcontroller.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Temperature range:** Commercial = 0–70 °C; Automotive = **-40 to +125 °C** (AEC-Q100 Grade 0). *(1 mark)*
2. **Functional safety:** Commercial has none; Automotive supports **ISO 26262 ASIL-A to ASIL-D** with lockstep cores. *(1 mark)*
3. **Longevity & qualification:** Commercial lifecycle ≈ 3–5 years; Automotive requires **15-year supply continuity** and **AEC-Q100 stress-test qualification**. *(1 mark)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**What is the role of a CAN module inside an automotive microcontroller? Name the higher-speed protocol that is replacing CAN in modern zonal architectures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **CAN module** in an automotive MCU implements the **ISO 11898** data-link layer in hardware. It provides bit-stuffing, arbitration, CRC, ACK, acceptance filtering, and time-stamping — offloading the CPU. *(2 marks)*
- The higher-speed replacement in modern zonal E/E architectures is **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1 / 1000BASE-T1)**, supporting up to **1 Gbps** over a single twisted pair. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Q3. Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** Classify automotive microcontrollers based on **bit-width** and **architecture**. List suitable applications for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **selection criteria** for choosing an automotive microcontroller for an **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** application. Justify the choice of a 32-bit lockstep RISC MCU with HSM. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification of Automotive MCUs:**

| Class | Bit-width | Architecture | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Entry-level | 8-bit | CISC, Von Neumann (e.g., Freescale S08) | Window lifter, mirror adjuster, simple LIN slaves |
| Mid-range | 16-bit | CISC/RISC, Harvard (Infineon XC164) | HVAC blower, transmission shift logic, wiper ECU |
| High-end | 32-bit | RISC, Lockstep Harvard (AURIX, RH850, S32K3) | Engine ECU, EPS, BMS, ADAS, Domain controllers |
| Compute SoC | 64-bit / multi-core | Cortex-A + NPU (Orin, Snapdragon Ride) | Autonomous driving L3+, central compute |

*(7 marks: 1 mark/bit-width class + 1 mark/example + 1 mark/architecture + 1 mark for full classification completeness = 7)*

**(b) Selection criteria for an Engine ECU:**

1. **Performance:** Engine control needs sub-millisecond loop time (crank angle synchronized, often < 100 µs for injection calculation). Requires 32-bit RISC ≥ 200 MHz, ≥ 1.5 DMIPS/MHz. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Memory:** Complex control algorithms (torque model, knock detection, lambda control) need ≥ 4 MB Flash, ≥ 512 KB RAM. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Peripherals:** Multiple CAN-FD channels, eGTM (enhanced Generic Timer Module) for crank-synced events, high-speed 12-bit ADC, SENT for sensors. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Functional Safety:** ISO 26262 **ASIL-D** requires lockstep dual-core, ECC on memory, BIST, window watchdog, FFI (Freedom From Interference). *(1.5 marks)*
5. **Security:** HSM for secure boot, key storage, OTA encryption — prevents malicious reflashing. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Automotive grade:** AEC-Q100 Grade 0, -40 to +125 °C, 15-year supply. *(0.5 mark)*

**Justification:** A 32-bit lockstep RISC MCU (e.g., **AURIX TC377**) satisfies all the above. The 8-bit/16-bit alternatives cannot meet the compute throughput, and a non-lockstep design cannot meet ASIL-D for engine control, where a fault could cause unintended acceleration. *(included in the 7 marks total)*

---

### **Q3. Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **internal architecture of a modern 32-bit automotive MCU** highlighting the **lockstep cores, HSM, CAN-FD, ADC, and eGTM timer modules**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A CAN module of an MCU is clocked at $f_{clk} = 64\,\text{MHz}$. The prescaler is $BRP = 2$, and the segments are configured as $Sync = 1$, $Prop = 5$, $Phase1 = 6$, $Phase2 = 6$. Compute the **CAN bit rate**. If the data phase uses $BRP_{data} = 1$ with the same segment count, what is the **CAN-FD data-phase bit rate**? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Internal architecture (refer to Section 4.1 diagram).** Key points to mention: *(7 marks = 1 mark × 7 points)*
- Two lockstep Cortex-R5 cores with comparator.
- MPU for memory protection.
- System bus matrix (AHB/AXI) connecting all peripherals.
- Embedded Flash with ECC (4 MB).
- SRAM with ECC.
- DMA controller for low-CPU-overhead data movement.
- eGTM (Generic Timer Module) for crank-synchronized events.
- 12-bit ADC (1 MSPS) for analog sensor inputs.
- Multiple CAN-FD modules for vehicle network.
- LIN / UART for low-cost body network.
- 100BASE-T1 Ethernet MAC for backbone.
- HSM with AES, SHA, RSA, ECC accelerators.
- Window Watchdog for safety.

**(b) Numerical:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Total segment count} & \\
N_{bit} &= Sync + Prop + Phase1 + Phase2 \\
&= 1 + 5 + 6 + 6 \\
&= 18
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Nominal (arbitration) bit rate} & \\
\text{BR}_{arb} &= \dfrac{f_{clk}}{BRP \times N_{bit}} \\
&= \dfrac{64 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 18} \\
&= \dfrac{64\,000\,000}{36} \\
&= 1\,777\,777.78\;\text{bps} \\
&\approx \mathbf{1.778\;\text{Mbps}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: CAN-FD data-phase bit rate} & \\
\text{BR}_{data} &= \dfrac{f_{clk}}{BRP_{data} \times N_{bit}} \\
&= \dfrac{64 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 18} \\
&= 3\,555\,555.56\;\text{bps} \\
&\approx \mathbf{3.556\;\text{Mbps}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Full 7 marks: 1 mark for segment sum, 2 marks for nominal calculation, 2 marks for CAN-FD data rate, 1 mark for unit conversion, 1 mark for final answer with proper units.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add 1** to segments in PWM formula: $PERIOD+1$, $PS+1$ — common 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Mixing up $f_{clk}$ units:** A student often forgets to convert MHz → Hz before substituting.
> 3. **Wrong ASIL justification:** Many students write "ASIL-D" without stating *why* — always link ASIL to a *hazard and risk*.
> 4. **No mention of AEC-Q100:** A complete answer on automotive grade must mention this qualification standard by name.
> 5. **Confusing CPI with clock cycles per second:** A CPI is dimensionless — don't add units.
> 6. **Ignoring lockstep requirement for ASIL-D:** Citing only HSM is insufficient.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- 🔹 An **automotive MCU** operates in **-40 °C to +125 °C**, certified to **AEC-Q100**, with **15-year** lifecycle.
- 🔹 The 4 key families by bit-width: **8-bit (S08, 78K0), 16-bit (XC164, RL78), 32-bit (AURIX, S32K3, RH850, SPC58), 64-bit SoC (Orin, S32G)**.
- 🔹 Modern automotive MCUs are **RISC, 32-bit, lockstep** for **ASIL-D** capability.
- 🔹 **Von Neumann** = shared bus (lower determinism). **Harvard** = separate buses (higher determinism) — preferred for real-time automotive.
- 🔹 **Lockstep** = two cores run same code, comparator checks equality → catches single-point faults.
- 🔹 **AEC-Q100** = automotive stress-test qualification. **ISO 26262** = functional safety. **AEC-Q104** = multi-chip modules.
- 🔹 **ASIL levels** A → B → C → D (lowest → highest). Airbag, EPS, Engine = **ASIL-D**.
- 🔹 In-vehicle networks: **LIN (19.2 kbps) < CAN (1 Mbps) < CAN-FD (5 Mbps) < FlexRay (10 Mbps) < Automotive Ethernet (100 Mbps – 1 Gbps)**.
- 🔹 **HSM** = hardware security module → secure boot, OTA, V2X, key storage.
- 🔹 **Watchdog timer** is mandatory in ASIL-rated ECUs (independent window WDT).
- 🔹 **CAN bit rate** = $\dfrac{f_{clk}}{BRP \times N_{bit}}$; **PWM frequency** = $\dfrac{f_{clk}}{(PS+1)(PERIOD+1)}$.
- 🔹 **Selection criteria** = performance, memory, peripherals, power, safety (ISO 26262), security, cost, AEC-Q100, supply longevity.
- 🔹 **Engine ECU** = 32-bit AURIX/RH850, 4 MB Flash, multiple CAN-FD, lockstep, HSM, ASIL-D.
- 🔹 **Body Control** = 16/32-bit low-power, LIN/CAN, ASIL-A/B, low sleep current.
- 🔹 **ADAS** = 64-bit SoC (Orin, Mobileye), Automotive Ethernet, GPU/NPU, ASIL-B (perception layer).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 8051: Features, Architecture, Pin Configurations, Interrupts, Registers, Timers, and Applications (Description Only, No Programming)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT632 - AUTOMOTIVE EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** 8051: Features, Architecture, Pin Configurations, Interrupts, Registers, Timers, and Applications (Description Only, No Programming)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the 8051 Microcontroller

The **Intel 8051** is an **8-bit Harvard architecture single-chip microcontroller (MCU)** originally introduced by Intel in 1980. It integrates a Central Processing Unit (CPU), program memory (ROM/EPROM), data memory (RAM), four parallel I/O ports, two 16-bit timers/counters, a full-duplex serial port, and a five-source interrupt controller onto a single silicon die packaged in a **40-pin DIP** form factor.

In the context of KTU's **PEMUT632 – Automotive Embedded Systems** syllabus, the 8051 is treated as a foundational reference architecture for understanding the evolution of automotive ECUs (Engine Control Units, BCMs, IMMOs). Despite being replaced in modern vehicles by 16/32-bit MCUs (Infineon XC2000, NXP S32K, Renesas RH850), the 8051's *register-level philosophy* survives in every automotive SoC derivative.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 8051 is the canonical *8-bit reference* taught before scaling up to the 16-bit C166 and 32-bit ARM Cortex-M architectures used in modern automotive powertrain and chassis ECUs.

### 1.2 Key Salient Features (Boards-Favourite)

| Feature Block | Specification |
|---|---|
| CPU Core | 8-bit ALU, optimized for control applications |
| On-chip Program Memory (ROM) | **4 KB** (in original 8051) |
| On-chip Data Memory (RAM) | **128 Bytes** (lower 128) + **128 Bytes SFR** (upper 128) |
| I/O Lines | **32** bidirectional lines organized as **4 ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3) |
| Timers/Counters | **Two 16-bit** Timers (T0, T1) |
| Serial Port | **Full-duplex UART** |
| Interrupt Sources | **5** (Reset, 2 External, 2 Timer, 1 Serial) |
| External Memory Expansion | **64 KB code** + **64 KB data** |
| Operating Frequency | **12 MHz** typical (range 1.2–12 MHz) |
| Power | **+5 V DC** supply |
| Package | 40-pin DIP, 44-pin PLCC/QFP |

> [!NOTE]
> Derivatives such as the **8052** extend RAM to 256 Bytes and add a third timer; the **AT89C51** (Atmel) replaces ROM with **4 KB Flash**; the **8xC51FA** adds PCA (Programmable Counter Array). In *automotive* applications, **TLE7810** (Infineon) is a 8051-core device with integrated LIN transceiver and voltage regulator.

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy: "8051 as a Self-Contained City"

Imagine a modern city that must run entirely on its own resources:
- **CPU (City Hall)** – The mayor who signs all decrees (executes instructions).
- **ROM (City Charter)** – Permanent laws written in stone, never changed at runtime.
- **RAM (Working Desks)** – Whiteboards that store temporary calculations.
- **I/O Ports (City Gates)** – Four gates (P0–P3) where vehicles (data) enter and exit.
- **Timers (Town Clock Tower)** – Synchronizes events like school bells and streetlights.
- **Serial Port (Telephone Line)** – A two-way voice line to other cities (other MCUs/PC).
- **Interrupts (Emergency Sirens)** – A priority-based mechanism that interrupts the mayor during routine work for an urgent event (e.g., a fire alarm).
- **Oscillator (Heartbeat)** – The quartz crystal giving the city a steady rhythm; one *machine cycle* = 12 heartbeats.

This "self-contained city" analogy is what makes the 8051 an **embedded** controller – the CPU, memory, and peripherals are *co-located*, eliminating the bus and memory bottlenecks that plague multi-chip microprocessor systems.

## 1.4 Why 8051 Still Matters in Automotive Embedded Systems (2024 Perspective)

Although no new car since ~2010 uses a stock 8051, the architecture persists in three automotive niches:
1. **LIN (Local Interconnect Network) slave nodes** – Window lifters, mirror adjusters, seat controllers (e.g., Infineon TLE7259-3 with embedded 8051 core).
2. **Immobilizer / RKE (Remote Keyless Entry)** – Low-power 8051 cores with AES-128 accelerators.
3. **Body Control Modules (BCM) entry-level** – Where cost-per-node dominates over MIPS.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU board exams, the examiner expects you to know **why** the 8051 is taught before ARM/AVR: it isolates the *fundamentals of memory mapping, SFR access, timer modes, and interrupt vectors* without the distraction of complex pipelines and MMUs.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of the 8051 — Block-by-Block Analysis

The 8051 uses a **modified Harvard architecture**: separate address and data buses for code (ROM) and data (RAM), but the ALU and instruction decoder are shared. Below is the logical block decomposition that is **guaranteed to appear in KTU descriptive questions (14-mark sub-parts)**.

### 2.1.1 Central Processing Unit (CPU)
- **Accumulator (A)** – The implicit operand for ~70% of instructions (ADD, ANL, ORL, MOV). Every arithmetic result lands here.
- **B Register** – Secondary accumulator used only by **MUL AB** and **DIV AB**.
- **Program Status Word (PSW)** – 8-bit flag register. The four key flags are:
  - **CY (Carry)** – Borrow/overflow for unsigned arithmetic.
  - **AC (Auxiliary Carry)** – Carry between bit 3 and bit 4 (BCD correction).
  - **F0 (User Flag)** – General-purpose 1-bit scratchpad.
  - **RS1, RS0** – Register bank select bits (00B = Bank 0, 01B = Bank 1, 10B = Bank 2, 11B = Bank 3).
  - **OV (Overflow)** – Signed arithmetic overflow.
  - **P (Parity)** – Hardware-set even parity of the accumulator.

### 2.1.2 Oscillator and Clock Circuit
- An external quartz crystal is connected between **XTAL1 (Pin 19)** and **XTAL2 (Pin 18)**.
- Two ceramic capacitors (typically **30 pF**) shunt each crystal lead to ground.
- The internal oscillator generates the master clock at the crystal's resonant frequency (e.g., **11.0592 MHz** for serial communication; **12 MHz** for general timing).

### 2.1.3 Program Memory (Code ROM, 0000H–FFFFH)
- 4 KB on-chip (addresses 0000H–0FFFH), expandable to 64 KB externally.
- The **Program Counter (PC)** is **16 bits** wide, allowing addressing of the full 64 KB space.
- **Reset vector:** Address 0000H (first instruction after power-up or reset).
- **Interrupt vectors:** Six 8-byte slots starting at 0003H (described in §2.4).

### 2.1.4 Data Memory (RAM) — The Two-Region Model
The 128-byte on-chip RAM is split into three logical regions:

| Region | Address Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Register Banks** | 00H–1FH (32 Bytes) | 4 banks × 8 registers (R0–R7). Selected via PSW[RS1:RS0]. |
| **Bit-Addressable Area** | 20H–2FH (16 Bytes) | 128 individually addressable bits (00H–7FH of bit-addressable space). |
| **Scratchpad / General RAM** | 30H–7FH (80 Bytes) | User variables and stack. |
| **SFR Space (Upper 128)** | 80H–FFH | 21 Special Function Registers (P0, P1, P2, P3, TCON, TMOD, etc.) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boards Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *bit-addressable* region (20H–2FH) with *bit-banded* regions in ARM Cortex-M3. In 8051, the bit-addressable region supports special instructions like `SETB bit` and `CLR bit` (described conceptually here, not coded).

### 2.1.5 Special Function Registers (SFRs)
The SFR space (80H–FFH) contains **21 important 8-bit registers**. The top 10 KTU-favourite SFRs are:

| SFR | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **A (Accumulator)** | E0H | Arithmetic/Logic primary operand |
| **B** | F0H | Used in MUL/DIV |
| **PSW** | D0H | Program Status Word (flags) |
| **SP** | 81H | Stack Pointer (resets to 07H) |
| **DPTR (DPL/DPH)** | 82H / 83H | 16-bit Data Pointer for MOVX |
| **P0–P3** | 80H, 90H, A0H, B0H | Port latches |
| **TCON** | 88H | Timer/Counter control & ext. interrupt flags |
| **TMOD** | 89H | Timer/Counter mode select |
| **SCON** | 98H | Serial port control |
| **IE** | A8H | Interrupt Enable |
| **IP** | B8H | Interrupt Priority |
| **TH0, TL0, TH1, TL1** | 8CH, 8AH, 8DH, 8BH | Timer high/low bytes |

## 2.2 Pin Configuration (40-Pin DIP — Exam-Critical)

```
        +---v---+
  P1.0  | 1  40| Vcc (+5V)
  P1.1  | 2  39| P0.0 (AD0)
  P1.2  | 3  38| P0.1 (AD1)
  P1.3  | 4  37| P0.2 (AD2)
  P1.4  | 5  36| P0.3 (AD3)
  P1.5  | 6  35| P0.4 (AD4)
  P1.6  | 7  34| P0.5 (AD5)
  P1.7  | 8  33| P0.6 (AD6)
   RST  | 9  32| P0.7 (AD7)
  P3.0  |10  31| EA#/VPP
  P3.1  |11  30| ALE/PROG#
  P3.2  |12  29| PSEN#
  P3.3  |13  28| P2.7 (A15)
  P3.4  |14  27| P2.6 (A14)
  P3.5  |15  26| P2.5 (A13)
  P3.6  |16  25| P2.4 (A12)
  P3.7  |17  24| P2.3 (A11)
 XTAL2  |18  23| P2.2 (A10)
 XTAL1  |19  22| P2.1 (A9)
   GND  |20  21| P2.0 (A8)
        +-------+
```

### 2.2.1 Pin Function Classification (Boards Table)

| Pin(s) | Name | Type | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1–8 | P1.0–P1.7 | I/O | General-purpose Port 1 (no alternate functions) |
| 9 | RST | Input | Reset (active HIGH; must stay HIGH for **2 machine cycles** to reset) |
| 10–17 | P3.0–P3.7 | I/O | Port 3 with **alternate functions** (see below) |
| 18, 19 | XTAL2, XTAL1 | Osc | Crystal connections |
| 20 | GND | Power | Ground |
| 21–28 | P2.0–P2.7 | I/O | Port 2 (also carries high-byte address A8–A15 for external memory) |
| 29 | PSEN# | Output | Program Store Enable (active LOW; enables external code memory read) |
| 30 | ALE/PROG# | Output | Address Latch Enable (latches low-byte address for external memory) |
| 31 | EA#/VPP | Input | External Access (LOW → forces external code memory) |
| 32–39 | P0.0–P0.7 | I/O | Port 0 (also serves as multiplexed AD0–AD7) |
| 40 | Vcc | Power | +5 V |

### 2.2.2 Port 3 Alternate Functions (Often a 7-Mark Sub-Question)

| P3 Bit | Alternate Name | Alternate Function |
|---|---|---|
| P3.0 | **RXD** | Serial data input |
| P3.1 | **TXD** | Serial data output |
| P3.2 | **INT0#** | External interrupt 0 |
| P3.3 | **INT1#** | External interrupt 1 |
| P3.4 | **T0** | Timer 0 external input |
| P3.5 | **T1** | Timer 1 external input |
| P3.6 | **WR#** | External data memory write strobe |
| P3.7 | **RD#** | External data memory read strobe |

## 2.3 Reset Circuit and Operating Modes

- **Power-on reset:** Vcc rises → capacitor on RST pin charges → RST pin held HIGH for ≥ 2 machine cycles → SFRs initialize.
- **On reset:** PC ← 0000H, SP ← 07H, all ports ← FFH, ACC ← 00H, B ← 00H, PSW ← 00H, IE ← 0×00H (interrupts disabled).
- **Operating modes** (selected via SFR bits):
  - *Normal mode* – executes from internal ROM/Flash.
  - *Idle mode* – CPU stopped, peripherals running (set by PCON.0, cleared by any interrupt).
  - *Power-down mode* – oscillator stopped (set by PCON.1, exited only by hardware reset).

## 2.4 Interrupt Subsystem — Complete Theory

The 8051 supports **5 interrupt sources** + the **Reset** vector. Each source has:
1. A **flag bit** in TCON or SCON.
2. An **enable bit** in the IE register.
3. A **priority level** (high or low) in the IP register.
4. A **fixed vector address** in program memory.

### 2.4.1 Vector Table (Must Memorize)

| Source | Flag | Vector Address | Default Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reset** | — | **0000H** | Highest (non-maskable) |
| External Interrupt 0 (INT0#) | IE0 (TCON.1) | **0003H** | 1 (highest after reset) |
| Timer 0 Overflow | TF0 (TCON.5) | **000BH** | 2 |
| External Interrupt 1 (INT1#) | IE1 (TCON.3) | **0013H** | 3 |
| Timer 1 Overflow | TF1 (TCON.7) | **001BH** | 4 |
| Serial Port (TI/RI) | TI/RI (SCON.0/1) | **0023H** | 5 (lowest) |

### 2.4.2 Trigger Modes (IT0/IT1 in TCON)
- **Level-triggered (ITx = 0):** INTx# pin must be held LOW until the CPU completes the current instruction; flag is set but not latched — used when the interrupt is from a *slow* source.
- **Edge-triggered (ITx = 1):** A falling edge on INTx# sets the flag and latches it; cleared only by hardware upon service. Preferred for *automotive* applications where noise pulses must not retrigger.

### 2.4.3 Interrupt Enable Register (IE, A8H)
| Bit | 7 (EA) | 6 (—) | 5 (—) | 4 (ES) | 3 (ET1) | 2 (EX1) | 1 (ET0) | 0 (EX0) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Function | Global Enable | — | — | Serial Enable | Timer 1 Enable | Ext 1 Enable | Timer 0 Enable | Ext 0 Enable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EA = 0** disables *all* interrupts irrespective of other bits. The KTU examiner will expect you to state this *masking hierarchy*.

## 2.5 Timer/Counter Subsystem — Modes 0, 1, 2, 3

Each timer uses two 8-bit SFRs: **THx (high byte)** and **TLx (low byte)**, controlled by **TMOD** (mode) and **TCON** (control/flags).

### 2.5.1 TMOD Register (89H) Layout
| Bit | 7 (GATE) | 6 (C/T#) | 5 (M1) | 4 (M0) | 3 (GATE) | 2 (C/T#) | 1 (M1) | 0 (M0) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Applies to | Timer 1 | Timer 1 | Timer 1 | Timer 1 | Timer 0 | Timer 0 | Timer 0 | Timer 0 |

- **C/T# = 0** → Timer mode (clock from internal oscillator ÷ 12).
- **C/T# = 1** → Counter mode (clock from external pin T0/T1).
- **GATE = 1** → Timer runs only when INTx# pin is HIGH (hardware gating — used for pulse-width measurement).

### 2.5.2 Mode Comparison

| Mode | M1 M0 | Width | Behaviour | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mode 0** | 00 | 13-bit | TLx (5 bits) + THx (8 bits) | Legacy 8048 compatibility |
| **Mode 1** | 01 | **16-bit** | Full THx:TLx | **Long delays** (most common) |
| **Mode 2** | 10 | 8-bit auto-reload | TLx counts; THx is reload value | **Baud rate generation** |
| **Mode 3** | 11 | Split | T0 → two 8-bit timers; T1 → stopped | Extra timer in T1 emulation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boards Insight:** In automotive ECU applications, **Mode 2 (auto-reload)** of Timer 1 is almost always used to generate the serial baud rate because it produces an exact, jitter-free clock even at varying Vcc.

## 2.6 Serial Port (UART) Subsystem

- **SCON (98H)** configures 4 modes:
  - **Mode 0** – 8-bit shift register, fixed baud = f_osc / 12.
  - **Mode 1** – 8-bit UART, variable baud = Timer 1 overflow rate.
  - **Mode 2** – 9-bit UART, fixed baud = f_osc / 32 or /64.
  - **Mode 3** – 9-bit UART, variable baud (same as Mode 1).
- **PCON (87H)** — bit 7 (SMOD) doubles the baud rate in Modes 1, 2, 3.
- **Baud rate generation** uses **Timer 1 in Mode 2 (auto-reload)**.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Machine Cycle Period $T_{MC}$ | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | Seconds; 1 MC = 12 oscillator periods |
| Timer Increment Frequency $f_{TMR}$ | $f_{TMR} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12}$ | Hz |
| Mode 1 Delay (16-bit) | $T_{d1} = (65536 - N) \cdot T_{MC}$ | $N$ = initial value loaded in TH:TL |
| Mode 2 Delay (8-bit auto-reload) | $T_{d2} = (256 - TH) \cdot T_{MC}$ | Reload value is in TH; TL free-runs |
| Timer Reload Value (Mode 1) | $N = 65536 - \dfrac{T_{d}}{T_{MC}}$ | Round down to nearest integer |
| Baud Rate (Mode 1, 3) | $BR = \dfrac{2^{SMOD} \cdot f_{OSC}}{32 \cdot 12 \cdot (256 - TH1)}$ | SMOD ∈ {0, 1} |
| TH1 for desired baud | $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{384 \cdot BR}$ (SMOD=0) | Derivation in §3.4 |
| Mode 0 Baud | $BR_0 = f_{OSC} \;\; / \;\; 12$ | Fixed |
| Mode 2 Baud | $BR_2 = \dfrac{2^{SMOD} \cdot f_{OSC}}{64}$ | Fixed (uses PCON bit) |
| Reset pulse width | $T_{RST} \ge 2 \cdot T_{MC}$ | Two machine cycles minimum |
| Maximum external memory | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes = 64 KB | Per bank (code or data) |
| Number of bit-addressable bits | 128 (in 20H–2FH) + 64 (in SFR bit-region) = 192 | Total |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always substitute numeric values step-by-step. KTU examiners award partial marks at *each transition*, not just the final number. A partially-correct derivation with clear units scores more than a single "magic number" answer.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Machine Cycle from Oscillator Frequency

**Objective:** Show that one machine cycle of the 8051 equals 12 oscillator periods.

Let the crystal oscillator produce a square wave of frequency $f_{OSC}$ Hz. Each oscillator period is:

$$
T_{OSC} = \frac{1}{f_{OSC}}
$$

The 8051 internally divides this clock by a **fixed prescaler of 12**, generating a slower "state clock" that drives the CPU state machine. Therefore, the machine-cycle period is:

$$
T_{MC} = 12 \cdot T_{OSC}
$$

Substituting $T_{OSC} = 1 / f_{OSC}$:

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}}
$$

**Example:** For the canonical automotive-standard crystal $f_{OSC} = 11.0592$ MHz:

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.0851 \; \mu s
$$

For the generic board example $f_{OSC} = 12$ MHz:

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1.0 \; \mu s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why divide by 12?** It gives the CPU enough time to complete an instruction fetch, decode, and execute in one cycle. Most 8051 instructions take 1–4 machine cycles. This is why 11.0592 MHz is preferred for serial port work — it produces integer machine cycle periods (1.0851 µs) and *also* divides evenly into standard baud rates (9600, 19200).

## 3.2 Derivation of Timer Delay in Mode 1 (16-Bit)

**Setup:** Configure Timer 0 in Mode 1 with an initial value $N$ (combined TH0:TL0). The timer increments every machine cycle until it rolls over from FFFFH → 0000H, at which point the **TF0** flag is set.

**Step 1 — Number of timer ticks before overflow:**

$$
\text{Ticks} = 2^{16} - N = 65536 - N
$$

**Step 2 — Each tick lasts one machine cycle:**

$$
T_{tick} = T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}}
$$

**Step 3 — Total delay:**

$$
T_{d1} = \text{Ticks} \cdot T_{tick} = (65536 - N) \cdot \frac{12}{f_{OSC}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for N (timer reload value) given desired delay $T_d$:**

$$
N = 65536 - \left\lfloor \frac{T_d \cdot f_{OSC}}{12} \right\rfloor
$$

### Worked Example (Board-Favourite)

> *Generate a 50 ms delay using Timer 0 in Mode 1 with an 11.0592 MHz crystal.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{MC} &= \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.0851 \; \mu s \\
\text{Ticks} &= \frac{T_d}{T_{MC}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0851 \times 10^{-6}} = 46{,}080 \; \text{ticks} \\
N &= 65536 - 46{,}080 = 19{,}456 \\
N_{hex} &= 19{,}456_{10} = 4C00_{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore **TH0 = 4CH** and **TL0 = 00H**.

## 3.3 Derivation of Timer Delay in Mode 2 (8-Bit Auto-Reload)

In Mode 2, **TLx** counts 0 → 255 → rolls back to the value pre-stored in **THx**. The reload value $R$ is in THx, and the effective count is $2^8 - R = 256 - R$.

$$
T_{d2} = (256 - R) \cdot T_{MC} = (256 - R) \cdot \frac{12}{f_{OSC}}
$$

Solving for the reload value:

$$
R = 256 - \left\lfloor \frac{T_d \cdot f_{OSC}}{12} \right\rfloor
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Timer-1 Reload Value for Serial Baud Rate (Mode 1)

The serial port in Mode 1 overflows at **$\dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32}$** of the Timer 1 overflow rate.

**Timer 1 overflow rate** in Mode 2:

$$
f_{OV1} = \frac{1}{(256 - TH1) \cdot T_{MC}} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{12 \cdot (256 - TH1)}
$$

Therefore, the **baud rate** is:

$$
BR = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \cdot f_{OV1} = \frac{2^{SMOD} \cdot f_{OSC}}{32 \cdot 12 \cdot (256 - TH1)}
$$

Solving for **TH1** (with SMOD = 0):

$$
TH1 = 256 - \frac{f_{OSC}}{384 \cdot BR}
$$

### Worked Example: 9600 Baud @ 11.0592 MHz, SMOD = 0

$$
\begin{aligned}
TH1 &= 256 - \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times 9600} \\
&= 256 - \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{3.6864 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= 256 - 3 = 253 = FDH
\end{aligned}
$$

This is why **TH1 = FDH** is a memorized "magic number" in every 8051 textbook — and why KTU frequently sets serial-port questions on this derivation.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Timer Mode for Automotive Use

| Application Scenario | Recommended Mode | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Engine RPM sampling (every 1 ms) | Mode 1 | 16-bit range covers 1–65535 µs without reload jitter |
| CAN/LIN bus baud generator | Mode 2 | Auto-reload ensures glitch-free continuous frames |
| Pulse-width measurement (PWM input) | Mode 1 with GATE=1 | Hardware gating measures INT0# HIGH duration |
| Crank-shaft tooth counting | Counter Mode (C/T#=1) | External pulses on T0/T1 pin |
| Watchdog-equivalent timeout | Mode 2 | Independent of code path; can interrupt on overflow |

## 3.6 Interrupt Latency Derivation (Description)

The minimum latency from an external interrupt edge to the first instruction of the ISR is:

$$
T_{latency} = T_{current} + 2 \cdot T_{MC} + T_{vector}
$$

where:
- $T_{current}$ = remaining time of the currently-executing instruction (max 4 machine cycles for MUL/DIV).
- $2 \cdot T_{MC}$ = hardware call latency to push PC onto stack and jump to vector.
- $T_{vector}$ = time to execute a jump instruction at the vector address (typically 2 MC).

For a 12 MHz crystal:

$$
T_{latency}^{max} = 4 + 2 + 2 = 8 \text{ machine cycles} = 8 \; \mu s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a hard real-time automotive context (e.g., airbag deployment ECU), a maximum 8 µs latency on an 8051 is acceptable; modern ARM Cortex-M0 ECUs target < 1 µs. The 8051 therefore remains a *soft real-time* baseline.

```mermaid
    cpuBlock --> ramBus["DATA BUS 8 BIT ADDRESS"]

    ramBus --> sfr["SFR SPACE 128 BYTES"]

    userRam --> rb1["REGISTER BANK 1"]
    userRam --> rb2["REGISTER BANK 2"]
    userRam --> rb3["REGISTER BANK 3"]

    cpuBlock --> ports["P0 P1 P2 P3 32 I O LINES"]
    timersBlock --> cpuBlock
    serialBlock --> cpuBlock
    intCtrl --> cpuBlock
    sfr --> cpuBlock
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

```mermaid
    p3 --> p31["P3 1 TXD SERIAL OUTPUT"]
    p3 --> p32["P3 2 INT0 EXTERNAL INTERRUPT 0"]
    p3 --> p33["P3 3 INT1 EXTERNAL INTERRUPT 1"]
    p3 --> p34["P3 4 T0 TIMER 0 EXTERNAL CLOCK"]
    p3 --> p35["P3 5 T1 TIMER 1 EXTERNAL CLOCK"]
    p3 --> p36["P3 6 WR EXTERNAL DATA WRITE"]
    p3 --> p37["P3 7 RD EXTERNAL DATA READ"]
```

## 4.6 Reset and Power-On Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant VCC as Vcc Rail
    participant RST as RST Pin
    participant OSC as Oscillator
    participant CPU as CPU
    participant SFR as SFR Space
    VCC->>RST: Vcc rises from 0 to 5V
    RST->>RST: Capacitor charges pin HIGH
    RST->>CPU: Hold HIGH for at least 2 MC
    OSC->>CPU: Clock stabilises
    CPU->>SFR: Initialise PC=0000H, SP=07H
    CPU->>SFR: All ports=FFH, ACC=B=00H
    CPU->>SFR: Interrupts disabled, default mode
    CPU->>CPU: Begin fetch at 0000H
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any six salient features of the 8051 microcontroller.**

**Model Answer:**
The salient features of the 8051 microcontroller are:
1. **8-bit CPU** with an 8-bit ALU optimized for control applications.
2. **On-chip 4 KB program memory (ROM)** for code storage.
3. **On-chip 128 bytes data memory (RAM)** with 16 bytes of bit-addressable area.
4. **32 I/O lines** organised as four 8-bit parallel ports (P0, P1, P2, P3).
5. **Two 16-bit timers/counters** (T0 and T1) with four operating modes each.
6. **Full-duplex programmable UART** for serial communication.
7. **Five interrupt sources** with two priority levels and a fixed vector table.
8. **On-chip oscillator** requiring only an external quartz crystal between XTAL1 and XTAL2.
9. **64 KB external code** and **64 KB external data memory** expansion capability.
10. Single **+5 V** power supply in a 40-pin DIP package.

*(Any six well-described points — full 3 marks)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the function of the pins PSEN#, ALE, and EA# in the 8051.**

**Model Answer:**
- **PSEN# (Program Store Enable, Pin 29):** This is an *active-low* output signal that enables the external code memory for a read operation. The 8051 activates PSEN# twice per machine cycle when fetching an instruction byte from external ROM; it remains HIGH for on-chip ROM accesses.

- **ALE (Address Latch Enable, Pin 30):** This output signal latches the low-byte address (A0–A7) into an external latch (e.g., 74LS373) so that the same port (P0) can be reused as the multiplexed data bus (D0–D7). ALE pulses at one-sixth of the oscillator frequency, providing a steady external clock for synchronization.

- **EA# (External Access, Pin 31):** When EA# is held HIGH, the 8051 fetches code from internal ROM for the first 4 KB; when held LOW, the CPU fetches *all* code from external ROM (used for testing or for systems with no internal memory). The same pin also serves as the programming voltage input (VPP = 12 V) during EPROM burning.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture & Memory

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Draw the internal block diagram of the 8051 and explain the function of any **seven major blocks** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the on-chip data memory organization of the 8051 in detail, explaining the register banks, bit-addressable area, and SFR space. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The internal block diagram (see §4.1) consists of the following interconnected blocks:

1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit):** Contains the 8-bit **ALU**, the **Accumulator (A)**, the **B register** (used in MUL AB / DIV AB), and the **Program Status Word (PSW)** with flags CY, AC, OV, P, RS0, RS1. It performs arithmetic, logic, and data-transfer operations.

2. **Oscillator and Clock Circuit:** Accepts an external quartz crystal between XTAL1 and XTAL2. Generates the master clock and divides it by 12 to produce the machine-cycle clock, which drives all CPU operations.

3. **Program Memory (ROM):** Stores program instructions in 4 KB of on-chip ROM (addresses 0000H–0FFFH). Each instruction is fetched under the control of the 16-bit Program Counter (PC).

4. **Data Memory (RAM):** Provides 128 bytes of on-chip user RAM for variables and stack. The lower 32 bytes (00H–1FH) form four register banks; the next 16 bytes (20H–2FH) are bit-addressable.

5. **Special Function Register (SFR) Space:** A 128-byte region at 80H–FFH containing 21 special registers (P0–P3, TCON, TMOD, SCON, IE, IP, TH/TL, SP, DPTR, etc.) that control ports, timers, serial port, and interrupts.

6. **I/O Ports (P0–P3):** Four 8-bit bidirectional ports providing 32 I/O lines. Port 0 is open-drain (needs external pull-ups); Port 3 has alternate peripheral functions (RXD, TXD, INT0#, INT1#, T0, T1, WR#, RD#).

7. **Timers/Counters (T0, T1):** Two 16-bit programmable timer/counter registers (TH0:TL0, TH1:TL1) that operate in four modes for interval timing, event counting, and baud-rate generation.

8. **Serial Port (UART):** A full-duplex serial communication interface controllable in four modes (8-bit shift register, 8-bit UART, 9-bit UART, 9-bit UART with variable baud).

9. **Interrupt Controller:** Manages five maskable interrupt sources plus the reset vector, with a fixed vector table and two priority levels. It pushes the current PC onto the stack and branches to the appropriate ISR.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The on-chip **128-byte data memory** (lower RAM) is partitioned as:

- **Register Bank Region (00H–1FH, 32 Bytes):** Divided into four banks of 8 registers each (R0–R7). The active bank is selected by the **RS1:RS0** bits of the PSW register. On reset, Bank 0 is active; the Stack Pointer is initialized to 07H. Software may switch banks to conserve the physical R0–R7 namesakes (e.g., for fast context switching in interrupt-driven code).

- **Bit-Addressable Region (20H–2FH, 16 Bytes):** This 128-bit region allows individual bit access via special instructions. Each byte's 8 bits are mapped to bit addresses 00H–7FH. Useful for boolean variables and pin-level manipulation.

- **General-Purpose / Scratchpad Region (30H–7FH, 80 Bytes):** Available for user variables, arrays, and the *stack* (which grows upward from the SP). The default stack begins at 08H and may grow to 7FH before overflowing into the SFR space.

- **SFR Space (80H–FFH, 128 Bytes, only 21 implemented):** Holds 21 special registers. Each SFR has a fixed address and controls a specific peripheral. Examples: **TCON (88H)** controls timer run flags and external interrupt edges; **TMOD (89H)** configures timer modes; **SCON (98H)** controls the serial port; **IE (A8H)** enables interrupts; **IP (B8H)** sets priority.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Candidates often mistakenly count the SFR space (80H–FFH) as part of the 128-byte *user* RAM. They are **separate** address regions: 00H–7FH is user RAM, 80H–FFH is SFR. Confusing them loses 1–2 marks per board pattern.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Interrupts, Timers & Pin Configuration

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the 8051 interrupt structure in detail. List the interrupt sources, vector addresses, default priorities, and the role of the IE and IP registers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the four operating modes of the 8051 timers. Explain how Mode 2 is used to generate a standard 9600 baud rate with an 11.0592 MHz crystal. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The 8051 supports **five maskable interrupt sources** plus the **non-maskable Reset**.

| Source | Flag | Vector | Default Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reset | — | 0000H | Highest |
| External INT0# | IE0 | 0003H | 1 |
| Timer 0 overflow | TF0 | 000BH | 2 |
| External INT1# | IE1 | 0013H | 3 |
| Timer 1 overflow | TF1 | 001BH | 4 |
| Serial port (TI/RI) | TI/RI | 0023H | 5 (lowest) |

- **IE Register (Interrupt Enable, A8H):** Each interrupt has an individual enable bit (EX0, ET0, EX1, ET1, ES). Bit 7 (EA) is the **global enable** — if EA=0, all interrupts are disabled regardless of other bits. This two-level masking gives software fine-grained control.

- **IP Register (Interrupt Priority, B8H):** Assigns **high** or **low** priority to each source. A high-priority interrupt can preempt a low-priority ISR; two simultaneous high-priority interrupts follow the polling sequence above.

- **Trigger Modes:** Set by IT0/IT1 in TCON. **Edge-triggered (ITx=1)** latches a falling-edge event; **level-triggered (ITx=0)** requires the pin to be held LOW. Edge-triggered is preferred in automotive noisy environments.

- **ISR Mechanism:** On an accepted interrupt, the CPU completes the current instruction, pushes the 16-bit PC onto the stack, and vectors to the appropriate address. The ISR ends with a **RETI** instruction that pops PC and re-arms the priority logic.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The 8051 timers operate in four modes (selected by M1:M0 bits of TMOD):

- **Mode 0 (13-bit):** Legacy mode that mimics the 8048 counter. Uses TLx (5 bits) + THx (8 bits). Rarely used today.

- **Mode 1 (16-bit):** Full THx:TLx counter, ranges 0000H–FFFFH, rolls over at 65536. Each increment takes one machine cycle. Used for long one-shot delays.

- **Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload):** TLx counts 0 → 255, then automatically reloads from THx, with **TFx** set on each overflow. The reload is hardware-instantaneous, giving a jitter-free periodic interrupt — the preferred mode for **baud-rate generation**.

- **Mode 3 (Split Timer):** T0 splits into two independent 8-bit timers (TL0 and TH0); T1 stops functioning. Used when the application needs three timers but cannot use a derivative MCU.

**Generating 9600 Baud (Mode 1 UART) with $f_{OSC} = 11.0592$ MHz:**

Step 1 — Configure Timer 1 in **Mode 2** (auto-reload) with the appropriate TH1 value.

Step 2 — Apply the formula:

$$
TH1 = 256 - \frac{f_{OSC}}{384 \cdot BR}
$$

Step 3 — Substitute $f_{OSC} = 11.0592 \times 10^{6}$, BR = 9600, SMOD = 0:

$$
TH1 = 256 - \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times 9600} = 256 - 3 = 253 = FDH
$$

Step 4 — Set the serial port to **Mode 1 (8-bit UART, variable baud)**, set SM0=1, SM1=0, REN=1, and start Timer 1 by setting **TR1 = 1** in TCON.

The resulting baud rate is exactly **9600 bps** with **0% error**, which is why the 11.0592 MHz crystal is industry-standard for 8051 serial designs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. Forgetting the **SMOD** doubling in the baud-rate formula (loses 1 mark).
> 2. Writing the TH1 value in **decimal** instead of hex FD (loses 0.5–1 mark).
> 3. Confusing the **vector address** with the flag bit address (e.g., writing 000BH for IE0 instead of TF0 — loses 1 mark).
> 4. Stating the reset vector is at 0003H (it is at **0000H** — common slip).
> 5. Forgetting **EA = 0** disables *all* interrupts in the IE description.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **last-30-minutes-before-exam** checklist. Memorize it cold.

- **Architecture type:** Modified Harvard (separate code & data buses, shared CPU).
- **Data bus width:** 8-bit; **Address bus width:** 16-bit.
- **Internal ROM:** 4 KB; **Internal RAM:** 128 B + 128 B SFR.
- **I/O Ports:** 4 (P0, P1, P2, P3) = 32 lines. **P0** is open-drain, needs pull-ups.
- **Timers:** Two 16-bit (T0, T1) with 4 modes each.
- **Interrupts:** 5 maskable + Reset = 6 vectors at 0000H, 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H.
- **Reset vector:** **0000H**. SP initialises to **07H**. All ports reset to **FFH**.
- **Oscillator pins:** XTAL1 (19), XTAL2 (18). Crystal range typically **1.2–12 MHz**.
- **Machine cycle:** 1 MC = **12 oscillator periods** → 1 µs at 12 MHz, 1.0851 µs at 11.0592 MHz.
- **Special pins:** PSEN# (external code read), ALE (address latch), EA# (force external code).
- **P3 alternate functions:** RXD, TXD, INT0#, INT1#, T0, T1, WR#, RD#.
- **Register banks:** 4 banks × 8 bytes (R0–R7), selected by PSW[RS1:RS0].
- **Bit-addressable RAM:** 20H–2FH (128 bits).
- **SFR bit-addressable subset:** 80H–FFH (only 16 of 21 SFRs support bit access).
- **Timer Mode 2 reload formula:** $R = 256 - \dfrac{T_d \cdot f_{OSC}}{12}$.
- **Baud-rate TH1 formula (SMOD=0):** $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{384 \cdot BR}$.
- **11.0592 MHz + 9600 baud → TH1 = FDH** (the all-time board favourite number).
- **Power:** +5 V; **Package:** 40-pin DIP.
- **Automotive derivatives:** Infineon TLE7810, TLE7259 — 8051 core + LIN/CAN transceiver.
- **Why study 8051 today:** Foundational reference for *memory map, SFR, timer modes, and vectored interrupts* used in every subsequent automotive MCU (C166, RH850, S32K).
- **Application domains:** Body Control Modules, RKE/Immobilizer, LIN slaves, instrumentation clusters, simple sensor interfaces.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automotive Sensors and Actuators: Types and Functionality of Sensors (Position, Temperature, Lambda, Speed, Pressure, and Radar)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT632 - AUTOMOTIVE EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Automotive Sensors and Actuators: Types and Functionality of Sensors (Position, Temperature, Lambda, Speed, Pressure, and Radar)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Automotive Sensors?

An **Automotive Sensor** is a transducer that converts a physical, chemical, or dynamic operating parameter of a vehicle into an equivalent electrical signal that can be read by an Electronic Control Unit (ECU). The ECU then executes closed-loop control algorithms (e.g., fuel injection, ABS, ADAS) using the digitized output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMUT632):** A sensor in the automotive context is a device that monitors real-time vehicular parameters — mechanical (position, speed, pressure), thermal (temperature), chemical (exhaust gas composition), and electromagnetic (radar ranging) — and provides a conditioned voltage, current, frequency, or digital output to the embedded controller.

> [!NOTE]
> A sensor is to an ECU what the **five human senses** are to the brain. The brain (ECU) cannot "feel" the world (engine rpm, coolant temp) without peripheral receptors (sensors). Without sensors, there is no closed-loop control — only blind open-loop operation.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — The Human Nervous System

Imagine driving a car in total darkness wearing earplugs and gloves. You would not know:
- How fast the engine is spinning (**Speed Sensor** missing),
- Whether the coolant is boiling (**Temperature Sensor** missing),
- Where the throttle pedal is (**Position Sensor** missing),
- Whether the air-fuel ratio is correct (**Lambda Sensor** missing),
- How far the car in front is (**Radar Sensor** missing).

An automotive sensor is the engineering embodiment of human perception. Modern premium vehicles integrate **70 to 150 sensors**; an entry-level car contains at least **30 sensors**. They are categorized along three orthogonal axes:

| Axis | Categories | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Signal Type** | Analog, Digital, Frequency/PWM | Output electrical representation |
| **Power Mode** | Active vs. Passive | Active sensors need excitation; passive generate own signal |
| **Physical Domain** | Position, Thermal, Chemical, Electromagnetic | Nature of input parameter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Energy Domains of an Automotive Sensor:** Mechanical (force, pressure) → Electrical, Thermal (heat) → Electrical, Chemical (O₂, NOₓ) → Electrical, Radiant (light, radar) → Electrical, Magnetic (Hall, VR) → Electrical.

## 1.3 The Core Functional Loop

A sensor rarely interfaces directly with the ECU. The canonical conditioning chain is:

> **Physical Phenomenon → Sensing Element → Transduction → Signal Conditioning (Amplification, Filtering, Linearization) → ADC → ECU Microcontroller**

The signal conditioning step is critical because many sensors (thermistors, LVDTs, piezoresistive bridges) produce non-linear, low-amplitude outputs (often in the millivolt range) that are unsuitable for direct digital sampling without amplification and linearization.

> [!NOTE]
> A simple example: A **NTC thermistor** in the coolant circuit has a resistance of **~10 kΩ at 25 °C** that drops to **~1 kΩ at 100 °C**. The ECU cannot read resistance directly; a **voltage divider** and an **ADC** convert it to a 0–5 V digital code, which firmware then converts back to temperature using the **Steinhart-Hart equation**.

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot for KTU 2024 Module 2

| Sensor Family | Key Sub-Types | Primary Automotive Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Position** | Potentiometric, Hall-Effect, LVDT, Optical Encoder | Throttle, EGR, Crankshaft, Steering Angle |
| **Temperature** | NTC/PTC Thermistor, RTD (PT100), Thermocouple | Coolant, Intake Air, Oil, Exhaust Gas |
| **Lambda (O₂)** | Narrowband Zirconia, Wideband Pump-Cell, Titania | Closed-loop fuel trim (Sonde) |
| **Speed** | Hall-Effect, Variable Reluctance (VR), Optical | Wheel Speed (ABS/ESP), Crankshaft Position |
| **Pressure** | Piezoresistive (MEMS), Capacitive, Strain Gauge | MAP, TMAP, Oil, Fuel Rail, Brake, Tire (TPMS) |
| **Radar** | 24 GHz SRR, 77 GHz MRR/LRR, FMCW, Doppler | AEB, ACC, Blind-Spot Detection, BSD |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

This section dissects the operating physics, signal nature, and boundary conditions of every sensor category listed in KTU Module 2.

## 2.1 Position Sensors

### 2.1.1 Potentiometric Position Sensor
A linear or rotary **rheostat** whose wiper position determines the output voltage. It is a passive, ratiometric sensor that requires a regulated excitation voltage $V_{ref}$ (typically **5.0 V**).

**Principle:** Resistive element (carbon track or cermet) acts as a voltage divider. Output voltage is directly proportional to mechanical displacement or angle.

**Equation:**
$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \times \frac{R_{wiper}}{R_{total}}$$

where:
- $V_{ref}$ = supply voltage (**5.0 V** typical),
- $R_{wiper}$ = resistance from wiper to reference end,
- $R_{total}$ = full-scale resistance (**1 kΩ to 10 kΩ** typical).

> [!NOTE]
> **Application:** Throttle Position Sensor (TPS), Accelerator Pedal Position (APP), Fuel Level Sender, EGR valve position feedback. The Hall-effect throttle sensor is replacing potentiometric types in modern drive-by-wire systems due to **wear-free contactless operation**.

### 2.1.2 Hall-Effect Position Sensor
Named after physicist **Edwin Hall (1879)**. A permanent magnet and a semiconductor Hall element are separated by a moving ferrous target or flux concentrator.

**Operating Principle:** When a current-carrying conductor is placed in a perpendicular magnetic field, the charge carriers are deflected by the **Lorentz force**, producing a transverse Hall voltage.

**Equation:**
$$V_H = \frac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot e \cdot t}$$

where:
- $V_H$ = Hall voltage (**typically 0.1 V to 0.5 V**),
- $I$ = control current through the plate (**1 mA to 10 mA**),
- $B$ = magnetic flux density (**Tesla**),
- $n$ = charge carrier density of semiconductor,
- $e$ = electron charge (**1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**),
- $t$ = thickness of the Hall element.

Modern ICs integrate a **signal conditioner, voltage regulator, and Schmitt-trigger comparator** on-chip, producing a clean digital pulse train. Three-wire devices output a **PWM signal** with duty cycle proportional to position.

### 2.1.3 LVDT — Linear Variable Differential Transformer
A contactless, absolute position transducer with **infinite resolution** and **inherent linearity** (<0.1 %).

**Construction:** One primary winding, two secondary windings (S₁ and S₂) wound in series-opposition on a cylindrical former. A soft-iron core slides axially inside the former. The primary is excited by a sinusoidal carrier (**1 kHz to 10 kHz**, amplitude **3 V to 24 V rms**).

**Operating Equation:**
$$V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2} = k \cdot x$$

where:
- $k$ = LVDT sensitivity (**V/mm**), typically **0.1 to 1 V/mm**,
- $x$ = core displacement from null (electrical center).

The phase of $V_{out}$ (relative to primary) indicates direction of displacement. The amplitude indicates magnitude. At null position ($x = 0$), $V_{out} = 0$ — a critical calibration check used in KTU exam problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive Use:** Earlier generations of EGR valve position, suspension ride-height sensors, and clutch pedal travel sensors. Largely replaced by Hall-effect ICs in cost-sensitive applications but retained in **precision ride-height modules** of luxury vehicles.

### 2.1.4 Optical Encoder Position Sensor
Uses a **coded disc** (transparent/absoluted or slotted/incremental) and a **phototransistor / photodiode** pair. As the disc rotates, light pulses are generated. The ECU counts pulses (incremental) or reads a unique Gray code (absolute).

**Resolution Equation:**
$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{N}$$

where $N$ = number of slots or lines on the disc (e.g., 3600 lines → **0.1°** resolution).

> [!NOTE]
> **Application:** Steering angle sensor (SAS) in EPS, electric power steering, throttle-by-wire. Modern steering angle encoders are **multi-turn absolute** (e.g., 12-bit, 4096 positions per turn, 4+ turns).

## 2.2 Temperature Sensors

### 2.2.1 NTC Thermistor
A **Negative Temperature Coefficient** thermistor is a semiconductor ceramic (typically **manganese, nickel, cobalt oxides**) whose resistance **decreases** exponentially with temperature. This is the **most commonly used automotive temperature sensor**.

**Steinhart-Hart Equation (High-Order Model):**
$$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \cdot \ln(R) + C \cdot (\ln(R))^3$$

where:
- $T$ = absolute temperature (**Kelvin**),
- $R$ = thermistor resistance at temperature $T$ (**Ohms**),
- $A, B, C$ = calibrated coefficients (vendor-supplied).

**Simplified Beta Equation (Two-Point Calibration, KTU-Standard):**
$$R(T) = R_0 \cdot \exp\left[\beta \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

where:
- $R(T)$ = resistance at temperature $T$ (Kelvin),
- $R_0$ = resistance at reference $T_0$ (typically **25 °C = 298.15 K**),
- $\beta$ = material constant (**3000 K to 5000 K** for automotive NTCs).

**Voltage Divider (in-circuit):**
$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{th}(T)}{R_{th}(T) + R_{pullup}}$$

where $R_{pullup}$ is typically **2.2 kΩ to 4.7 kΩ** matched to mid-range resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Applications:** Engine Coolant Temperature (ECT), Intake Air Temperature (IAT), Transmission Fluid Temperature, Battery Temperature (BMS), HVAC cabin sensor. Highly non-linear — firmware must apply lookup table or Steinhart-Hart inversion.

### 2.2.2 PTC Thermistor
A **Positive Temperature Coefficient** thermistor exhibits a sharp resistance increase above a **Curie temperature** ($T_C$). Used primarily as a **self-resetting overcurrent protection** in electric window motors and as a **cold-start enrichment** sensor on some diesels.

### 2.2.3 RTD — Resistance Temperature Detector
Made of pure platinum (PT) or nickel. **PT100** ($R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at 0 °C) and **PT1000** ($R_0 = 1000\ \Omega$ at 0 °C) are industrial standards. The **Callendar-Van Dusen equation** describes PT100:

$$R(T) = R_0 \cdot \left[1 + A \cdot T + B \cdot T^2 + C \cdot (T - 100) \cdot T^3\right]$$

where $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\ °C^{-1}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\ °C^{-2}$, $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}\ °C^{-4}$. For $T \geq 0\ °C$, the $C$ term vanishes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Application:** High-accuracy exhaust gas temperature (EGT) sensing in turbocharged engines and SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction) systems of diesel engines, where temperatures can reach **900 °C**.

### 2.2.4 Thermocouple
Two dissimilar metals joined at a "hot junction," generating a **Seebeck voltage** proportional to the temperature difference between hot and cold junctions. Standard automotive types: **Type K** (Chromel-Alumel, range −200 °C to 1372 °C, sensitivity **~41 µV/°C**) and **Type J**.

**Equation:**
$$V = S \cdot \Delta T$$

where $S$ = Seebeck coefficient (**µV/°C**). Cold-junction compensation is mandatory (typically via an NTC in the ECU harness connector).

### 2.2.5 Semiconductor (IC) Temperature Sensor
Bipolar transistor-based sensor with a bandgap reference (e.g., **LM35**, **TMP36**). Linear output: **10 mV/°C** (LM35). Used for **non-critical** cabin HVAC and battery pack monitoring where simplicity outweighs accuracy.

## 2.3 Lambda (Oxygen) Sensor

The **lambda sensor** measures the residual oxygen in the exhaust gas to determine the **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** relative to the stoichiometric value.

### 2.3.1 Definition of Lambda
$$\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoichiometric}}$$

For gasoline, the stoichiometric A/F ratio is **14.7 : 1 by mass**. Therefore:
- $\lambda = 1.00$ → stoichiometric,
- $\lambda > 1.00$ → lean (excess air),
- $\lambda < 1.00$ → rich (excess fuel).

### 2.3.2 Narrowband Zirconia (ZrO₂) Sensor
A **thimble** of zirconium dioxide ceramic (stabilized with yttria Y₂O₃), coated inside and outside with porous platinum electrodes. The ceramic becomes an **oxygen-ion conductor** above **350 °C**. The differential oxygen partial pressure between exhaust gas (outer) and ambient air (inner reference) generates a Nernst voltage:

$$E = \frac{R \cdot T}{4 \cdot F} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{P_{O_2,\ exhaust}}{P_{O_2,\ reference}}\right)$$

where:
- $R$ = universal gas constant (**8.314 J/(mol·K)**),
- $T$ = absolute temperature,
- $F$ = Faraday constant (**96,485 C/mol**),
- $P_{O_2}$ = partial pressure of oxygen.

**Behavior:** Output voltage is **~0.1 V (lean)** or **~0.9 V (rich)**, with a near-vertical step at $\lambda = 1.0$. Acts as a **binary switch** — ECU drives the closed-loop integrator across $\lambda = 1.0$ with a target frequency of **~1 Hz** in the active catalytic converter control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Application:** The classical "O₂ sensor" — pre-catalyst and post-catalyst (one before, one after the Three-Way Catalytic Converter, TWC). TWC efficiency reaches >95 % only when $\lambda$ is held within **±1 %** of unity.

### 2.3.3 Wideband Lambda Sensor (5-Wire)
Used in **gasoline direct injection (GDI)** and **diesel** applications where continuous $\lambda$ tracking over a wide range is required. Contains a **pump cell** and a **Nernst cell**. A control loop forces the Nernst cell voltage to a reference (**450 mV**), and the current required by the pump cell is the measurement variable — it is proportional to $\lambda$ over the range **0.65 to ∞** (i.e., from very rich to pure air).

### 2.3.4 Titania (TiO₂) Sensor
A resistive sensor; the resistance of titania changes with oxygen exposure. Acts as a lean/rich switch but is less common than the zirconia type.

## 2.4 Speed Sensors

### 2.4.1 Variable Reluctance (VR) Speed Sensor
A **passive** sensor consisting of a permanent magnet wrapped with a coil. A ferrous target wheel (with teeth, usually **60-2, 36-1, or 24-1** in pattern) rotates in front of the pole piece. As tooth approaches, magnetic flux increases (low reluctance); as tooth leaves, flux decreases. The **rate of change of flux** induces a voltage in the coil:

$$V(t) = -N \cdot \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

where:
- $N$ = number of coil turns (typically **1000 to 5000**),
- $\Phi$ = magnetic flux through the coil.

**Output:** A bipolar sinusoidal waveform. The ECU's input circuit contains a **zero-crossing detector** that converts the sine to a digital square wave. Amplitude is **speed-dependent** — at low speeds (engine cranking) the signal can be as low as **50 mV**; at high speeds it can exceed **100 V**. Automatic gain control (AGC) in the ECU handles this dynamic range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Application:** Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP), Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP) on legacy vehicles, transmission input/output speed.

### 2.4.2 Hall-Effect Speed Sensor
The dominant modern technology. A **tooth/pole wheel** modulates the magnetic field across a Hall IC. The output is a **clean, fixed-amplitude square wave** (0 V to 5 V or 0 V to 12 V) regardless of wheel speed. Three-wire variants have a regulated supply; two-wire variants use a current interface (e.g., **7 mA low / 14 mA high**).

### 2.4.3 Optical Speed Sensor
Used in high-precision applications. A slotted disc interrupts an LED-phototransistor pair. **Subject to contamination** and therefore rare in under-hood automotive use.

## 2.5 Pressure Sensors

### 2.5.1 Piezoresistive (MEMS) Pressure Sensor
The **workhorse** of modern automotive pressure measurement. A silicon diaphragm with four piezoresistors arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge** is micromachined on a single die. Pressure deflects the diaphragm, unbalancing the bridge and producing a differential output voltage.

**Wheatstone Bridge Output Equation:**
$$V_{out} = V_{exc} \cdot \left(\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}\right)$$

For a fully active bridge (all four resistors change with strain), the simplified sensitivity is:

$$V_{out} \approx V_{exc} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$$

where:
- $V_{exc}$ = bridge excitation voltage (**5.0 V** typical),
- $G$ = gauge factor of the piezoresistor (**100 to 200** for silicon),
- $\varepsilon$ = mechanical strain.

For differential output (commonly used):
$$\Delta V_{out} = V_{exc} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive Use Cases:** Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP), Turbo Boost (TMAP combines temp and pressure), Fuel Rail Pressure (high-pressure common rail diesels operate at **>2000 bar** — these use a different thick-film strain technology), Oil Pressure, Brake Line Pressure (for ESP/ABS), Tire Pressure Monitoring (TPMS, **±0.05 bar** accuracy required), and the **particle filter differential pressure**.

### 2.5.2 Capacitive Pressure Sensor
Pressure changes the gap between two capacitor plates, changing capacitance:
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{d}$$

where $d$ is the plate separation that changes with pressure. Lower power, higher temperature stability than piezoresistive, but requires a dedicated ASIC for capacitance-to-voltage conversion.

### 2.5.3 Strain-Gauge Pressure Sensor
A metal diaphragm with bonded foil strain gauges. Used for **high-pressure** applications (common rail diesels).

## 2.6 Radar Sensors

### 2.6.1 Operating Principle — FMCW
Modern automotive radar uses **Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW)**. The transmitted signal is a chirp whose frequency ramps linearly from $f_0$ to $f_0 + \Delta f$ over a sweep time $T_{sw}$.

**Range Measurement Equation:**
$$R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta f}{2 \cdot \left(\frac{\Delta f_{chirp}}{T_{sw}}\right) \cdot 2} = \frac{c \cdot T_{sw} \cdot \Delta f}{4 \cdot \Delta f_{chirp}}$$

**Simpler Form:**
$$R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta f}{4 \cdot \dot{f}}$$

where:
- $c$ = speed of light (**2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**),
- $\Delta f$ = measured beat frequency (**Hz**),
- $\dot{f}$ = chirp slope (**Hz/s**).

**Velocity Measurement (Doppler):**
$$v = \frac{\Delta f_{Doppler} \cdot c}{2 \cdot f_0}$$

where $\Delta f_{Doppler}$ is the Doppler frequency shift between transmitted and received chirps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive Radar Frequency Bands:** **24 GHz** (Short Range Radar, SRR — now being phased out for UWB restrictions), **77 GHz** (Mid-Range and Long-Range Radar, MRR/LRR, the modern standard since 2020s). MMIC chipsets from **NXP, Infineon (RASIC), Texas Instruments, and Arbe** integrate the RF front-end with embedded processors.

### 2.6.2 Applications
- **ACC** — Adaptive Cruise Control (77 GHz, range up to **250 m**).
- **AEB** — Autonomous Emergency Braking.
- **BSD** — Blind-Spot Detection (24 GHz / 77 GHz corner radar).
- **FCW** — Forward Collision Warning.
- **LCA** — Lane Change Assist.
- **RCTA** — Rear Cross-Traffic Alert.
- **Parking Assist** — Ultrasonic complements radar at low speeds.

### 2.6.3 MIMO Radar
Modern automotive radars use **Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO)** antenna arrays. With $M$ transmit and $N$ receive antennas, the virtual array has $M \times N$ elements, dramatically improving angular resolution.

**Angular Resolution:**
$$\theta_{res} \approx \frac{\lambda}{N \cdot d}$$

where $\lambda$ = wavelength (4 mm at 77 GHz), $d$ = element spacing (typically $\lambda/2$), $N$ = number of virtual elements.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear directly in KTU examination numericals and derivations.**

| # | Sensor / Concept | Governing Equation | Key Parameters / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Potentiometric position | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot R_w / R_{tot}$ | $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V, $R_{tot} = 1{-}10$ kΩ |
| 2 | Hall-effect voltage | $V_H = I B / (n e t)$ | $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C |
| 3 | LVDT output | $V_{out} = k \cdot x$ | $k$ in V/mm; null at $x = 0$ |
| 4 | Optical encoder resolution | $\theta_{res} = 360°/N$ | $N$ = lines/slots on disc |
| 5 | NTC thermistor (β-model) | $R(T) = R_0 \cdot \exp[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)]$ | $T$ in K, $\beta = 3000{-}5000$ K |
| 6 | PT100 RTD (linearized) | $R_T \approx R_0(1 + A T + B T^2)$ | $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\ °C^{-1}$ |
| 7 | Thermocouple Seebeck | $V = S \cdot \Delta T$ | $S_{Type\,K} \approx 41$ µV/°C |
| 8 | Lambda ratio | $\lambda = (A/F)_{actual}/(A/F)_{stoich}$ | Stoich A/F = 14.7 : 1 (gasoline) |
| 9 | Nernst (zirconia) | $E = (RT/4F) \cdot \ln(P_{O_2,ex}/P_{O_2,ref})$ | $F = 96485$ C/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) |
| 10 | VR sensor induced EMF | $V(t) = -N \cdot d\Phi/dt$ | $N = 1000{-}5000$ turns |
| 11 | Wheatstone bridge | $V_{out} = V_{exc} \cdot (R_1/(R_1+R_2) - R_3/(R_3+R_4))$ | $V_{exc} = 5.0$ V, $G = 100{-}200$ |
| 12 | Capacitive pressure | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m |
| 13 | Radar range (FMCW) | $R = c \cdot T_{sw} \cdot \Delta f / (4 \Delta f_{chirp})$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 14 | Radar velocity (Doppler) | $v = \Delta f_{Dop} \cdot c / (2 f_0)$ | 77 GHz: $\lambda \approx 3.9$ mm |
| 15 | Angular resolution | $\theta_{res} \approx \lambda / (N d)$ | $d \approx \lambda/2$ for MIMO |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These sensors are not academic curiosities — they form the **perception layer** of every modern ECU and ADAS domain controller. In 2024 KTU viva voce, expect a question like *"Explain how a TPMS sensor measures tire pressure without any wired connection."* The answer lies in the **MEMS piezoresistive bridge** plus a **wireless UHF transmitter (315 or 433 MHz)** powered by a long-life lithium coin cell, with the pressure data encoded as a **Manchester-coded OOK or FSK** signal received by the BCM antenna.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

This section performs the **detailed mathematical derivations** most commonly asked in KTU Part-B (14-mark) questions. No step is omitted.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — NTC Thermistor Temperature from ADC Reading

**Problem Setup:** A coolant temperature sensor uses an NTC thermistor with $R_0 = 10\ \text{kΩ}$ at $T_0 = 25\ °C = 298.15\ \text{K}$, and $\beta = 3950\ \text{K}$. It is connected in a voltage divider with $R_p = 4.7\ \text{kΩ}$ to a 5.0 V reference. The output feeds a 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref,ADC} = 5.0\ \text{V}$. The ADC reading is **N = 614**. Compute the measured temperature.

### Step 1: Convert ADC code to output voltage
The 10-bit ADC produces codes from **0 to 1023**, with code **1023** corresponding to **5.0 V**.

$$V_{out} = \frac{N}{1023} \cdot V_{ref,ADC} = \frac{614}{1023} \cdot 5.0$$

$$V_{out} = 0.6002 \cdot 5.0 = 3.001\ \text{V}$$

### Step 2: Write the voltage divider equation
With $V_{ref} = 5.0\ \text{V}$ excitation and $R_p = 4.7\ \text{kΩ}$ pull-up, the output voltage across the thermistor is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{th}}{R_{th} + R_p}$$

### Step 3: Solve for thermistor resistance $R_{th}$

Rearrange the equation by isolating $R_{th}$:

$$V_{out} \cdot (R_{th} + R_p) = V_{ref} \cdot R_{th}$$

$$V_{out} \cdot R_{th} + V_{out} \cdot R_p = V_{ref} \cdot R_{th}$$

$$V_{ref} \cdot R_{th} - V_{out} \cdot R_{th} = V_{out} \cdot R_p$$

$$R_{th} \cdot (V_{ref} - V_{out}) = V_{out} \cdot R_p$$

$$R_{th} = \frac{V_{out} \cdot R_p}{V_{ref} - V_{out}}$$

### Step 4: Substitute numerical values

$$R_{th} = \frac{3.001 \cdot 4700}{5.0 - 3.001} = \frac{14104.7}{1.999}$$

$$R_{th} = 7055.4\ \Omega \approx 7.06\ \text{kΩ}$$

### Step 5: Apply the NTC β-equation to find temperature

$$R_{th} = R_0 \cdot \exp\left[\beta \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

Divide both sides by $R_0$:

$$\frac{R_{th}}{R_0} = \exp\left[\beta \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

Take the natural logarithm:

$$\ln\left(\frac{R_{th}}{R_0}\right) = \beta \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)$$

Solve for $1/T$:

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{\beta} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{R_{th}}{R_0}\right)$$

### Step 6: Compute numerically

$$\frac{R_{th}}{R_0} = \frac{7055.4}{10000} = 0.70554$$

$$\ln(0.70554) = -0.3489$$

$$\frac{1}{\beta} = \frac{1}{3950} = 2.532 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{298.15} = 3.354 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{T} = 3.354 \times 10^{-3} + (2.532 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (-0.3489)$$

$$\frac{1}{T} = 3.354 \times 10^{-3} - 8.835 \times 10^{-5} = 3.266 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$T = \frac{1}{3.266 \times 10^{-3}} = 306.2\ \text{K}$$

### Step 7: Convert to Celsius

$$T\ (°C) = 306.2 - 273.15 = 33.0\ °C$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The coolant temperature is **33.0 °C** — a perfectly reasonable engine-off or warm-up reading. This derivation pattern is a **KTU favourite** for 7-mark sub-parts in 14-mark Module-2 questions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lambda Sensor Nernst Voltage

**Problem:** A narrowband zirconia lambda sensor operates at exhaust gas temperature $T = 700\ °C = 973.15\ \text{K}$. The exhaust gas contains $P_{O_2,ex} = 0.001\ \text{atm}$ (rich burn) and ambient air reference is $P_{O_2,ref} = 0.2095\ \text{atm}$. Calculate the Nernst voltage $E$.

### Step 1: Recall the Nernst equation for O₂ cell

$$E = \frac{R \cdot T}{4 \cdot F} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{P_{O_2,ex}}{P_{O_2,ref}}\right)$$

The factor **4** arises because each O₂ molecule accepts **4 electrons** during the reduction half-reaction:
$$\text{O}_2 + 4e^- \rightarrow 2\text{O}^{2-}$$

### Step 2: Substitute constants

$$R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)},\quad F = 96485\ \text{C/mol},\quad T = 973.15\ \text{K}$$

### Step 3: Compute the prefactor

$$\frac{R \cdot T}{4 \cdot F} = \frac{8.314 \cdot 973.15}{4 \cdot 96485}$$

Numerator:
$$8.314 \cdot 973.15 = 8091.7\ \text{J/mol}$$

Denominator:
$$4 \cdot 96485 = 385940\ \text{C/mol}$$

Prefactor:
$$\frac{8091.7}{385940} = 0.02096\ \text{V}$$

### Step 4: Compute the log term

$$\frac{P_{O_2,ex}}{P_{O_2,ref}} = \frac{0.001}{0.2095} = 4.773 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\ln(4.773 \times 10^{-3}) = \ln(0.004773) = -5.345$$

### Step 5: Multiply

$$E = 0.02096 \cdot (-5.345) = -0.1120\ \text{V}$$

### Step 6: Interpret the sign

A **negative** Nernst voltage corresponds to the **rich** condition (low exhaust O₂), matching the textbook "rich → ~0.1 V low-side" behavior. The absolute magnitude (≈ **0.11 V**) aligns with the typical narrowband output.

> [!NOTE]
> At $\lambda = 1.0$ (stoichiometric), the theoretical $E \approx 0\ \text{V}$ — this is the inflection point of the step response around which the ECU switches between rich and lean integrator action.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Radar Range and Velocity from FMCW Beat Frequencies

**Problem:** A 77 GHz automotive FMCW radar chirp sweeps from 76 GHz to 78 GHz over $T_{sw} = 40\ \text{ms}$ (a slow-chirp mode used for long-range detection). A target at range $R$ returns a beat frequency of $\Delta f_b = 250\ \text{kHz}$. Find (a) range, (b) the velocity if the Doppler frequency shift is $\Delta f_D = 1.5\ \text{kHz}$.

### Part (a) — Range

**Step 1:** Compute the chirp slope.
$$\dot{f} = \frac{\Delta f_{chirp}}{T_{sw}} = \frac{2 \times 10^9}{40 \times 10^{-3}} = 5 \times 10^{10}\ \text{Hz/s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the range equation.
$$R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta f_b}{2 \cdot \dot{f}}$$

The factor **2** (not 4) accounts for the two-way propagation (out to target and back) within the chirp time.

**Step 3:** Substitute.
$$R = \frac{(3 \times 10^8) \cdot (250 \times 10^3)}{2 \cdot (5 \times 10^{10})}$$

$$R = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{13}}{10^{11}} = 750\ \text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **750 m** range is far beyond the practical ACC range of **~250 m**, indicating the calculation is correct mathematically but the long sweep is impractical. Real automotive radars use $T_{sw}$ in the **tens of microseconds** range, giving practical ranges of 200–300 m.

### Part (b) — Velocity

**Step 1:** Apply the Doppler equation.
$$v = \frac{\Delta f_D \cdot c}{2 \cdot f_0}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.
$$v = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^3) \cdot (3 \times 10^8)}{2 \cdot (77 \times 10^9)}$$

$$v = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{11}}{1.54 \times 10^{11}} = 2.92\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to km/h.
$$v = 2.92 \cdot 3.6 = 10.5\ \text{km/h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a slowly moving target — typical of a pedestrian or a vehicle in stop-and-go traffic. The same 77 GHz radar can resolve velocities up to **~250 km/h**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Piezoresistive Bridge Sensitivity

**Problem:** A MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor uses a fully active Wheatstone bridge with all four resistors of $R = 1\ \text{kΩ}$ at zero strain. Each resistor changes by $\Delta R$ when strain is applied. The excitation is $V_{exc} = 5\ \text{V}$. The gauge factor is $G = 120$. If the diaphragm strain under maximum pressure is $\varepsilon = 0.001$, find the differential output voltage.

### Step 1: Compute the fractional resistance change
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon = 120 \cdot 0.001 = 0.12$$

So $\Delta R = 0.12 \cdot 1000 = 120\ \Omega$. The four resistors become approximately:
- $R_1 = R + \Delta R = 1120\ \Omega$ (rises),
- $R_2 = R - \Delta R = 880\ \Omega$ (falls),
- $R_3 = R + \Delta R = 1120\ \Omega$,
- $R_4 = R - \Delta R = 880\ \Omega$.

### Step 2: Compute each leg voltage
$$V_a = V_{exc} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = 5 \cdot \frac{1120}{1120 + 880} = 5 \cdot \frac{1120}{2000} = 2.8\ \text{V}$$

$$V_b = V_{exc} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} = 5 \cdot \frac{1120}{1120 + 880} = 2.8\ \text{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Hmm — for differential output in a fully active bridge, adjacent arms must have **opposite** strain. The correct bridge layout is: $R_1, R_4$ are tensile (+ΔR), $R_2, R_3$ are compressive (−ΔR). Let us re-evaluate.

### Step 3: Correct bridge layout
- $R_1 = R + \Delta R = 1120\ \Omega$ (tensile),
- $R_2 = R - \Delta R = 880\ \Omega$ (compressive),
- $R_3 = R - \Delta R = 880\ \Omega$ (compressive),
- $R_4 = R + \Delta R = 1120\ \Omega$ (tensile).

$$V_a = V_{exc} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = 5 \cdot \frac{1120}{2000} = 2.8\ \text{V}$$

$$V_b = V_{exc} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} = 5 \cdot \frac{880}{2000} = 2.2\ \text{V}$$

### Step 4: Compute differential output
$$V_{out} = V_a - V_b = 2.8 - 2.2 = 0.6\ \text{V}$$

### Step 5: Cross-check with simplified formula
$$V_{out} \approx V_{exc} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = 5 \cdot 0.12 = 0.6\ \text{V}\ \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> The differential output of **600 mV** is far too large to feed directly to an ECU ADC. A real-world pressure sensor ASIC applies this across a precision instrumentation amplifier (INA) with a gain of **~10** to produce a 0–5 V output, and adds a temperature-compensation stage. This shows why **signal conditioning** is integral to sensor design.

---

```mermaid
    subgraph sensorDomain [Sensing Domain - All KTU Module 2 Families]

    end

    analogbus --> pos
    analogbus --> temp
    analogbus --> lam
    digitalbus --> spd
    analogbus --> prs
    pwr --> rad

    pos --> sigcond1[Signal Conditioning Op-Amp and Filter]

```
