# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Define an actuator. Distinguish clearly between a linear actuator and a rotary actuator, giving one automotive example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

An actuator is a device that converts a control signal — typically electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal energy — into a mechanical output (linear displacement or rotary motion). It is the *final execution element* of an automotive embedded control system.

**Linear vs Rotary Actuator:**

| Aspect | Linear Actuator | Rotary Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| Motion type | Translates along a straight axis | Rotates about a fixed axis |
| Output variable | Displacement $x$ (m), velocity $v$ (m/s) | Angular displacement $\theta$ (rad), angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s) |
| Working principle | Force on piston / magnetic pull / screw translation | Torque from motor / hydraulic displacement |
| Automotive example | Fuel injector solenoid pushing the needle | Throttle body BLDC motor rotating the butterfly valve |

**Example – Linear:** The **solenoid in a fuel injector** translates the needle linearly to open the nozzle.

**Example – Rotary:** The **BLDC motor in Electric Power Steering (EPS)** rotates the steering rack pinion to assist the driver.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a single-acting hydraulic cylinder. State the formula relating piston force to pressure.**

**Model Answer:**

A single-acting hydraulic cylinder has fluid admitted only on one side (the cap end). The other side is open to atmosphere or vented to tank, and a return spring retracts the piston when fluid pressure is removed.

**Working Principle:**
1. Pressurized hydraulic fluid is admitted through port A at pressure $P$.
2. The pressure acts on the piston face of area $A_p$, generating force $F = P \cdot A_p$.
3. The piston rod extends, doing useful work on the external load.
4. When the inlet valve closes, the spring (or load weight) retracts the rod and expels the fluid back to tank.

**Formula:** $F = P \cdot A_p$ where $F$ is in Newtons, $P$ in Pascals, $A_p$ in m².

**Sketch (ASCII representation for written exam):**

```
        Return Spring
   ==============================
   |  |  |    ||    |  |  |     |
   |  |  |    ||    |  |  |  ---|---  Port A (fluid in)
   ============================ |
       |        ||       |      |
       |        ||       |      V
       |=================|   Tank
              Piston        (spring side)
              Rod  ========>
```

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — with KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**

**With the help of a block diagram, explain the principle of operation of a Brushless DC (BLDC) motor used as a rotary actuator in automotive applications. Derive the relationship between torque and motor constants.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Brushless DC (BLDC) motor** consists of a **rotor carrying permanent magnets** (typically NdFeB) and a **stator with three-phase windings**. Commutation is performed *electronically* (not mechanically via brushes) by an external inverter controlled by the ECU.

**Operating Principle:**
1. The rotor position is sensed using **Hall-effect sensors** or a **resolver**.
2. The ECU runs a commutation algorithm — for a 3-phase motor, each electrical cycle has **6 commutation steps** (one every 60° electrical).
3. Based on rotor position, the inverter switches two of the three phases ON (one high-side MOSFET, one low-side MOSFET), creating a magnetic field in the stator that pulls the rotor to align with it.
4. Sequential switching produces continuous rotation.

**Derivation of Torque-Current Relationship:**

The force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is given by Lorentz force:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L$$

where $B$ is the flux density, $I$ is the conductor current, and $L$ is the active conductor length.

For $N$ conductors at average radius $r$ from the axis, the torque is:

$$\tau = F \cdot r = B \cdot I \cdot L \cdot r \cdot N = k_t \cdot I$$

where $k_t = B \cdot L \cdot r \cdot N$ is the **torque constant** (in N·m/A).

**The fundamental motor constant relationship** is:

$$k_t = k_e \text{ (in SI units)}$$

where $k_e$ is the back-EMF constant (in V/(rad/s)). This is a consequence of energy conservation — every Joule of mechanical work corresponds to a Joule of electrical work.

**Automotive Use:** EPS (Electric Power Steering), throttle-by-wire, HVAC blower (variable speed), seat adjustment, and increasingly as traction motors in EVs.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter of 100 mm and a rod diameter of 50 mm. It is supplied by a pump delivering 20 L/min at a pressure of 140 bar. Calculate: (i) extending force, (ii) retracting force, (iii) extending speed, (iv) retracting speed. Neglect friction.**

**Given:**
- Piston diameter $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m}$
- Rod diameter $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050 \text{ m}$
- Flow rate $Q = 20 \text{ L/min} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Pressure $P = 140 \text{ bar} = 1.40 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$

**Solution:**

**(i) Extending Force:**
Full piston face is pressurized:
$$A_{ext} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$F_{ext} = P \cdot A_{ext} = 1.40 \times 10^7 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 1.0996 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 109.96 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Retracting Force:**
Effective area = full piston area minus rod cross-section:
$$A_{ret} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.100^2 - 0.050^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.01000 - 0.00250) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.00750)$$
$$A_{ret} = 5.890 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$F_{ret} = P \cdot A_{ret} = 1.40 \times 10^7 \times 5.890 \times 10^{-3} = 8.247 \times 10^4 \text{ N} \approx 82.47 \text{ kN}$$

**(iii) Extending Speed:**
$$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_{ext}} = \frac{3.333 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.244 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/s} \approx 42.4 \text{ mm/s}$$

**(iv) Retracting Speed:**
$$v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_{ret}} = \frac{3.333 \times 10^{-4}}{5.890 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.659 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/s} \approx 56.6 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $F_{ext}$ | **109.96 kN** |
| $F_{ret}$ | **82.47 kN** |
| $v_{ext}$ | **42.4 mm/s** |
| $v_{ret}$ | **56.6 mm/s** |

**Note:** Retracting speed is *higher* than extending speed because the same flow enters a smaller annular area on retraction. This is a *fundamental property* of double-acting cylinders with single-ended rods — and a very common exam observation.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** (Alternative Choice)

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Explain the working of a piezoelectric stack actuator used as a linear actuator in automotive diesel common-rail injectors. Why is it preferred over a solenoid in modern systems?**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
A piezoelectric stack actuator consists of multiple thin discs (typically **lead zirconate titanate — PZT**) sandwiched between electrodes. The discs are mechanically stacked in series so that their individual displacements add up. Each disc expands by typically 0.1 µm per 100 V applied.

**Working Principle:**
- The PZT ceramic exhibits the **inverse piezoelectric effect**: when an electric field is applied across the crystal, the lattice deforms — producing mechanical strain.
- The displacement is given by: $x = d_{33} \cdot n \cdot V$, where $d_{33}$ is the piezoelectric strain coefficient (typically $3 \times 10^{-10}$ m/V for PZT), $n$ is the number of discs (often 200–400), and $V$ is the applied voltage.
- The stack is hydraulically coupled to the injector needle via a **servo-fluid** (often diesel fuel itself).
- When the ECU energizes the stack, it expands rapidly — the hydraulic coupler transmits this motion to the injector needle, which lifts and begins injection.
- When de-energized, the needle closes by spring force.

**Advantages over Solenoid Actuators:**

| Aspect | Piezo Injector | Solenoid Injector |
|---|---|---|
| Switching time | **< 100 µs** | ~300 µs |
| Number of injections/cycle | Up to **9 pilot + main** | Typically 2–3 |
| Repeatability | Very high (micron level) | Good but lower |
| Energy per injection | Low (capacitive load) | Higher (inductive load) |
| Minimum dwell time | Very short | Limited by inductance |

**Result:** Piezo injectors deliver finer fuel atomization, lower emissions (NOx, soot), lower BSFC, and quieter engine operation — which is why all premium automotive diesel systems (Bosch CRI2-16, Denso G3, Continental) now use piezo injectors.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]

**A DC motor drives a lead-screw linear actuator. The motor delivers torque 2 N·m at 1500 rpm to a screw of lead 5 mm/rev and efficiency 35%. Determine the axial thrust force and the linear speed of the nut.**

**Given:**
- Motor torque $\tau_m = 2 \text{ N·m}$
- Motor speed $N = 1500 \text{ rpm}$
- Lead screw lead $L = 5 \text{ mm/rev} = 0.005 \text{ m/rev}$
- Efficiency $\eta = 0.35$ (35%)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Angular velocity:**
$$\omega = 2\pi \times \frac{N}{60} = 2\pi \times \frac{1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Screw rotational speed in rev/s:**
$$N_{rev/s} = \frac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 3 — Linear speed of nut:**
Each revolution advances the nut by one lead:
$$v = L \times N_{rev/s} = 0.005 \times 25 = 0.125 \text{ m/s} = 125 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 4 — Thrust force:**
Energy balance — useful axial work = input mechanical work × efficiency.

Per revolution: useful output work = $F \times L$, input work = $\tau_m \times 2\pi$.

$$F \times L = \tau_m \times 2\pi \times \eta$$

Solving for $F$:
$$F = \frac{\tau_m \times 2\pi \times \eta}{L}$$

$$F = \frac{2 \times 2\pi \times 0.35}{0.005} = \frac{4.3982}{0.005} = 879.6 \text{ N}$$

**Final Answers:**
- **Linear speed of nut:** $v = 125 \text{ mm/s}$
- **Axial thrust force:** $F \approx 880 \text{ N}$

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in This Module:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up units (1–2 marks lost frequently):** Failing to convert bar → Pa (multiply by $10^5$), or L/min → m³/s (multiply by $10^{-3}/60$). *Always* write the unit conversion step explicitly.
>
> 2. **Wrong area formula for retraction (2–3 marks lost):** For double-acting cylinder retraction, students often use $A = \pi D^2 / 4$ instead of the annular area $A = \pi (D^2 - d^2) / 4$. This is the **single most common error** in this topic.
>
> 3. **Forgetting volumetric/mechanical efficiency in hydraulic motor problems (1–2 marks):** The *ideal* formulas give $\tau = PV_d / 2\pi$ and $N = Q/V_d$ — but actual output uses $\eta_m$ and $\eta_v$. KTU examiners specifically test this.
>
> 4. **Confusing linear and rotary kinematic variables:** Writing $v = Q/A_p$ (correct) but applying it to motor speed, or using $\tau = P A_p$ instead of $\tau = P V_d / 2\pi$ for a motor.
>
> 5. **Skipping the "Why" in descriptive questions:** A 7-mark descriptive question requires *both* the "what" and the "why". Simply stating "PZT expands when voltage is applied" without explaining the inverse piezoelectric effect loses marks.
>
> 6. **No free-body or sketch:** Even in numerical problems, a brief sketch with labeled forces and areas earns 1–2 extra marks per KTU valuation key.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Actuator** = final execution element of an ECU loop; converts energy domain → mechanical motion.
- [x] **Linear actuators** produce straight-line displacement (solenoid, hydraulic cylinder, piezo stack, lead screw).
- [x] **Rotary actuators** produce angular motion (DC, BLDC, stepper, SRM, hydraulic motor).
- [x] **Hydraulic cylinder force:** $F = P \cdot A_p$ (use annular area on retraction).
- [x] **Hydraulic cylinder speed:** $v = Q / A_p$.
- [x] **Hydraulic motor torque:** $\tau = P V_d / (2\pi)$ — multiply by $\eta_m$ for actual.
- [x] **Hydraulic motor speed:** $N = Q \eta_v / V_d$.
- [x] **DC motor torque-speed:** $\tau = k_t I$, $\omega = (V - IR_a) / k_e$, with $k_t = k_e$ in SI.
- [x] **Lead screw force:** $F = 2\pi \eta \tau / L$; **speed:** $v = L \cdot N_{rev/s}$.
- [x] **Piezo displacement:** $x = d_{33} \cdot n \cdot V$ (PZT constant ~$3 \times 10^{-10}$ m/V).
- [x] **Energy balance:** Input power × efficiency = Output power — applies to ALL actuator types.
- [x] **Double-acting retracting area:** $A_{ret} = \pi (D^2 - d^2)/4$ — memorize this.
- [x] **Modern automotive trend:** Hydraulic → Electromechanical (for ECU integration, efficiency, emissions).
- [x] **Unit conversions:** $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$; $1 \text{ L/min} = 1.667 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$; $1 \text{ hp} = 745.7 \text{ W}$.
- [x] **Automotive examples to memorize:** Solenoid injector (linear), EGR valve (rotary BLDC), EPS motor (rotary BLDC), Active suspension (hydraulic cylinder), Common-rail piezo (linear piezo), Turbocharger vane actuator (rotary stepper).
- [x] **Embedded control keywords:** PWM for solenoids, FOC for BLDC, TPU/eTPU for crank-triggered actuators, CAN/LIN for setpoint communication.
- [x] **Safety standards:** ISO 26262 — actuators in brake-by-wire, steer-by-wire, throttle-by-wire are ASIL-D rated.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoelectric Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT632 - AUTOMOTIVE EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Piezoelectric Actuator** is a solid-state, precision electro-mechanical transduction device that converts an applied electrical input (voltage or electric field) into a finely controlled, sub-micron to millimetre-range mechanical displacement (strain, force, or motion) by exploiting the **converse (inverse) piezoelectric effect** exhibited by certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials (e.g., Lead Zirconate Titanate — PZT, Barium Titanate, Quartz, PVDF polymer).

In the context of **Automotive Embedded Systems**, piezoelectric actuators are the "muscle fibres" of the modern vehicle — they perform ultra-fast, deterministic, and programmable mechanical work (typically in the **microsecond** regime) under the supervision of an ECU/MCU. They are the fundamental building blocks of *common-rail piezo injectors*, *piezo valve systems*, *active fuel injection*, *active suspension*, *active noise control (ANC) tweeters*, *piezo buzzers*, and *precision mirror/headlight levelling* systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT632 — Module 2):**
> The module examines actuators *beyond conventional solenoids*. Piezoelectric actuators sit at the intersection of **materials science**, **high-voltage analog electronics**, and **embedded control theory**. Examiners expect students to draw a clear line between the *Direct Piezoelectric Effect* (sensor mode — e.g., knock sensor) and the *Converse Piezoelectric Effect* (actuator mode).

### 1.2 The Two Flavours of the Piezoelectric Effect

| Effect | Energy Flow | Automotive Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Effect** (Curie & Jacques, 1880) | Mechanical Stress $\rightarrow$ Electrical Charge | Knock Sensor, Accelerometer, Tyre-pressure MEMS |
| **Converse Effect** (Lippmann, 1881; proved by Curies) | Electrical Field $\rightarrow$ Mechanical Strain | Piezo Injector, Piezo Buzzer, Haptic Feedback |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge-Crystal"

Imagine a kitchen **sponge soaked in water**, but the sponge is made of an asymmetric crystal lattice (the unit cell has no centre of symmetry). When you **squeeze** the sponge (apply mechanical stress), droplets of charge literally "drip out" — this is the **direct effect**.

Now reverse the analogy: when the ECU applies a voltage (electric field) across the crystal, the internal "sponge cells" *stretch* or *compress* along a specific axis. This microscopic lattice deformation summates over millions of unit cells to produce a macroscopic, usable push/pull of a few **nanometres to micrometres**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the lattice must be non-centrosymmetric:**
> In a symmetric lattice (e.g., NaCl), a compressive force pushes positive and negative charges equally towards the centre — the net dipole moment cancels out, and *no* charge appears on the surface. Asymmetric crystals like PZT have their positive and negative charge centres *offset* even at rest, so a small deformation produces a large, net, measurable dipole shift.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Piezoelectric charge constant** $d_{ij}$: typically **$d_{33} \approx 300\text{–}600\ \text{pC/N}$** for PZT-5H.
- **Piezoelectric voltage constant** $g_{ij}$: typically **$g_{33} \approx 20\text{–}30 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V·m/N}$**.
- **Dielectric constant (relative)** $\epsilon_r$: **$1200\text{–}3500$** for soft PZT.
- **Curie Temperature** $T_C$: **$\sim 150\text{–}350^\circ\text{C}$** — above this, the spontaneous polarisation is destroyed and the material becomes paraelectric (no piezoelectricity).
- **Coupling factor** $k$: dimensionless, **$0.3\text{–}0.7$** depending on mode.
- **Mechanical Q-factor**: up to **$>1000$** for hard PZT.
- **Response time**: **$<10\ \mu\text{s}$** — orders of magnitude faster than solenoid actuators.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Layer by Layer

A piezoelectric actuator operates in three coupled physical domains simultaneously:

1. **Electrical domain** — the ECU drives a high-voltage (typically **$100\text{–}200\ \text{V}$** for stacks, up to **$1\text{–}2\ \text{kV}$** for tube actuators) across electrodes.
2. **Electro-mechanical domain** — the electric field $E$ polarises the ferroelectric domains, producing strain $S$ proportional to $E$.
3. **Mechanical domain** — the resulting strain $S$ generates stress $\sigma$, which pushes a piston/nozzle/injector needle against the fluid pressure.

**Why are multiple thin layers used instead of one thick disc?**
A single PZT disc requires thousands of volts to deform significantly. By slicing the ceramic into **$n = 100\text{–}200$** thin layers ($\sim 50\text{–}100\ \mu\text{m}$ each) and connecting them *electrically in parallel* but *mechanically in series*, the total displacement is multiplied by $n$, while the drive voltage is divided by $n$ — bringing it within the range of an automotive **$12\ \text{V}$** system after a DC-DC boost converter stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stack vs. Disc vs. Tube vs. Bimorph — the four topologies the KTU examiner loves to compare:**
> - **Stack (most common in injectors):** high force, moderate displacement, low voltage.
> - **Disc (buzzer/ANC):** low force, large displacement, requires high voltage.
> - **Tube (lens positioning):** omnidirectional, hollow for light passage.
> - **Bimorph (haptic / speaker):** two bonded plates — bends like a bimetallic strip.

### 2.2 Constitutive Equations — The Heart of the Theory

The linear constitutive equations of piezoelectricity (IEEE Standard on Piezoelectricity, 1987) couple electrical and mechanical variables. There are four equivalent forms; the most common are the **stress-charge** form and the **strain-charge** form.

**Strain-Charge Form (E-form, used in actuator analysis):**

$$S_i = s^{E}_{ij}\,\sigma_j + d_{in}\,E_n$$

$$D_m = d_{mj}\,\sigma_j + \epsilon^{T}_{mn}\,E_n$$

**Stress-Charge Form (D-form, used in sensor analysis):**

$$\sigma_i = c^{E}_{ij}\,S_j - e_{in}\,E_n$$

$$D_m = e_{mj}\,S_j + \epsilon^{S}_{mn}\,E_n$$

Where:
- $S_i$ = mechanical strain tensor (unitless, m/m)
- $\sigma_j$ = mechanical stress tensor ($\text{N/m}^2$ or Pa)
- $E_n$ = electric field ($\text{V/m}$)
- $D_m$ = electric displacement / charge density ($\text{C/m}^2$)
- $s^{E}_{ij}$ = elastic compliance at constant $E$ ($\text{m}^2/\text{N}$)
- $d_{in}$ = piezoelectric charge constant ($\text{C/N}$ or $\text{m/V}$)
- $e_{in}$ = piezoelectric stress constant ($\text{N/V·m}$ or $\text{C/m}^2$)
- $\epsilon^{T}_{mn}$ = permittivity at constant stress ($\text{F/m}$)
- $\epsilon^{S}_{mn}$ = permittivity at constant strain ($\text{F/m}$)
- $c^{E}_{ij}$ = elastic stiffness at constant $E$ ($\text{N/m}^2$)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "d-matrix" symmetry:** For the poled ceramic in its standard orientation, the most useful coefficient is $d_{33}$ — the strain produced *parallel* to the applied field. This is the parameter you cite for a stack actuator.

### 2.3 The Free-Displacement and Blocked-Force Equations

These two equations are the **most-tested** items in any piezoelectric actuator problem.

**Free Displacement $\Delta L_{\text{free}}$** — occurs when the actuator moves against *no* external load:

$$\Delta L_{\text{free}} = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$$

**Blocked Force $F_{\text{block}}$** — the force the actuator generates when its motion is *fully restrained*:

$$F_{\text{block}} = \frac{n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V \cdot A}{s^{E}_{33} \cdot L}$$

**Effective Stiffness of the stack** $k_p$:

$$k_p = \frac{A}{s^{E}_{33} \cdot L}$$

**Stroke under external load $F_L$** (linearised, no hysteresis):

$$\Delta L = \Delta L_{\text{free}} \left(1 - \frac{F_L}{F_{\text{block}}}\right)$$

### 2.4 Energy and Electrical Equivalent Circuit

A piezo stack behaves electrically like a **capacitor** (because it is a dielectric with electrodes). The equivalent circuit is a capacitor $C_p$ in series (or parallel, depending on configuration) with a dependent voltage/current source representing the electromechanical coupling.

**Capacitance of the stack:**

$$C_p = n \cdot \frac{\epsilon^{T}_{33} \cdot A}{t}$$

where $t$ is the thickness of a single layer. Typical values: **$1\text{–}10\ \mu\text{F}$** for injector stacks.

**Stored electrostatic energy** (at voltage $V$):

$$W_{\text{elec}} = \frac{1}{2}\,C_p\,V^{2}$$

**Mechanical work output** (linear, ideal):

$$W_{\text{mech}} = \frac{1}{2}\,F_{\text{block}}\,\Delta L_{\text{free}}$$

The **coupling factor $k$** relates electrical input to mechanical output:

$$k^{2} = \frac{W_{\text{mech}}}{W_{\text{elec}}} = \frac{d^{2}_{33}}{s^{E}_{33}\,\epsilon^{T}_{33}}$$

### 2.5 Automotive Drive Electronics — The Embedded View

The embedded ECU does not drive the piezo stack directly from a GPIO. A typical automotive piezo driver subsystem contains:

1. **Boost converter** — boosts $12\ \text{V}$ battery to **$200\text{–}250\ \text{V}$** DC link (often a multi-stage inductor-based flyback or SEPIC topology).
2. **Charge-recovery (energy-recycling) capacitor bank** — recovers up to **$60\text{–}80\%$** of the stored charge between injections.
3. **H-bridge or push-pull high-voltage switch** — uses IGBTs or MOSFETs rated for $>400\ \text{V}$ to apply reverse polarity for fast discharge.
4. **Current-sense and over-voltage protection** — prevents dielectric breakdown.
5. **MCU control** — typically a dedicated ASIC (e.g., Infineon TLE8242, TI DRV2700) running charge/discharge timing in the **$50\text{–}300\ \mu\text{s}$** window.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why charge-recovery matters in automotive:**
> A piezo injector charges/discharges up to **$10$ times per second per cylinder** (multi-injection strategies). Without energy recovery, the thermal load on the driver would exceed $50\ \text{W}$ — a major EMC and fuel-economy concern. Charge-recovery shrinks this dissipation by an order of magnitude.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta L_{\text{free}} = n\,d_{33}\,V$ | Free displacement | m | Actuator sizing |
| 2 | $F_{\text{block}} = k_p\,\Delta L_{\text{free}} = \dfrac{A\,d_{33}\,V}{s^{E}_{33}\,L}$ | Blocked force | N | Actuator sizing |
| 3 | $k_p = \dfrac{A}{s^{E}_{33}\,L}$ | Stack stiffness | N/m | Mechanics |
| 4 | $\Delta L = \Delta L_{\text{free}}\!\left(1 - F_L / F_{\text{block}}\right)$ | Stroke under load | m | Mechanics |
| 5 | $C_p = n\,\dfrac{\epsilon^{T}_{33}\,A}{t}$ | Stack capacitance | F | Driver design |
| 6 | $W_{\text{elec}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,C_p\,V^{2}$ | Stored electrical energy | J | Energy budget |
| 7 | $W_{\text{mech}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,F_{\text{block}}\,\Delta L_{\text{free}}$ | Mechanical work output | J | Efficiency |
| 8 | $k^{2} = \dfrac{d^{2}_{33}}{s^{E}_{33}\,\epsilon^{T}_{33}}$ | Electromechanical coupling factor | dimensionless | Material selection |
| 9 | $D_i = d_{ij}\,\sigma_j + \epsilon^{T}_{ij}\,E_j$ | Electric displacement (actuator eq.) | C/m² | Constitutive |
| 10 | $S_i = s^{E}_{ij}\,\sigma_j + d_{ik}\,E_k$ | Strain (actuator eq.) | m/m | Constitutive |
| 11 | $V_{\text{out, sensor}} = g_{33}\,t\,\sigma$ | Open-circuit sensor voltage | V | Knock sensor |
| 12 | $Q = d_{33}\,F$ | Charge generated (sensor) | C | Knock sensor |
| 13 | $\tau_{\text{discharge}} = R_{\text{leak}}\,C_p$ | Discharge time constant | s | Driver timing |
| 14 | $f_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k_p/m}$ | Mechanical resonance | Hz | Dynamics |
| 15 | $T_C$ — Curie temperature | Phase transition | °C | Material limit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick sanity check for the table:**
> Notice that for a typical Bosch piezo common-rail injector: $n \approx 200$ layers, $d_{33} \approx 600\ \text{pC/N}$, $V \approx 200\ \text{V}$, giving $\Delta L_{\text{free}} \approx 200 \times 600\times10^{-12} \times 200 = 24\ \mu\text{m}$ — which matches manufacturer datasheets.

### 2.7 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Emission control:** Piezo injectors enable *multiple precisely-timed injections per combustion cycle* (pilot, main, after), drastically reducing $\text{NO}_x$ and particulates in diesel engines.
- **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness):** Active mounts using piezo stacks cancel engine vibration in real time.
- **Fuel economy:** Faster, cleaner injection means more complete combustion.
- **Miniaturisation:** Piezo valves are replacing solenoid valves in transmission control units (TCUs) and EGR loops.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are *exhaustive* and *unabridged* — no algebraic step is skipped. This is the level of rigour expected for a KTU 14-mark question.

---

### 3.1 Derivation of the Free-Displacement Equation $\Delta L_{\text{free}} = n\,d_{33}\,V$

**Starting point — definition of $d_{33}$:**

The piezoelectric charge constant $d_{33}$ is defined as the strain produced per unit applied electric field *in the same direction* (polar axis = 3-direction):

$$d_{33} \;\equiv\; \frac{S_3}{E_3} \quad \text{(for } \sigma_3 = 0 \text{)}$$

**Step 1 — Single-layer strain.**
For a single ceramic disc of thickness $t$, with electrodes on the two faces, the applied field is uniform:

$$E_3 = \frac{V_{\text{layer}}}{t}$$

The induced strain along the polar axis is:

$$S_3 = d_{33}\,E_3 = d_{33}\,\frac{V_{\text{layer}}}{t}$$

**Step 2 — Single-layer displacement.**
Strain is displacement per unit length:

$$S_3 = \frac{\Delta L_{\text{single}}}{t}$$

Equating the two expressions for $S_3$:

$$\frac{\Delta L_{\text{single}}}{t} = d_{33}\,\frac{V_{\text{layer}}}{t}$$

$$\Delta L_{\text{single}} = d_{33}\,V_{\text{layer}}$$

Note that the thickness $t$ cancels — a single layer always displaces by $d_{33}\,V$, *independent* of its thickness.

**Step 3 — Stack of $n$ layers in mechanical series.**
When $n$ layers are stacked, their mechanical displacements *add* (each layer expands by $\Delta L_{\text{single}}$). However, electrically the layers are connected in *parallel* (each layer sees the same voltage $V_{\text{layer}} = V$):

$$\Delta L_{\text{free}} = n \cdot \Delta L_{\text{single}} = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V_{\text{layer}}$$

Because in the parallel connection all layers see the full applied voltage $V$:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta L_{\text{free}} = n\,d_{33}\,V\;}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical sanity check.**
For a Bosch-style injector: $n = 200$, $d_{33} = 600 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m/V}$, $V = 200\ \text{V}$:

$$\Delta L_{\text{free}} = 200 \times 600\times10^{-12} \times 200 = 2.4 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 24\ \mu\text{m}$$

This is the *unloaded* stroke — the actual stroke under spring + fuel pressure is somewhat smaller (handled by the next derivation).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Blocked-Force Equation

**Step 1 — Mechanical definition of stiffness.**
A stack of cross-section $A$ and total length $L = n\,t$ behaves mechanically like a spring. The stiffness $k_p$ is defined as the force required to produce unit displacement:

$$k_p = \frac{F}{\Delta L} = \frac{A}{s^{E}_{33}\,L} = \frac{E_p\,A}{L}$$

where the second equality uses the relation between compliance $s^{E}_{33}$ and Young's modulus $E_p = 1/s^{E}_{33}$ for the poled ceramic.

**Step 2 — Apply the constitutive strain equation at constant electric field.**
The total strain is the sum of mechanical strain (due to blocked stress) and piezoelectric strain:

$$S_3 = s^{E}_{33}\,\sigma_3 + d_{33}\,E_3$$

**Step 3 — Blocked condition.**
"Blocked" means $\Delta L = 0$, hence $S_3 = 0$:

$$0 = s^{E}_{33}\,\sigma_3 + d_{33}\,E_3$$

Solving for the blocked stress:

$$\sigma_3 = -\frac{d_{33}}{s^{E}_{33}}\,E_3$$

**Step 4 — Convert stress to force.**

$$F_{\text{block}} = \sigma_3 \cdot A = -\frac{d_{33}\,A}{s^{E}_{33}}\,E_3$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $E_3 = V/t$ and $L = n\,t$:**

$$F_{\text{block}} = -\frac{d_{33}\,A}{s^{E}_{33}} \cdot \frac{V}{t} = -\frac{d_{33}\,A\,V}{s^{E}_{33}\,t}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $n$:

$$F_{\text{block}} = -\frac{n\,d_{33}\,A\,V}{s^{E}_{33}\,n\,t} = -\frac{n\,d_{33}\,A\,V}{s^{E}_{33}\,L}$$

The sign simply indicates direction (compression or tension). The magnitude is:

$$\boxed{\;F_{\text{block}} = \frac{n\,d_{33}\,A\,V}{s^{E}_{33}\,L} = k_p\,\Delta L_{\text{free}}\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Stroke Under External Load

**Step 1 — Set up force equilibrium.**
When an external load $F_L$ is applied, the actuator produces stroke $\Delta L$ and exerts a force equal and opposite to $F_L$ (quasi-static assumption). The mechanical strain is:

$$S_3^{\text{mech}} = -\frac{F_L}{k_p\,L} \quad \text{(Hooke's law, compressive convention)}$$

**Step 2 — Total strain is mechanical strain + piezoelectric strain:**

$$S_3 = s^{E}_{33}\,\sigma_3 + d_{33}\,E_3 = -\frac{F_L}{k_p\,L} + d_{33}\,\frac{V}{t}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\Delta L = S_3 \cdot L$:**

$$\Delta L = -\frac{F_L}{k_p} + d_{33}\,\frac{V}{t}\,L$$

**Step 4 — Recognise that $d_{33}\,V\,L/t = n\,d_{33}\,V = \Delta L_{\text{free}}$:**

$$\Delta L = \Delta L_{\text{free}} - \frac{F_L}{k_p}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $k_p = F_{\text{block}}/\Delta L_{\text{free}}$:**

$$\Delta L = \Delta L_{\text{free}} - \frac{F_L}{F_{\text{block}}/\Delta L_{\text{free}}} = \Delta L_{\text{free}}\left(1 - \frac{F_L}{F_{\text{block}}}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta L = \Delta L_{\text{free}}\!\left(1 - \frac{F_L}{F_{\text{block}}}\right)\;}$$

**Interpretation:**
- When $F_L = 0$: full free displacement is achieved.
- When $F_L = F_{\text{block}}$: stroke collapses to zero — all the strain is "used up" pushing against the load.
- The actuator is at its **maximum mechanical work output** at the midpoint $F_L = F_{\text{block}}/2$, where:

$$W_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{4}\,F_{\text{block}}\,\Delta L_{\text{free}}$$

This is analogous to the **maximum power transfer theorem** in electrical circuits (impedance matching).

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Coupling Factor

**Step 1 — Electrical energy input.**
The stack stores energy like a capacitor charged to $V$:

$$W_{\text{elec}} = \frac{1}{2}\,C_p\,V^{2} = \frac{1}{2}\,\frac{\epsilon^{T}_{33}\,A}{t}\,V^{2}$$

(for a single layer — multiply by $n$ later for a stack, the ratio is unchanged.)

**Step 2 — Mechanical energy output at maximum-power point:**

$$W_{\text{mech, max}} = \frac{1}{4}\,F_{\text{block}}\,\Delta L_{\text{free}} = \frac{1}{4}\cdot\frac{d_{33}\,A\,V}{s^{E}_{33}\,t}\cdot d_{33}\,V = \frac{d^{2}_{33}\,A\,V^{2}}{4\,s^{E}_{33}\,t}$$

**Step 3 — Take the ratio:**

$$\frac{W_{\text{mech}}}{W_{\text{elec}}} = \frac{d^{2}_{33}\,A\,V^{2}/(4\,s^{E}_{33}\,t)}{(1/2)\,\epsilon^{T}_{33}\,A\,V^{2}/t} = \frac{d^{2}_{33}}{2\,s^{E}_{33}\,\epsilon^{T}_{33}}$$

**Step 4 — Define the squared coupling factor conventionally as twice this ratio:**

$$k^{2}_{33} = \frac{d^{2}_{33}}{s^{E}_{33}\,\epsilon^{T}_{33}}$$

$$\boxed{\;k^{2}_{33} = \frac{d^{2}_{33}}{s^{E}_{33}\,\epsilon^{T}_{33}}\;}$$

A higher $k$ means the material converts electrical energy to mechanical energy more efficiently.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — KTU-Style

**Problem:** A PZT-5H piezo stack has $n = 150$ layers, each of thickness $t = 80\ \mu\text{m}$ and electrode area $A = 7.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2$. Material data: $d_{33} = 593 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C/N}$, $s^{E}_{33} = 16.5 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}^2/\text{N}$, $\epsilon^{T}_{33} = 3.42 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{F/m}$. The driver applies $V = 200\ \text{V}$. The injector needle spring exerts a load $F_L = 300\ \text{N}$ against the stack. Find: (a) free displacement, (b) blocked force, (c) actual stroke, (d) coupling factor.

**Solution:**

**(a) Free displacement:**

$$\Delta L_{\text{free}} = n\,d_{33}\,V = 150 \times 593\times10^{-12} \times 200$$

$$\Delta L_{\text{free}} = 1.779 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 17.79\ \mu\text{m}$$

**(b) Blocked force:**

Total stack length $L = n\,t = 150 \times 80\times10^{-6} = 0.012\ \text{m}$

$$F_{\text{block}} = \frac{n\,d_{33}\,A\,V}{s^{E}_{33}\,L} = \frac{150 \times 593\times10^{-12} \times 7.0\times10^{-6} \times 200}{16.5\times10^{-12} \times 0.012}$$

Numerator:

$$150 \times 593\times10^{-12} \times 7.0\times10^{-6} \times 200 = 1.2453 \times 10^{-10}$$

Denominator:

$$16.5\times10^{-12} \times 0.012 = 1.98 \times 10^{-13}$$

$$F_{\text{block}} = \frac{1.2453 \times 10^{-10}}{1.98 \times 10^{-13}} = 628.94\ \text{N}$$

**(c) Actual stroke under load $F_L = 300\ \text{N}$:**

$$\Delta L = 17.79\ \mu\text{m} \times \left(1 - \frac{300}{628.94}\right) = 17.79 \times (1 - 0.4770) = 17.79 \times 0.5230$$

$$\Delta L = 9.30\ \mu\text{m}$$

**(d) Coupling factor:**

$$k^{2}_{33} = \frac{d^{2}_{33}}{s^{E}_{33}\,\epsilon^{T}_{33}} = \frac{(593\times10^{-12})^{2}}{16.5\times10^{-12} \times 3.42\times10^{-8}}$$

$$k^{2}_{33} = \frac{3.516 \times 10^{-19}}{5.643 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.6231$$

$$k_{33} = 0.789$$

This is typical for soft PZT — about **$62\%$** of the input electrical energy is theoretically convertible to mechanical work.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagrams below use **Mermaid v10+** syntax. Node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `m1`, `step1`) to avoid conflicts with reserved keywords. Labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting.

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Piezo Injector Driver (Automotive Subsystem)

### 4.3 Equivalent Circuit of the Piezo Stack with Mechanical Load

```mermaid
graph TD
    n1[Voltage Source V Driver Output] --> n2[Series Resistance R_on Switch]
    n2 --> n3[Parallel Capacitance C_p Stack Electrodes]
    n3 --> n4[Dependent Current Source i_p d33 dS/dt]
    n4 --> n5[Mechanical Domain Mass m Spring k_p Damper c]
    n5 --> n6[External Load F_L Spring plus Fuel Pressure]
    n3 -.->|Dielectric Loss R_leak| n7[Ground]

```

### 4.4 Conceptual Cross-Section of a Piezo Stack Actuator (Block Mapping)

```mermaid
graph TD
    a1[Top Electrode Positive] --> a2[PZT Layer 1 80 microns]
    a2 --> a3[Internal Electrode Grounded]
    a3 --> a4[PZT Layer 2 80 microns]
    a4 --> a5[Internal Electrode Positive]
    a5 --> a6[PZT Layer 3 80 microns]
    a6 --> a7[More Layers Repeating n equals 150]
    a7 --> a8[Bottom Electrode Positive]
    a8 --> a9[Mechanical Output Piston]
    a9 --> a10[Coupling Capillary Seal]
    a10 --> a11[Hydraulic Fluid Coupling]
    a11 --> a12[Injector Needle]

    style a1 fill:#cccccc
    style a3 fill:#f5a623
    style a5 fill:#f5a623
    style a7 fill:#f5a623
    style a8 fill:#cccccc
    style a12 fill:#d0021b,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the alternating electrode pattern?** Adjacent layers are polarised in opposite directions, but the *electric field* in every layer points the *same way* relative to the polarisation vector — so every layer expands simultaneously. The result is a "cumulative" expansion with a *low* drive voltage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Distinguish clearly between the **direct** and **converse** piezoelectric effects. Give one automotive application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Direct piezoelectric effect (1 mark):** The generation of an electric charge (or voltage) on the surface of certain non-centrosymmetric crystals when they are subjected to mechanical stress. Energy conversion: *mechanical $\rightarrow$ electrical*.
- **Converse (inverse) piezoelectric effect (1 mark):** The production of mechanical strain or deformation in a piezoelectric crystal when an external electric field is applied across it. Energy conversion: *electrical $\rightarrow$ mechanical*.
- **Automotive applications (1 mark):**
  - *Direct*: Piezoelectric **knock sensor** on the engine block — converts cylinder vibration into a voltage signal processed by the ECU for ignition timing.
  - *Converse*: **Common-rail piezo injector** — converts a high-voltage pulse into the mechanical lift of the injector needle to meter fuel.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the **Curie temperature** of a piezoelectric material? Why is it critical for an automotive under-hood application?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The *Curie temperature* $T_C$ is the temperature above which a ferroelectric piezoelectric material loses its spontaneous polarisation and transitions to a paraelectric (centrosymmetric) phase, thereby *losing all piezoelectric properties*.
- **Criticality in under-hood use (2 marks):** Engine bay temperatures routinely reach $120\text{–}150^\circ\text{C}$, and turbo-charged applications may exceed $180^\circ\text{C}$. If the PZT actuator's $T_C$ is exceeded, the domains de-pol and the actuator permanently fails. Hence, automotive-grade PZT must be selected with $T_C > 200^\circ\text{C}$ (e.g., PZT-5A1 with $T_C \approx 350^\circ\text{C}$) and operated with substantial thermal margin.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Design-Oriented**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working principle of a **multi-layer piezoelectric stack actuator**. Explain the role of internal electrodes and why they are connected alternately.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A piezo stack has $n = 200$ layers, each $t = 100\ \mu\text{m}$ thick, electrode area $A = 1.0\ \text{cm}^2$, $d_{33} = 500\ \text{pC/N}$, $s^{E}_{33} = 20 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}^2/\text{N}$, driven at $V = 150\ \text{V}$. The stack works against a spring load equivalent to $F_L = 250\ \text{N}$. Calculate:
  (i) the free displacement,
  (ii) the blocked force,
  (iii) the actual stroke under load,
  (iv) the stack stiffness $k_p$.

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

- **Construction (3 marks):** A piezoelectric stack actuator is fabricated by tape-casting thin PZT sheets ($\sim 50\text{–}100\ \mu\text{m}$), screen-printing *alternating* electrode patterns, stacking them, laminating under pressure, and sintering. The two ends are terminated with thick end-electrodes. The total stack length is typically $10\text{–}30\ \text{mm}$ for an automotive injector; cross-sectional area is on the order of $7\text{–}10\ \text{mm}^2$.
- **Role of alternating electrodes (2 marks):** Adjacent ceramic layers are polarised in opposite directions during poling. By connecting the *internal* electrodes in alternation, every layer is subjected to the *same* direction of electric field, which is also the *same* direction as its polarisation. Hence every layer expands in the same direction, and the displacements *add up* (mechanical series). Electrically, the layers are in *parallel* — they all see the full applied voltage.
- **Working (2 marks):** When the driver circuit applies a high voltage, the cumulative strain across $n$ layers lifts the actuator's output piston. The piston is mechanically coupled (often via hydraulic amplification) to the injector needle, opening the nozzle to inject fuel. On discharge, the stack contracts and a strong return spring snaps the needle closed, ending injection.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks — distribute 1.75 per sub-part):**

Given: $n = 200$, $t = 100 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$, $A = 1.0\ \text{cm}^2 = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$, $d_{33} = 500 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C/N}$, $s^{E}_{33} = 20 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}^2/\text{N}$, $V = 150\ \text{V}$, $F_L = 250\ \text{N}$.

**(i) Free displacement:**

$$\Delta L_{\text{free}} = n\,d_{33}\,V = 200 \times 500 \times 10^{-12} \times 150$$

$$\Delta L_{\text{free}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 15\ \mu\text{m}$$

**(ii) Blocked force:**

Total length $L = n\,t = 200 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 0.02\ \text{m}$

$$F_{\text{block}} = \frac{n\,d_{33}\,A\,V}{s^{E}_{33}\,L}$$

$$F_{\text{block}} = \frac{200 \times 500\times10^{-12} \times 1.0\times10^{-4} \times 150}{20\times10^{-12} \times 0.02}$$

Numerator: $200 \times 500\times10^{-12} \times 1.0\times10^{-4} \times 150 = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$
Denominator: $20\times10^{-12} \times 0.02 = 4.0 \times 10^{-13}$

$$F_{\text{block}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-9}}{4.0 \times 10^{-13}} = 3750\ \text{N}$$

**(iii) Actual stroke under load:**

$$\Delta L = \Delta L_{\text{free}}\left(1 - \frac{F_L}{F_{\text{block}}}\right) = 15\ \mu\text{m} \times \left(1 - \frac{250}{3750}\right)$$

$$\Delta L = 15\ \mu\text{m} \times (1 - 0.0667) = 15 \times 0.9333 = 14.0\ \mu\text{m}$$

**(iv) Stack stiffness:**

$$k_p = \frac{A}{s^{E}_{33}\,L} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{20 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.02} = \frac{1.0\times10^{-4}}{4.0\times10^{-13}} = 2.5 \times 10^{8}\ \text{N/m}$$

**Sanity check:** $k_p \times \Delta L_{\text{free}} = 2.5\times10^8 \times 1.5\times10^{-5} = 3750\ \text{N}$ ✓ — matches $F_{\text{block}}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Theory + Application**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Derive the constitutive equations of the piezoelectric effect in the **strain-charge form**. Define each term clearly. How does this formulation reduce to a simple displacement equation in the case of a *free* multi-layer stack actuator?

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Describe the **automotive common-rail piezo injector** system. With a block diagram, explain how the embedded ECU controls injection timing, and discuss the need for **charge-recovery** in the high-voltage driver stage.

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) Derivation of Constitutive Equations (7 marks):**

The piezoelectric constitutive equations are *empirical* linear relations coupling the two independent electrical variables (electric field $E$, electric displacement $D$) and the two independent mechanical variables (stress $\sigma$, strain $S$). The **strain-charge form** is preferred for actuator analysis because it expresses the *output* (strain, charge) in terms of the *independent* inputs (stress, field).

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Consider an applied electric field $E$ in the absence of mechanical stress. The resulting strain is purely piezoelectric:

$$S_i\big\vert_{\sigma=0} = d_{in}\,E_n$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Consider an applied mechanical stress $\sigma$ in the absence of electric field. The resulting strain is purely elastic (Hooke's law generalised):

$$S_i\big\vert_{E=0} = s^{E}_{ij}\,\sigma_j$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** By the **superposition principle** (linear piezoelectricity), the total strain is the sum:

$$S_i = s^{E}_{ij}\,\sigma_j + d_{in}\,E_n \quad \text{...(Strain equation)}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** By a similar argument for the electric displacement (charge density), but noting that the second-order coupling term is conventionally written with the *transpose* $d$-matrix:

$$D_m = d_{mj}\,\sigma_j + \epsilon^{T}_{mn}\,E_n \quad \text{...(Charge equation)}$$

**Step 5 — Reduction to free-displacement form (2 marks):**
In a *free* multi-layer stack, $\sigma = 0$ (no external stress), so the first equation reduces to:

$$S_3 = d_{33}\,E_3 = d_{33}\,\frac{V}{t}$$

Multiplying by the total length $L = n\,t$:

$$\Delta L_{\text{free}} = S_3\,L = d_{33}\,\frac{V}{t}\,n\,t = n\,d_{33}\,V$$

This is the displacement equation used throughout the design.

**(b) Common-Rail Piezo Injector (7 marks):**

- **System overview (2 marks):** A high-pressure (**$1500\text{–}2500\ \text{bar}$**) fuel rail supplies diesel to each cylinder. Each injector contains a piezo stack actuator that, when energised, lifts the needle off its seat via a hydraulic coupler, allowing fuel to spray into the combustion chamber. Bosch (since 2000, e.g., CRI2-16) and Siemens/Continental (now Vitesco) are leading manufacturers.
- **ECU control flow (2 marks):** The ECU receives sensor inputs (CAM, CRANK, rail pressure, coolant temp, $\text{O}_2$, knock) and computes a *fuel mass map*. It then schedules a sequence of **pilot, main, and post injections** (each $50\text{–}300\ \mu\text{s}$ long) and triggers the high-voltage driver to charge the stack to $200\ \text{V}$ for opening and to discharge it (with energy recovery) for closing.
- **Need for charge-recovery (3 marks):**
  - A typical injector stack has $C_p \approx 3\ \mu\text{F}$. At $V = 200\ \text{V}$, the stored energy per pulse is $W = \tfrac{1}{2}\,C_p\,V^2 = 0.06\ \text{J}$.
  - At 8 injections per combustion cycle $\times$ 4 cylinders $\times$ engine speed $4000\ \text{rpm}$: $\sim 2.1 \times 10^6$ pulses/min $\approx 35{,}000$ pulses/s.
  - Total energy throughput: $35{,}000 \times 0.06 = 2100\ \text{J/s} = 2100\ \text{W}$.
  - Without recovery, **$2.1\ \text{kW}$** would be dissipated in the IGBTs and diodes — unacceptable. With $75\%$ charge recovery, dissipation falls to $\sim 525\ \text{W}$, manageable with a small heat sink.
  - Recovery also reduces battery load, improves cold-start, and decreases $\text{CO}_2$ indirectly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $d_{33}$ units:** Many students write "$d_{33} = 600\ \text{pC/N}$" and then misuse it as if it were $\text{m/V}$ in a strain equation. *The numerical value is the same* (because $1\ \text{C/N} \equiv 1\ \text{m/V}$ by dimensional analysis), but in a derivation you must show the unit conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Sign errors in $F_{\text{block}}$:** A blocked force is *compressive*; depending on the sign convention for stress, you may get a negative answer. Always state the magnitude.
> 3. **Forgetting $n$:** A frequent error is to compute the stroke of a *single* layer and forget to multiply by the number of layers. Always state the system topology first.
> 4. **Ignoring hysteresis/creep:** Real PZT exhibits **5\%–15\% hysteresis** and **creep** over time. A purely linear analysis *underestimates* worst-case positioning error. Mention this if the question is "explain the limitations".
> 5. **Mixing up stack capacitance and dielectric constant:** Use the formula $C_p = n\,\epsilon^{T}_{33}\,A/t$, *not* $C_p = \epsilon^{T}_{33}\,A/L$ (the latter would give the capacitance of a *single* solid block).
> 6. **Missing the charge-recovery argument:** In any 14-mark question on automotive piezo drivers, *not* discussing energy recovery loses at least 2–3 marks. Examiners consider it essential.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Piezoelectricity = asymmetric crystal lattice + mechanical or electrical excitation.** Without broken inversion symmetry, there is no coupling.
- **Direct effect** $\Rightarrow$ *sensor* (mechanical $\rightarrow$ electrical). **Converse effect** $\Rightarrow$ *actuator* (electrical $\rightarrow$ mechanical). Same material can do both.
- **Key material:** **PZT** (Lead Zirconate Titanate) — the workhorse of automotive piezo applications. Soft PZT for high strain, hard PZT for high force and low loss.
- **Multi-layer stack geometry:** layers are *electrically parallel* and *mechanically series* — gives low drive voltage with multiplied displacement.
- **Free displacement:** $\Delta L_{\text{free}} = n\,d_{33}\,V$ — independent of layer thickness.
- **Blocked force:** $F_{\text{block}} = k_p\,\Delta L_{\text{free}}$ — depends on geometry and material compliance.
- **Stroke under load:** Linear taper from $\Delta L_{\text{free}}$ (at $F_L = 0$) to $0$ (at $F_L = F_{\text{block}}$). **Maximum mechanical work** at $F_L = F_{\text{block}}/2$.
- **Coupling factor $k_{33}$** for soft PZT: **$\sim 0.6\text{–}0.75$** — high, but not unity. Defines the upper limit of energy conversion.
- **Capacitive load:** The stack looks like a **$1\text{–}10\ \mu\text{F}$** capacitor to the driver. Charge and discharge currents can exceed **$10\ \text{A}$** during transitions.
- **Drive electronics chain:** Battery $12\ \text{V}$ $\rightarrow$ Boost converter $\rightarrow$ HV DC link ($\sim 200\ \text{V}$) $\rightarrow$ H-bridge switch $\rightarrow$ Piezo stack. Charge-recovery recycles **$60\text{–}80\%$** of the energy.
- **Curie temperature $T_C$** is the *hard limit* — must remain $>200^\circ\text{C}$ margin from peak under-hood temperature.
- **Hysteresis and creep** are the *practical* limits — open-loop accuracy is only $85\text{–}90\%$; closed-loop feedback (charge-sensing or position-sensing) is mandatory in high-precision applications.
- **Response time** is in the **microsecond** regime — $1000\times$ faster than solenoid valves, enabling multi-injection strategies in modern diesel and direct-injection petrol engines.
- **Automotive uses:** common-rail injectors, piezo buzzer (reverse-warning), knock sensor, ESP hydraulic unit pre-charge, active engine mounts, ANC speakers, piezo haptic touchscreens, tyre-pressure sensors.
- **Comparison with solenoid actuator:** Solenoid $\sim 1\ \text{ms}$ response, larger force, lower precision; Piezo $\sim 10\ \mu\text{s}$, finer metering, higher electronic complexity. *Trade-off is cost vs. emissions performance.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra for this topic:**
> *Memorise $\Delta L_{\text{free}} = n d_{33} V$, $F_{\text{block}} = k_p \Delta L_{\text{free}}$, and the $\Delta L$-vs-$F_L$ linear relation. These three equations appear in nearly every numerical question on piezoelectric actuators. The rest is materials science + driver electronics context.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration of Sensors and Actuators in Embedded Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT632 - AUTOMOTIVE EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Integration of Sensors and Actuators in Embedded Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sensor:** A transducer that converts a physical, chemical, or biological measurand (e.g., temperature, pressure, position, velocity, chemical concentration) into a corresponding electrical signal (voltage, current, frequency, or digital word) that can be processed by an embedded controller.

**Actuator:** A transducer that converts an electrical control signal from an embedded controller into a physical action — mechanical motion, heat, light, or fluid flow — to influence the system under control.

**Integration of Sensors and Actuators in Embedded Systems:** The systematic process of interfacing multiple heterogeneous sensing elements and actuating devices to an Embedded Control Unit (ECU) such that real-time acquisition, signal conditioning, computation, and command delivery occur within deterministic timing, electrical, and safety constraints defined by automotive standards (ISO 26262, ISO 11452, AEC-Q100).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In an Automotive Embedded System, the **sensor-actuator-ECU triad** forms a closed feedback loop. The ECU acts as the decision-making core, sensors as the *nervous system* (input), and actuators as the *muscular system* (output).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a human body:

- **Eyes, ears, skin (Sensors)** → detect light, sound, temperature.
- **Nerves (Signal conditioning + wiring harness)** → carry weak signals to the brain.
- **Brain (ECU / Microcontroller)** → processes and decides.
- **Muscles (Actuators)** → execute the decision (move hand, brake the leg).

If any of these connections is broken or slow, the body fails. Likewise, in a car, if the crankshaft position sensor is noisy, the fuel injector may fire at the wrong time — the engine misfires. Integration ensures the *signal path* is clean, *timing* is right, and the *actuator* gets enough power when needed.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Integration

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **AEC-Q100** — Stress test qualification for automotive integrated circuits.
> - **ISO 26262** — Functional safety standard for automotive E/E systems.
> - **CAN 2.0B / CAN-FD** — Dominant automotive communication bus speeds of **500 kbps** and up to **5 Mbps**.
> - **LIN Bus** — Low-cost sub-bus running at **19.2 kbps**.
> - **Operating Temperature Range:** **−40 °C to +125 °C** (Automotive Grade 1).
> - **ADC Resolution:** commonly **10-bit (1024 steps)** or **12-bit (4096 steps)** in ECUs.

## 1.4 The Five-Layer Integration Model

The integration of sensors/actuators in an embedded automotive system can be visualized as a **5-layer model**:

| Layer | Function | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Physical Layer** | Sensor/actuator hardware | Thermistor, MAP sensor, fuel injector |
| **L2 — Signal Conditioning** | Amplification, filtering, isolation | Op-amp, RC filter, optocoupler |
| **L3 — I/O Interface** | ADC, DAC, PWM, GPIO, communication | SPI, I2C, CAN, LIN |
| **L4 — Embedded Software** | Drivers, RTOS, application logic | AUTOSAR MCAL, OSEK OS |
| **L5 — Application / Diagnostics** | Control laws, OBD-II, fault handling | Torque control, ABS algorithm |

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensor Characteristics (Operational Breakdown)

Each sensor used in an automotive embedded system is characterized by a set of static and dynamic parameters. The ECU's ADC, calibration tables, and fault-detection logic all depend on these.

### 2.1.1 Static Characteristics

1. **Range (Span):** The minimum and maximum input the sensor can measure.
   - *Why it matters:* ECU maps look-up tables (LUTs) only within the calibrated range.

2. **Sensitivity (S):** The slope of the output vs. input curve.
   - *How:* Higher sensitivity → smaller input changes are detectable, but noise also amplifies.

3. **Linearity:** How closely the transfer function follows a straight line.
   - *Why it matters:* Non-linear sensors (e.g., thermistor) require polynomial or piecewise-linear LUT compensation.

4. **Accuracy vs. Precision:**
   - *Accuracy* = closeness to true value.
   - *Precision* = repeatability of measurement.

5. **Hysteresis:** Difference in output for the same input approached from increasing vs. decreasing direction.

6. **Resolution:** Smallest detectable change in input.

### 2.1.2 Dynamic Characteristics

1. **Response Time (τ):** Time to reach 63.2% of final value (first-order system).
2. **Bandwidth (BW):** Frequency at which output power drops to **−3 dB**.
3. **Settling Time:** Time to stay within ±2% of final value.
4. **Step Response Overshoot:** Critical in position/angle sensors (e.g., throttle).

## 2.2 Actuator Characteristics

Actuators are characterized by:

- **Force/Torque:** Mechanical output capability.
- **Stroke / Angular Range:** Maximum displacement.
- **Bandwidth:** Frequency response of the actuator + driver pair.
- **Power Consumption:** Critical for ECU load budgeting.
- **Latency:** Delay between command and motion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the table below as your last-minute revision anchor. Note: all absolute values use `\vert` to remain markdown-safe.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ADC Quantization Step (LSB) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}$ | V | $n$ = ADC bits |
| 2 | ADC Quantization Error (max) | $E_q = \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2(2^{n}-1)}$ | V | Worst-case |
| 3 | Sensitivity (linear sensor) | $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta x_{in}}$ | V/unit | Slope |
| 4 | Wheatstone Bridge Output | $V_{out} = V_{ex}\!\left(\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)$ | V | At balance $V_{out}=0$ |
| 5 | Non-inverting Amplifier Gain | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | unitless | Signal conditioning |
| 6 | Inverting Amplifier Gain | $A_v = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | unitless | Phase inversion |
| 7 | RC Low-Pass Cutoff Freq. | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Hz | Anti-alias filter |
| 8 | RC High-Pass Cutoff Freq. | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Hz | DC-block |
| 9 | Thermistor (β-model) | $R_T = R_0 \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$ | Ω | $T$ in Kelvin |
| 10 | Thermocouple (Seebeck) | $V = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | V | α in µV/°C |
| 11 | Voltage Divider Output | $V_{out} = V_{in}\!\left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}\right)$ | V | Sensor biasing |
| 12 | RMS Noise (Johnson) | $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | V | Thermal noise |
| 13 | Power Dissipated by Actuator | $P = V \cdot I = \dfrac{V^2}{R} = I^2 R$ | W | Driver design |
| 14 | PWM Average Voltage | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{cc}$ | V | $D$ = duty cycle |
| 15 | Closed-Loop Error | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | unitless | Feedback |
| 16 | First-Order Step Response | $y(t) = K(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | — | 63.2% at $t = \tau$ |
| 17 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | Anti-aliasing |
| 18 | Strain Gauge Factor | $GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | unitless | $\varepsilon$ = strain |
| 19 | Hall Sensor Output (linear) | $V_H = S_H \cdot B$ | V | $B$ = flux density |
| 20 | Actuator Torque (DC motor) | $\tau_m = K_t \cdot I_a$ | N·m | $K_t$ = torque const. |

## 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Real Engineering

- **Equation 1–2:** Determines whether your ADC can resolve a 10 mV change from a 5 V sensor.
- **Equation 4:** Core of all pressure/strain/load-cell measurement in cars (e.g., pedal force sensor).
- **Equation 7:** You *must* choose R, C such that $f_c$ is below half the sampling rate — otherwise aliasing corrupts the data.
- **Equation 9:** Cold-start fuel calculations in ECU use this for engine coolant temperature (ECT) sensor.
- **Equation 14:** Drive-by-wire throttle, EGR valve, fuel pump speed — all use PWM.
- **Equation 20:** Window lift motor, electronic throttle body, ABS pump motor sizing.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No result is stated without the prior transition. This is the format expected in KTU 14-mark derivations.

## 3.1 Derivation — ADC Quantization Step and Error

**Problem:** An ECU uses a **12-bit ADC** with reference voltage $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V. Find the (a) quantization step, (b) worst-case quantization error, and (c) the smallest temperature change detectable if the sensor sensitivity is **10 mV/°C**.

### Step 1: Number of Discrete Codes
For an $n$-bit ADC, the total number of output codes is $2^n$.

$$N = 2^{n} = 2^{12} = 4096$$

This means the input range is divided into 4096 discrete steps.

### Step 2: Quantization Step (LSB Voltage)
The LSB voltage (smallest distinguishable input change) is the full reference divided by the number of intervals.

$$\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}$$

Substituting $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V and $n = 12$:

$$\Delta V = \dfrac{5.0}{4096 - 1} = \dfrac{5.0}{4095} \approx 1.221 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$$

$$\Delta V \approx 1.221 \text{ mV per LSB}$$

### Step 3: Worst-Case Quantization Error
The actual input can fall anywhere inside one LSB interval. The maximum deviation of the *quantized* value from the *true* value is half an LSB.

$$E_q = \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2(2^{n} - 1)}$$

Plugging numbers:

$$E_q = \dfrac{5.0}{2 \times 4095} = \dfrac{5.0}{8190} \approx 6.105 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$$

$$E_q \approx 0.6105 \text{ mV}$$

### Step 4: Convert Error to Temperature
Sensor sensitivity is $S = 10$ mV/°C. So:

$$\Delta T_{min} = \dfrac{E_q}{S} = \dfrac{0.6105 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}}$$

$$\Delta T_{min} = 0.06105 \text{ °C}$$

**Conclusion:** This 12-bit ADC can resolve temperature changes as small as **≈ 0.061 °C** with this sensor — well within ECU calibration requirements.

## 3.2 Derivation — RC Low-Pass Anti-Alias Filter Cutoff

**Problem:** A wheel-speed sensor produces a signal with maximum meaningful frequency **800 Hz**. The ECU samples at **2 kHz**. Design a first-order RC low-pass filter.

### Step 1: Apply Nyquist Criterion
To avoid aliasing, the filter cutoff $f_c$ must be ≤ $f_s/2$ (Nyquist limit).

$$f_{Nyquist} = \dfrac{f_s}{2} = \dfrac{2000}{2} = 1000 \text{ Hz}$$

Choose $f_c = 1000$ Hz (a safe design value).

### Step 2: Apply the Cutoff Formula

$$f_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$$

Solving for the RC product:

$$R C = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f_c} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 1000}$$

$$R C = \dfrac{1}{6283.19} \approx 1.5915 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

### Step 3: Choose a Standard Capacitor and Compute Resistor
Choose $C = 0.1 \mu F = 1 \times 10^{-7}$ F (a common, low-leakage value).

$$R = \dfrac{1.5915 \times 10^{-4}}{1 \times 10^{-7}} = 1591.5 \ \Omega$$

Use a standard **1.6 kΩ** resistor.

### Step 4: Verify
With $R = 1.6$ kΩ and $C = 0.1$ μF:

$$f_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 1600 \times 1 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 995 \text{ Hz}$$

**Conclusion:** Signal components above 1 kHz are attenuated at **−20 dB/decade**, safely suppressing aliasing.

## 3.3 Derivation — Sensor Sensitivity from Two-Point Calibration

**Problem:** An MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure) sensor outputs **0.5 V at 20 kPa** and **4.5 V at 100 kPa**. Find sensitivity and the transfer function.

### Step 1: Apply the Linear Sensitivity Definition
Sensitivity is the slope of output vs. input.

$$S = \dfrac{V_{out2} - V_{out1}}{P_2 - P_1}$$

Substituting:

$$S = \dfrac{4.5 - 0.5}{100 - 20} = \dfrac{4.0}{80} = 0.05 \text{ V/kPa}$$

### Step 2: Write the Linear Transfer Function
A straight line in slope-intercept form:

$$V_{out} = S \cdot P + b$$

where $b$ is the y-intercept (output at $P = 0$).

Using the first point:

$$0.5 = (0.05)(20) + b$$

$$0.5 = 1.0 + b \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = -0.5 \text{ V}$$

### Step 3: Final Transfer Function

$$V_{out} = 0.05 \cdot P - 0.5$$

**Check:** At $P = 100$ kPa → $V_{out} = 5 - 0.5 = 4.5$ V ✓
At $P = 20$ kPa → $V_{out} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$ V ✓

**Conclusion:** The ECU software uses the inverse $P = (V_{out} + 0.5)/0.05 = 20(V_{out} + 0.5)$ to recover pressure in kPa.

## 3.4 Derivation — PWM Average Voltage for Solenoid Driver

**Problem:** An EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) solenoid is driven with $V_{cc} = 12$ V at 100 Hz with duty cycle $D = 35\%$. Find the average voltage seen by the solenoid coil.

### Step 1: PWM Period

$$T = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{100} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 2: ON-Time

$$T_{on} = D \cdot T = 0.35 \times 10 = 3.5 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 3: Average Voltage

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{cc} = 0.35 \times 12 = 4.2 \text{ V}$$

**Conclusion:** The solenoid receives an effective **4.2 V DC** equivalent, allowing precise control of the EGR valve opening.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity Computed | Result | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| ADC LSB @ 12-bit, 5 V | 1.221 mV | Resolution check |
| Quantization error | 0.6105 mV | Worst-case design margin |
| Min detectable ΔT | 0.061 °C | ECT sensor design |
| Anti-alias $f_c$ | 995 Hz | Wheel-speed input |
| MAP sensitivity | 50 mV/kPa | Engine load mapping |
| PWM EGR average | 4.2 V | Solenoid drive law |

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram — Sensor–ECU–Actuator Integration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[PHYSICAL DOMAIN] --> nodeB[SENSORS]
    nodeB --> nodeC[SIGNAL CONDITIONING]

```

**Reading the diagram:** The signal flows from the physical measurand (e.g., engine RPM) → sensor (magnetic pickup) → signal conditioning (amplifier + filter) → ECU input (timer capture) → computation (RPM calculation + control law) → ECU output (PWM module) → driver H-bridge → actuator (DC motor) → physical action → feedback closes the loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

```

**Reading the diagram:** The plant output is measured by a sensor with transfer function $H(s)$. The error $E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot Y(s)$ is fed into the controller $C(s)$. The control law is most often a **PID controller** in automotive ECUs (engine idle speed, ABS wheel slip, electronic stability).

**Reading the diagram:** The crankshaft position sensor (CKP) is the *master timing reference* for the ECU. Based on tooth count, the ECU schedules injector pulse width, spark timing, and variable valve timing — all within sub-millisecond precision.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
Differentiate between a *sensor* and a *transducer*. List four static characteristics of a sensor used in an automotive ECU.

**Model Answer:**

A **transducer** is a general device that converts energy from one form to another. A **sensor** is a *specific type* of transducer whose output is an electrical signal proportional to a physical measurand. Every sensor is a transducer, but not every transducer is a sensor (e.g., an actuator is also a transducer, but it converts *electrical → mechanical*).

**Four static characteristics:**
1. **Range / Span** — the input interval over which the sensor operates.
2. **Sensitivity** — slope $S = \Delta V_{out}/\Delta x_{in}$.
3. **Linearity** — deviation from a best-fit straight line.
4. **Hysteresis** — output difference for same input on increasing vs. decreasing sweep.

(Mention also accuracy, precision, resolution for full 3-mark depth.)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
What is *signal conditioning*? Why is it essential in the integration of sensors with a microcontroller-based ECU?

**Model Answer:**

**Signal conditioning** is the set of analog/digital processing steps applied between the raw sensor output and the microcontroller's input pin to make the signal usable. The standard chain includes **amplification, filtering, isolation, linearization, and level-shifting**.

**Why it is essential:**
1. Sensor outputs are typically **low-amplitude** (mV level) — microcontrollers need 0–5 V or 0–3.3 V at the ADC input, so **amplification** is mandatory.
2. Sensors pick up **electromagnetic interference (EMI)** from ignition, motors, and other actuators — **filtering** removes noise.
3. Sensors may have **non-linear** characteristics — **linearization** (via LUT or polynomial) restores accuracy.
4. Automotive ECUs require **electrical isolation** between high-voltage actuators and low-voltage sensor front-end to prevent damage.
5. Protection against **over-voltage, reverse polarity, ESD** is needed for AEC-Q100 compliance.

Without signal conditioning, the ADC would see noisy, low-amplitude, possibly out-of-range voltages — leading to wrong control decisions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question below is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, matching KTU 2024 ESE pattern. Two independent alternatives are provided as the official internal choice.

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Set A]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Sensor Characteristics and Interfacing
Explain the static and dynamic characteristics of an automotive sensor. With a neat block diagram, describe the integration of a **MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure) sensor** with an ECU, including signal conditioning.

**Model Solution:**

**Static Characteristics** (definition + automotive meaning):
- **Range:** MAP sensor typically 10 kPa to 115 kPa (vacuum to atmospheric).
- **Sensitivity:** e.g., 45 mV/kPa.
- **Linearity:** Pressure-to-voltage curve is approximately linear; ECU uses linear interpolation.
- **Hysteresis:** < 0.5% FS.
- **Accuracy:** ±1% FS.
- **Resolution:** 10-bit ADC gives 1.22 mV → 0.027 kPa resolution.

**Dynamic Characteristics:**
- **Response time:** < 1 ms for MEMS MAP sensors.
- **Bandwidth:** ~1 kHz (sufficient for engine cycles up to 8000 rpm × 4 strokes = 533 Hz).

**Block Diagram (use Section 4.3 chain):**
MAP sensor → protection (TVS diode) → instrumentation amplifier (gain ~10) → RC low-pass filter ($f_c$ ≈ 500 Hz) → level shifter → ADC pin → digital IIR low-pass → calibration LUT → engine control application.

**Interfacing to ECU:** The amplified, filtered analog voltage is read by the ADC at a sampling rate of ≥ 1 kHz. The software applies a **moving-average filter** for noise suppression and then converts the digital value back to pressure using the inverse transfer function.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Actuator Integration
With suitable examples, classify automotive actuators and explain the role of a **PWM driver** in controlling a DC motor actuator (e.g., electronic throttle body). Derive the average voltage equation.

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Automotive Actuators:**

| Class | Example | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Electromagnetic** | Solenoid injector, relay | ON/OFF or pulse |
| **DC motor** | Throttle body, fuel pump | Continuous position/speed |
| **Stepper motor** | EGR valve, idle air control | Discrete angular steps |
| **Piezoelectric** | Common-rail injector | Fast precision actuation |
| **Thermal** | Glow plug, heated seat | Heat output |
| **Hydraulic** | ABS modulator | Brake pressure |

**PWM Driver for DC Motor:**

A PWM driver rapidly switches a power transistor (MOSFET/IGBT) at a fixed frequency $f$ with variable duty cycle $D$. The motor's mechanical inertia averages the pulsed voltage into a smooth effective DC level.

**Derivation of average voltage:** Over one PWM period $T$, the switch is ON for time $T_{on} = D \cdot T$ and OFF for $T_{off} = (1-D) \cdot T$. The average is:

$$V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T}\!\int_0^{T_{on}} V_{cc} \, dt + \dfrac{1}{T}\!\int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0 \, dt = \dfrac{V_{cc} \cdot T_{on}}{T}$$

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{cc}$$

**Numerical:** If $V_{cc} = 12$ V and $D = 0.45$, then $V_{avg} = 5.4$ V. The motor develops torque proportional to this average.

**Closed-loop position control:** The throttle position sensor (TPS) feeds the actual angle back to the ECU; the PID controller adjusts $D$ to minimize error between desired and actual throttle angle.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Set B]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Anti-Aliasing Filter and ADC Resolution
A pressure sensor outputs 0.5 V to 4.5 V over a 0–100 kPa range. It is interfaced to an ECU with a 10-bit ADC (reference 5 V) through a first-order RC low-pass filter.
1. Calculate the ADC LSB voltage and worst-case quantization error.
2. If the signal contains frequency components up to 250 Hz, design the RC filter (assume $C = 100$ nF).
3. Determine the smallest pressure change detectable.

**Model Solution:**

**1. LSB and Quantization Error**

Number of codes:

$$N = 2^{n} = 2^{10} = 1024$$

LSB step:

$$\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{N - 1} = \dfrac{5.0}{1023} \approx 4.89 \text{ mV}$$

Worst-case quantization error:

$$E_q = \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = \dfrac{4.89}{2} \approx 2.445 \text{ mV}$$

**2. RC Filter Design**

Choose cutoff $f_c$ at the Nyquist limit. The ADC is typically sampled at 1 kHz in such applications, so $f_s/2 = 500$ Hz. Choose $f_c = 250$ Hz (matches the signal bandwidth).

$$R C = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f_c} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 250} \approx 6.366 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

With $C = 100$ nF $= 1 \times 10^{-7}$ F:

$$R = \dfrac{6.366 \times 10^{-4}}{1 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 6366 \ \Omega$$

Use a standard **6.2 kΩ** resistor.

**3. Smallest Detectable Pressure Change**

Sensor sensitivity:

$$S = \dfrac{4.5 - 0.5}{100 - 0} = 0.04 \text{ V/kPa} = 40 \text{ mV/kPa}$$

Smallest detectable pressure:

$$\Delta P_{min} = \dfrac{E_q}{S} = \dfrac{2.445 \text{ mV}}{40 \text{ mV/kPa}} \approx 0.0611 \text{ kPa}$$

**Conclusion:** This 10-bit system resolves ≈ **0.061 kPa** pressure changes — adequate for MAP and boost-pressure applications.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Communication Bus Integration
Compare CAN, LIN, and FlexRay buses used to integrate sensors and actuators in an automotive network. State any one automotive standard governing such integration.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | CAN 2.0B | LIN | FlexRay |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | up to 1 Mbps | 19.2 kbps | up to 10 Mbps |
| **Topology** | Multi-master, bus | Single-master, bus | Star or bus, time-triggered |
| **Wiring** | Twisted pair | Single wire | Twisted pair (2 channels) |
| **Cost** | Medium | Very low | High |
| **Determinism** | Event-triggered (CSMA/CR) | Event-triggered | Time-triggered (TDMA) |
| **Typical Use** | Powertrain, chassis | Body electronics, mirrors | Drive-by-wire, ADAS |
| **Max Nodes** | ~30 practical | 16 | 64 |

**Standard:** **ISO 11898** governs CAN. **ISO 17987** governs LIN. **ISO 17458** governs FlexRay. **ISO 26262** is the overarching *functional safety* standard for all integration.

**Integration role:** A modern car uses a **gateway ECU** to bridge CAN, LIN, and FlexRay domains. Sensors broadcast on CAN (e.g., wheel speed), simpler sensors use LIN (e.g., window position switch), and safety-critical subsystems use FlexRay (e.g., electric power steering).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "sensor" and "transducer"** — sensors are input-side, actuators are output-side, and the umbrella term *transducer* covers both. Examiners check this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting units in formula derivations** — every numerical result (mV, kPa, °C, Hz) *must* carry its unit. Loss of 1–2 marks per question is common.
> 3. **Skipping the Nyquist argument in filter design** — examiners want you to *state* $f_c \leq f_s/2$ before plugging numbers.
> 4. **Ignoring the feedback path** — block diagrams without a closed loop lose credit on actuator questions.
> 5. **Naming buses without comparing** — listing CAN, LIN, FlexRay without a *comparison* (speed, cost, determinism) scores only 50% on that sub-part.
> 6. **Not mentioning AEC-Q100 / ISO 26262** — at least one standard reference is expected in a 7-mark answer for a high score.
> 7. **Writing code in non-CS subjects** — keep answers formulaic and conceptual; pseudocode is acceptable only when the question demands it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **Sensor** = physical → electrical; **Actuator** = electrical → physical; **Transducer** = both.
- **Static characteristics:** Range, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, accuracy, precision, resolution.
- **Dynamic characteristics:** Response time τ, bandwidth (–3 dB), settling time, overshoot.
- **Signal conditioning chain:** Protection → Amplification → Filtering → Level shifting → ADC → Digital filter → Calibration → Application.
- **Anti-aliasing rule:** $f_c \leq f_s/2$ (Nyquist). RC low-pass is the standard first-order filter.
- **ADC resolution:** $\Delta V = V_{ref}/(2^{n}-1)$. At 12-bit, 5 V → **1.22 mV** LSB.
- **PWM average:** $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{cc}$. Used for motor speed, solenoid force, LED brightness.
- **Wheatstone bridge:** Foundation of all resistive sensors (strain gauge, pressure, load cell).
- **Thermistor model:** $R_T = R_0 \exp[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)]$ — non-linear, needs LUT.
- **Automotive buses:** CAN (high speed, event), LIN (low cost, body), FlexRay/Ethernet (time-triggered, safety).
- **Standards to memorize:** ISO 26262 (safety), ISO 11898 (CAN), ISO 17987 (LIN), AEC-Q100 (IC qualification).
- **Temperature range:** Automotive Grade 1: **−40 °C to +125 °C**.
- **Closed-loop control:** Error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$, PID controller, feedback sensor.
- **Integration layers:** Physical → Signal conditioning → I/O → Embedded software → Application/diagnostics.
- **Common sensors to remember:** CKP, CMP, MAP, MAF, TPS, ECT, IAT, O2/Lambda, Knock, Wheel-speed, Yaw-rate, Steering-angle, Lidar/Radar/Camera.
- **Common actuators to remember:** Fuel injector, Spark coil, EGR valve, Electronic throttle, ABS modulator, VVT solenoid, HVAC blend door.
- **EMC/EMI awareness:** Twisted-pair wiring, shielded cables, ferrite beads, opto-isolation, common-mode chokes.
- **Key formula: Wheatstone bridge imbalance** $V_{out} = V_{ex}(R_1/(R_1+R_2) - R_4/(R_3+R_4))$.
- **Key formula: First-order step response** $y(t) = K(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ — at $t = \tau$, output reaches **63.2%** of final value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Communication Protocols: Introduction and Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT632 - AUTOMOTIVE EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Communication Protocols: Introduction and Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vehicle Communication Protocols** are formally defined as the standardized set of **rules, electrical specifications, timing constraints, message formats, and arbitration mechanisms** that govern the reliable, deterministic, and real-time exchange of digital data between Electronic Control Units (ECUs), sensors, actuators, and external infrastructure within modern automotive and off-road vehicular systems.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this domain is treated as the **communication backbone** of the *Automotive Embedded System Stack*. It bridges the physical wiring layer (the **Physical Layer** of the OSI model) up to the **Application Layer**, ensuring that dozens of microcontrollers distributed across the vehicle can share sensor data, coordinate actuator responses, and execute safety-critical functions within microsecond-level deadlines.

### Key Protocol Families Studied in Module 3

| Protocol | Primary Domain | Typical Data Rate |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CAN (Controller Area Network)** | Powertrain, Chassis, Body | **125 kbps – 1 Mbps** |
| **LIN (Local Interconnect Network)** | Low-cost body electronics | **1 kbps – 20 kbps** |
| **FlexRay** | X-by-Wire (Brake, Steer) | **up to 10 Mbps** |
| **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1)** | ADAS, Infotainment, Backbone | **100 Mbps – 1 Gbps** |
| **CAN FD** | Next-gen powertrain | **up to 5 Mbps** |
| **MOST** | Infotainment, Multimedia | **up to 150 Mbps** |
| **V2X (DSRC / C-V2X)** | External infrastructure comms | **up to 27 Mbps** |
