   - **Data Flash / EEPROM:** For adaptive learn values (long-term fuel trim, idle air).

3. **Analog Front-End (AFE):** A **12-bit, 1 MSPS** multiplexed ADC for reading sensor signals (MAP, IAT, TPS, O2 sensors). Signal conditioning includes anti-aliasing filters and pull-up/pull-down biasing.

4. **PWM Generation Units:** Hardware timers generating **variable-frequency PWM** for injector drive (typically **10–20 Hz** bipolar), ignition dwell, electronic throttle motor, and transmission solenoids.

5. **Communication Controllers:** Hardware CAN 2.0B / CAN FD MACs supporting **500 kbps** on powertrain CAN, plus LIN for sub-bus devices.

6. **Safety Island:** Independent watchdog window timer (**3 ms** typical), voltage monitor, and clock monitor. These reset the MCU if firmware hangs or operates out of spec.

7. **Power Supply:** Reverse-battery protection, **5V/3.3V LDOs**, and crank-survive hold-up capacitors to tolerate supply drops down to **6V for 100 ms** during engine cranking.

> A clean Mermaid block diagram is mandatory in the answer (similar to Section 4.1 of these notes).

---

**(b)** Explain the **real-time task scheduling architecture** of a typical PCM firmware. Discuss the role of the **crank-angle synchronous fast loop**, the **10 ms synchronous loop**, and the **100 ms asynchronous loop** in engine and transmission control. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The PCM firmware runs under a **preemptive priority-based RTOS** (AUTOSAR Classic OS or OSEK/VDX). Tasks are categorized by their trigger source and deadline:

**1. Crank-Angle Synchronous Fast Loop (100–200 μs period):**
- Triggered by the **CKP sensor** every **6°** of crankshaft rotation.
- Executes **injector pulse-width calculation**, **ignition spark advance**, and **misfire detection**.
- Highest priority — must preempt any other task.
- At **6000 RPM**, fires every **~166 μs**.

**2. Synchronous Slow Loop (10 ms period):**
- Triggered by a hardware timer interrupt.
- Handles **closed-loop fuel trim** (based on oxygen sensor feedback), **idle speed control**, **long-term fuel adaptation**, and **electronic throttle body position PID**.

**3. Asynchronous Background Loop (100 ms period):**
- Triggered by a low-priority timer.
- Runs **OBD-II readiness checks**, **diagnostic trouble code (DTC) management**, **CAN network management**, and **adaptive learn updates**.

**4. Communication Task (Event-Driven):**
- Triggered on CAN frame reception or transmission completion.
- Handles inter-ECU messaging with **ABS, ESP, BCM, ADAS**.

The scheduling guarantee is proven using **Rate Monotonic Analysis (RMA)**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

For a 4-task system, the bound is **~75.7% CPU utilization**. PCMs typically operate below **60% utilization** to leave headroom for transient load and interrupt latency.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**(a)** Describe the **closed-loop torque-based control architecture** of a modern PCM. How does the central torque arbiter coordinate engine torque and transmission interventions? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Traditional ECUs controlled fuel and spark independently. Modern PCMs use a **centralized torque-based architecture** where *torque is the unified currency* across all subsystems.

**The Architecture Has Three Layers:**

1. **Torque Demand Layer:** Computes the driver's requested torque from pedal position ($\theta_{pedal}$), vehicle speed ($v$), current gear, and drive mode.

   $$\tau_{req} = K_{pedal} \cdot \theta_{pedal} + \tau_{creep} + \tau_{ASR\ req} + \tau_{ESC\ req}$$

2. **Central Torque Arbiter Layer:** The PCM receives **torque interventions** from:
   - **Traction control (TCS):** Requests torque reduction during wheel slip.
   - **Electronic stability control (ESC):** Requests asymmetric torque for yaw correction.
   - **Transmission (TCU):** Requests torque reduction during gear shifts (for shift quality).
   - **Cruise control / Adaptive cruise:** Requests steady-state torque maintenance.
   - **Idle speed control:** Requests minimum torque to prevent stalling.

   The arbiter sums all requests and computes the **final torque to deliver**:

   $$\tau_{final} = \tau_{req} - \sum \tau_{intervention,i}$$

3. **Actuator Allocation Layer:** The PCM distributes $\tau_{final}$ across:
   - **Throttle plate angle** (primary — slow response).
   - **Spark advance retard** (fast — ~50 ms).
   - **Fuel cut** (very fast — ~10 ms).
   - **Transmission torque converter slip** (for dampening).

This architecture eliminates the *fighting* that occurs between subsystems in older architectures and is the foundation for modern **hybrid powertrains** where the e-motor and engine must coordinate seamlessly.

---

**(b)** Discuss the **diagnostic and functional safety features** of a PCM compliant with **OBD-II (SAE J1979)** and **ISO 26262 ASIL-D**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**OBD-II Diagnostics (SAE J1979):**

The PCM monitors dozens of **continuous and non-continuous** diagnostics:

- **Misfire detection** (via crankshaft instantaneous speed variation).
- **Catalyst efficiency monitoring** (front + rear O2 sensor switching correlation).
- **Evaporative emissions system** leak detection.
- **Oxygen sensor response time** monitoring.
- **Comprehensive component monitor** for sensors/actuators.

When a fault is detected, the PCM:
1. Sets a **Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC)** — e.g., P0301 (Cylinder 1 Misfire).
2. Stores a **freeze frame** of sensor values at the moment of fault.
3. Illuminates the **Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL)**.
4. Logs the DTC in **non-volatile memory** for retrieval by a scan tool.

**ISO 26262 ASIL-D Functional Safety:**

For powertrain control, ISO 26262 mandates **ASIL-D** (highest automotive integrity level). The PCM implements:

1. **Lockstep dual-core MCU** — Two cores execute the same instruction stream; a hardware comparator flags divergence within **~10 ns**.
2. **Windowed watchdog timer** — Firmware must service the watchdog within a precise time window; a late or early service triggers MCU reset.
3. **Voltage and clock monitoring** — Brown-out detector and PLL lock monitor reset the MCU on power/clock anomalies.
4. **Memory protection** — MPU regions prevent stack overflow or unauthorized writes to safety-critical RAM.
5. **End-of-line (EOL) testing** — Each PCM unit is functionally tested for **~30 seconds** across voltage and temperature extremes before delivery.
6. **Safe-state default** — On unrecoverable fault, the PCM defaults to **limp-home mode** (limited RPM, fixed gear) and remains drivable to a service station.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PCM with ECU:** The PCM is an *integrated* controller. Simply listing ECU functions gets only partial credit.
> 2. **Ignoring the real-time aspect:** Many answers describe what the PCM does but fail to mention **deterministic scheduling, deadlines, and ASIL-D**.
> 3. **No mention of torque coordination:** Module 4 specifically emphasizes the *integrated* nature of the PCM. Answering only engine or only transmission control loses marks.
> 4. **Skipping OBD-II:** OBD-II compliance is a KTU syllabus mandate — omitting it costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Missing safety vocabulary:** Examiners look for terms like **lockstep, watchdog, ASIL-D, safe-state, ISO 26262**. Use them explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Powertrain Control Module (PCM)**

- [x] **PCM = Integrated ECU + TCU.** Not the same as a standalone ECU.
- [x] Operates on a **lockstep dual-core MCU** clocked at **150–300 MHz**.
- [x] Flash memory **4–8 MB**, SRAM **~1 MB**.
- [x] **Crank-angle synchronous fast loop** fires every **6°** (~143 μs at redline).
- [x] **Liu & Layland bound** governs RTOS schedulability: $U_{max} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.
- [x] **Torque-based architecture**: pedal input → torque demand → central arbiter → actuator allocation.
- [x] Speed-density injection formula: $\tau_{inj} = \frac{P_{man} V_d \eta_{vol}}{R_{air} T_{man} \dot{m}_{inj} AFR \lambda}$.
- [x] OBD-II (SAE J1979) — DTCs, freeze frames, MIL.
- [x] Functional safety: **ISO 26262 ASIL-D**, windowed watchdog, brown-out detector.
- [x] Communication: **CAN HS @ 500 kbps**, CAN FD, LIN, optional FlexRay/Ethernet.
- [x] Crank-survive: PCM must operate at **6V for 100 ms** during cranking.
- [x] Default safe state: **limp-home mode** on unrecoverable fault.
- [x] Battery voltage correction factor $k_{bat}$ is mandatory for accurate injector pulse-width.
- [x] The PCM sits at the top of the **powertrain CAN bus** as master node.
- [x] Modern PCMs use **AUTOSAR Classic** architecture for software layering.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engine Control Module (ECM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT632 - AUTOMOTIVE EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Engine Control Module (ECM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Engine Control Module (ECM) — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Engine Control Module (ECM)** — also called the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** — is the central, real-time embedded electronic controller in a modern Spark-Ignition (SI) or Compression-Ignition (CI) powertrain. It is a **safety-critical, hard-real-time embedded system** built around a deterministic microcontroller (typically an **Infineon AURIX TC3xx**, **NXP MPC5xx/MPC57xx**, or **Renesas RH850** family device) running under an **OSEK/VDX / AUTOSAR Classic Platform** real-time operating system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Standard Wording):**  
> The ECM is an embedded electronic control unit that continuously samples multiple engine and vehicle sensors, executes closed-loop control algorithms at sub-millisecond latency, and drives actuators (fuel injectors, ignition coils, throttle, EGR, VVT) to deliver the commanded **torque**, **fuel economy**, **emissions**, and **driveability** targets across all operating regimes.

The ECM is the **brain of the engine management system (EMS)**. It implements three classical control loops simultaneously:
1. **Air-charge estimation** (MAF / MAP / volumetric efficiency based)
2. **Fuel injection metering** (port or direct injection)
3. **Ignition timing control** (spark advance / dwell)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain-English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The ECM as an Orchestra Conductor 🎼**

Think of your engine as an **orchestra of 200+ instruments** (injectors, spark plugs, valves, pumps, throttle, EGR). Without a conductor, every instrument plays at the wrong time and the result is noise — the engine misfires, stalls, or pollutes.  

The **ECM is the conductor standing on the podium**.  
- Its **eyes** = sensors (crankshaft position, air mass, oxygen, coolant, knock, pedal position).  
- Its **ears** = feedback signals (closed-loop lambda sensor, idle speed sensor).  
- Its **baton** = the real-time scheduler and control algorithms.  
- Its **musicians** = actuators (injectors, coils, throttle, VVT).  
- Its **sheet music** = the **calibration maps / lookup tables** burned into Flash memory.  

Just as a conductor must respond to the audience's reactions within milliseconds, the ECM must compute and actuate **within one crankshaft revolution (≤ 12 ms @ 5000 rpm)** or the engine will misfire.

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Performance Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

| Metric | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Operating voltage | **9 V – 16 V** (nominal **13.5 V** at 28°C) |
| Cranking survival voltage | **6 V (minimum)** |
| Operating temperature | **−40 °C to +125 °C** (under-hood) |
| CPU clock | **100 MHz – 300 MHz** (multi-core in modern ECUs) |
| Crank-cycle period @ 6000 rpm | **10 ms** |
| Tooth-period (60-2 wheel) @ 6000 rpm | **~833 µs** |
| Fuel injection resolution | **< 1 µs** (sub-millisecond scheduling) |
| Ignition dwell accuracy | **± 0.1°** crank angle |
| ADC resolution for analog sensors | **10-bit to 12-bit** |
| Communication buses | **CAN 2.0B / CAN FD / LIN / FlexRay** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Decomposition of the ECM

The ECM performs five tightly coupled tasks, all executed inside a deterministic cyclic scheduler:

### Task-1: Crankshaft Synchronisation & Engine Speed Tracking
- Reads the **CKP (Crankshaft Position)** sensor (typically a variable-reluctance or Hall-effect pickup on a **60-minus-2 tooth wheel**).
- Determines **instantaneous engine speed $N$ [rpm]** and **absolute crank angle $\theta$ [°]**.
- Triggered by a hardware timer capture (no software polling).

> **Why?** Without sub-degree crank-angle resolution, fuel injection and spark timing cannot be scheduled accurately.

### Task-2: Air-Charge / Load Calculation
Two standard strategies exist:
- **Speed-Density Method:**  
$$ m_a = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot P_{im} \cdot N}{2 \cdot R \cdot T_{im}} $$
where $V_d$ = displacement, $P_{im}$ = intake-manifold absolute pressure (MAP), $T_{im}$ = intake-manifold temperature (IAT), $\eta_{vol}$ = volumetric efficiency (lookup), $R$ = specific gas constant of air.

- **Mass Air-Flow (MAF) Method:**  
$$ m_a = \text{MAF sensor reading} $$ (direct).

### Task-3: Fuel Injection Control (Stoichiometric Target)
Target air-fuel ratio for a 3-way catalyst is the **stoichiometric value**:
$$ \left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = 14.7 \text{ (gasoline)} = 14.67 \text{ exact} $$

The ECM injects fuel to satisfy:
$$ m_f = \frac{m_a}{(\lambda \cdot A/F)_{stoich}} $$

**Effective injection pulse width** (the famous "fuel equation"):
$$ t_{inj} = \frac{m_f \cdot C}{Q_{inj}} $$

where $C$ = compensating factors (battery voltage, temperature, transient enrichment), $Q_{inj}$ = injector static flow rate [g/s].

### Task-4: Ignition Timing Control
The commanded **spark advance** is taken from a 2-D map:
$$ \theta_{spark} = f(N, m_a \text{ or } MAP) $$

Knock control then **retards** $\theta_{spark}$ in real-time when knock is detected by the **piezoelectric knock sensor**.

### Task-5: Closed-Loop Lambda Control
A **PI controller** drives the wide-band O₂ sensor (UEGO / LSU 4.9) toward $\lambda = 1.000 \pm 0.01$:
$$ \lambda(t) \;\to\; \lambda_{target} = 1.00 $$

$$ u(t) = K_P \cdot e(t) + K_I \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau $$

where $e(t) = \lambda_{target} - \lambda_{measured}$ and the integral action removes steady-state error.

## 2.2 Architecture of a Typical ECM

The ECM hardware is a **layered, fault-tolerant** structure:

1. **Power Supply Stage** — buck/boost + reverse-battery + load-dump protection (qualified to **ISO 7637-2** and **ISO 16750-2**).
2. **Microcontroller Core** — typically **dual-core lockstep** (e.g., Aurix TC3xx TriCore) for **ASIL-D** functional safety.
3. **Memory Subsystem** — internal Flash (calibration maps + program), internal SRAM (working data), EEPROM/Emulated EEPROM (adaptive learns).
4. **Analog Front-End (AFE)** — precision bias, protection, anti-aliasing filters for MAP, MAF, TPS, ECT, IAT, O₂.
5. **Digital I/O** — crankshaft/camshaft capture timers, low-side / high-side drivers for injectors and coils.
6. **Communication Transceivers** — CAN (ISO 11898), LIN, optional FlexRay or 100BASE-T1 Ethernet.
7. **Watchdog & BIST** — window watchdog + built-in self-test.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbol / Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = \frac{60}{T_{rev}}$ | Engine speed | rpm |
| 2 | $m_a = \frac{\eta_{vol}\cdot V_d \cdot P_{im}\cdot N}{2 R T_{im}}$ | Air-mass (speed-density) | kg/s |
| 3 | $(A/F)_{stoich} = 14.67$ | Stoichiometric ratio (gasoline) | — |
| 4 | $m_f = \dfrac{m_a}{\lambda \cdot (A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Fuel mass target | kg/s |
| 5 | $t_{inj} = \dfrac{m_f}{Q_{inj}/\rho_f}$ | Injector pulse width | s |
| 6 | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Relative air-fuel ratio | — |
| 7 | $u(t) = K_P e(t) + K_I \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau$ | PI lambda controller | — |
| 8 | $\theta_{spark}^{*} = f(N,\,MAP)$ | Spark advance map | ° crank |
| 9 | $\Delta\theta_{knock} = -K_{ret}\cdot \text{PIEZO}_{rms}$ | Knock-based retard | ° crank |
| 10 | $f_{sample} \geq \dfrac{N \cdot C_{teeth}}{60}$ | Min. crank-event sample rate | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never write `\vert x \vert` inside a markdown table cell — it silently breaks the table renderer. Use the **$\vert$ or $\mid$ LaTeX** delimiter inside math mode.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ECM is the foundation of every modern compliance regime:
- **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** / **Euro 6** emissions → needs sub-percent closed-loop λ accuracy.
- **Corporate Average Fuel Economy (CAFE)** → needs calibrated $\eta_{vol}$ maps and adaptive fuel trims.
- **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II)** → the ECM hosts the diagnostic trouble codes (P0xxx, P1xxx) mandated by **SAE J2012 / ISO 15031**.
- **Functional Safety (ISO 26262)** → torque-path ECM must achieve **ASIL-D** through dual-core lockstep, ECC memory, and window watchdog.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Speed-Density Air-Mass Equation

**Start with the ideal gas law** for the intake manifold acting as a control volume:
$$ P_{im} \cdot V_m = m_{im} \cdot R \cdot T_{im} $$

Differentiating with respect to time gives the **ideal-gas mass-flow derivative**:
$$ \frac{dm_{im}}{dt} = \frac{V_m}{R \cdot T_{im}} \cdot \frac{dP_{im}}{dt} + \frac{P_{im} \cdot V_m}{R} \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_{im}}\right) $$

**Assumption 1 (engineering simplification accepted on the KTU board):** Intake-manifold temperature dynamics are slow compared to pressure dynamics (water-cooled wall). Therefore $\frac{d}{dt}(1/T_{im}) \approx 0$.

Applying assumption 1, the derivative collapses to:
$$ \frac{dm_{im}}{dt} = \frac{V_m}{R \cdot T_{im}} \cdot \frac{dP_{im}}{dt} $$

The **volumetric efficiency** $\eta_{vol}$ is defined as the actual mass inducted divided by the theoretical (displacement) mass at $\rho_{atm}$:
$$ \eta_{vol} = \frac{m_{a,\,actual}}{\rho_{atm} \cdot V_d / 2} $$

Per revolution the inducted mass is therefore:
$$ m_{a,\,per\_rev} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \frac{V_d}{2} \cdot \frac{P_{im}}{R \cdot T_{im}} $$

Multiplying by $N$ revolutions per second ($N$ in rpm ⇒ divide by 60) gives the **speed-density air-mass flow**:
$$ m_a = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot P_{im} \cdot N}{2 \cdot R \cdot T_{im}} $$

**This is the canonical equation** the KTU examiner expects in derivations.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Fuel-Injection Pulse Width

Start from the requirement that the **relative air-fuel ratio equals unity (stoichiometric)**:
$$ \lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} = 1 $$

Solving for fuel mass flow:
$$ m_f = \frac{m_a}{(A/F)_{stoich}} $$

Now relate $m_f$ to the injector flow. A saturated injector delivers a **constant mass flow rate** $Q_{inj}$ [kg/s] (after the mechanical dead-time $\tau_{d}$). For an injection pulse of duration $t_{inj}$:
$$ m_{injected} = Q_{inj} \cdot (t_{inj} - \tau_{d}) $$

Equating:
$$ Q_{inj} \cdot (t_{inj} - \tau_{d}) = \frac{m_a}{(A/F)_{stoich}} $$

Solving explicitly for $t_{inj}$:
$$ t_{inj} = \tau_{d} + \frac{m_a}{Q_{inj} \cdot (A/F)_{stoich}} $$

Plugging the speed-density form of $m_a$:
$$ t_{inj} = \tau_{d} + \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot P_{im} \cdot N}{2 \cdot R \cdot T_{im} \cdot Q_{inj} \cdot (A/F)_{stoich}} $$

**Text-row commentary (each step):**
- Step 1: Injector delivers constant $Q_{inj}$ once the solenoid is fully open.
- Step 2: Subtracting mechanical dead-time $\tau_d$ gives the effective fuel delivery.
- Step 3: Stoichiometric constraint sets the target fuel mass.
- Step 4: Substituting the air-mass expression yields a real-time computable control law.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Lambda PI Update

The closed-loop λ controller adjusts a **fuel correction factor** $\mu$ each event:

$$ \mu_{k+1} = \mu_{k} + \Delta \mu_{k} $$

For a **PI digital controller** with sampling period $T_s$:
$$ \Delta \mu_{k} = K_{P}\!\left(e_{k} - e_{k-1}\right) + K_{I}\,T_s\,e_{k} $$

where $e_{k} = \lambda_{target} - \lambda_{measured,\,k}$.

**Velocity (incremental) form** — preferred on KTU because it prevents integrator wind-up:
$$ \mu_{k+1} = \mu_{k} + K_{P}\!\left(e_{k} - e_{k-1}\right) + K_{I}\,T_s\,e_{k} $$

**Anti-windup clamp:**
$$ \mu_{min} \le \mu_{k} \le \mu_{max} \quad\quad \text{typically} \quad \mu \in [0.7,\,1.3] $$

**Tuning rule of thumb (Ziegler-Nichols for lambda):**
$$ K_{P} = 0.5\,K_{u}, \qquad K_{I} = \dfrac{K_{P}\,T_{u}}{0.83} $$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Sensors → Algorithms → Actuators

| Sensor Input | Quantity Measured | Algorithm Triggered | Actuator Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| CKP | Crank angle & speed | Synchronisation, event scheduling | All timed actuators |
| CMP | Cylinder identification | Sequential injection phasing | Injector pairing |
| MAP | Manifold absolute pressure | Speed-density load calc. | Injector pulse width |
| MAF | Mass air flow | Direct air-mass metering | Injector pulse width |
| TPS | Throttle position | Transient fuel enrichment, idle drop | Throttle body / IAC |
| ECT | Coolant temp. | Warm-up & cold-start enrichment | Injector, IAC, fan |
| IAT | Intake air temp. | Density correction, knock limit | Spark retard, injector |
| UEGO (O₂) | Lambda | Closed-loop PI control | Long-term fuel trim $\mu$ |
| Knock sensor | Vibration RMS | Spark retard | Ignition coil dwell |
| Vehicle speed | Road speed | Idle / fuel-cut logic | Injector cut, IAC |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ECM Hardware Block-Level Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PWR[Power Supply and Protection]
        p1[Battery 12V] --> p2[Reverse and Load Dump ISO 7637]
        p2 --> p3[5V and 3.3V LDO Regulators]
    end

    subgraph MCU[Microcontroller Core]
        m1[Dual Core Lockstep CPU]
        m2[Flash and ECC SRAM]
        m3[Hardware Capture Timer for CKP]
        m4[12 bit ADC Bank]
        m5[CAN and LIN Controllers]
    end

    subgraph AFE[Analog and Digital Front End]
        a1[MAP Sensor]
        a2[MAF Sensor]
        a3[TPS Potentiometer]
        a4[ECT and IAT NTC]
        a5[UEGO Lambda Probe]
        a6[Knock Piezo Sensor]
        a7[CKP and CMP Hall Pickups]
    end

    subgraph ACT[Actuator Drivers]
        d1[Injector Low Side Drivers]
        d2[Ignition Coil IGBT Drivers]
        d3[Throttle Motor H Bridge]
        d4[EGR and VVT Solenoid Drivers]
        d5[Fuel Pump Relay]
    end

    subgraph NET[Vehicle Network]
        n1[CAN 2.0B and CAN FD Bus]
        n2[LIN Subbus for Sensors]
        n3[Diagnostic K Line or CAN]
    end

    PWR --> MCU
    AFE --> MCU
    MCU --> ACT
    MCU --> NET
    NET --> TCM[Transmission Control Module]
    NET --> BCM[Body Control Module]
    NET --> ABS[ABS and ESC Module]
```

## 4.2 ECM Software / RTOS Layered Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Application Software: torque, lambda, spark, idle]
    L1[ASAM AUTOSAR RTE Run Time Environment]
    L2[AUTOSAR BSW: COM, MEM, ECU State]
    L3[OSEK OS: Tasks, ISRs, Alarms, Schedule Table]
    L4[MCAL: ADC, PWM, GPT, ICU, SPI, CAN, LIN Drivers]
    L5[Microcontroller Hardware and On-chip Peripherals]

    L0 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
```

## 4.3 ECM Control Loop Timing Model (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CRANK as CKP Timer ISR
    participant ECU as ECM Scheduler
    participant AIR as Air-charge Task
    participant FUEL as Fuel Task
    participant IGN as Spark Task
    participant LAMB as Lambda PI Task

    CRANK->>ECU: Tooth event at 833 µs
    ECU->>AIR: Trigger every 10 ms
    AIR->>FUEL: Air mass m_a available
    FUEL->>ECU: Injector pulse width t_inj
    ECU->>IGN: Spark advance theta_spark
    LAMBDA->>LAMB: Read UEGO every 100 ms
    LAMB-->>FUEL: Update long term trim mu
    ECU->>ECU: Window Watchdog Kick
```

## 4.4 State Machine of the ECM Operating Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init
    Init --> Cranking: Key ON and CKP detected
    Cranking --> WarmUp: Engine speed greater than 500 rpm
    WarmUp --> ClosedLoop: ECT greater than 60 °C and UEGO warm
    ClosedLoop --> OpenLoop: Hard accel or fuel cut
    OpenLoop --> ClosedLoop: Steady state and lambda sensor ready
    ClosedLoop --> Shutdown: Key OFF
    Shutdown --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Never confuse ECU with ECM** — ECU is the generic term; ECM is specifically the engine-management controller. Writing the wrong abbreviation costs at least **½ mark**.
> 2. **Never omit the integrator term** in the lambda PI controller — the KTU model answer specifically allocates **2 marks** for the $K_I \int e\,dt$ term.
> 3. **Stoichiometric A/F is 14.67, not 14.7** if the question asks for the precise value; 14.7 is the rounded form.
> 4. **Always state the engine speed range** when writing injection pulse-width expressions — the 60-2 tooth wheel gives $N \cdot 60$ events per second.
> 5. **Closed-loop vs open-loop** must be explained with the **UEGO sensor** status — without UEGO warm, the ECM cannot enter closed-loop operation.
> 6. For speed-density, you **must write $\eta_{vol}$ as a calibration lookup**, not as a constant — examiners deduct 1 mark if you treat it as a fixed value.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]  
**Define the Engine Control Module (ECM) and list its three primary closed-loop control functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Engine Control Module (ECM) is a real-time embedded electronic controller, built around a deterministic automotive-grade microcontroller, that continuously samples engine sensors and drives actuators to deliver the commanded torque, fuel economy, emissions, and driveability.

**Three primary closed-loop control functions:**
1. **Air-charge estimation** — using MAP / MAF / $\eta_{vol}$ maps.
2. **Fuel-injection metering** — closed-loop via UEGO lambda sensor (PI control).
3. **Ignition-timing control** — closed-loop via knock-sensor feedback for retard.

*(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]  
**What is meant by "stoichiometric air-fuel ratio"? State its value for gasoline and explain why the ECM targets it under closed-loop operation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio** is the chemically exact mass ratio of air to fuel at which all the carbon and hydrogen in the fuel are completely oxidised, leaving no excess $\text{O}_2$ or unburnt hydrocarbons.

- For gasoline: $(A/F)_{stoich} = \mathbf{14.67}$ (rounded to **14.7**).

**Why the ECM targets it:** At $\lambda = 1.000 \pm 0.01$, the three-way catalytic converter simultaneously oxidises CO/HC and reduces $\text{NO}_x$ with maximum conversion efficiency (≥ 98 %), thereby satisfying **BS-VI / Euro-6** emissions norms. The UEGO lambda sensor is used in a closed-loop PI controller to maintain this ratio within ±1 %.

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of the Engine Control Module (ECM)**. List the major hardware sections and state the role of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **speed-density equation for the air-mass flow** $m_a$ ingested per second, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Functional Architecture of the ECM (7 marks)**

The ECM hardware consists of the following sections:

1. **Power Supply & Protection Stage (1 mark)** — Accepts the **9 V – 16 V** battery rail, provides **reverse-battery**, **load-dump** (per **ISO 7637-2**), and **over-voltage** protection. Generates **5 V** and **3.3 V** rails for the microcontroller and analog front-end.

2. **Microcontroller Core (1 mark)** — A dual-core **lockstep** CPU (e.g., AURIX TC3xx) running up to **300 MHz**; internal **Flash** for program/calibration and **ECC SRAM** for transient data. Provides hardware capture timers for crank and camshaft.

3. **Analog Front-End (1 mark)** — Biases and anti-alias-filters analog signals from MAP, MAF, TPS, ECT, IAT, UEGO. The signals are digitised by a **12-bit ADC**.

4. **Digital I/O & Capture (1 mark)** — Hardware timers capture **CKP** and **CMP** events with sub-microsecond resolution; GPIO feeds the watchdog.

5. **Actuator Drivers (1 mark)** — Low-side drivers for injectors, **IGBT** drivers for ignition coils, **H-bridge** for throttle motor, and solenoid drivers for **EGR** and **VVT**.

6. **Communication Transceivers (1 mark)** — **CAN 2.0B / CAN FD** (ISO 11898), **LIN** (ISO 17987), optional **FlexRay** or **100BASE-T1 Ethernet** for inter-ECU networking.

7. **Watchdog & BIST (1 mark)** — A **window watchdog** ensures the software resets if the scheduler stalls. Built-In Self-Test runs on every key-on.

*Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1.*

**(b) Speed-Density Derivation (7 marks)**

Start with the ideal gas law applied to the intake manifold volume $V_m$:

$$ P_{im} \cdot V_m = m_{im} \cdot R \cdot T_{im} $$

Differentiate w.r.t. time:

$$ \frac{V_m}{R \cdot T_{im}} \cdot \frac{dP_{im}}{dt} = \frac{dm_{im}}{dt} $$

Assuming $T_{im}$ is quasi-steady (water-cooled walls), we get the rate of mass accumulation in the manifold equals the difference between in-flow and engine in-take.

The mass ingested per revolution by the engine is:

$$ m_{a,\,per\_rev} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \frac{V_d}{2} \cdot \frac{P_{im}}{R \cdot T_{im}} $$

Multiply by revolutions per second ($N$ in rpm $\Rightarrow$ divide by 60):

$$ m_a = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot P_{im} \cdot N}{2 \cdot R \cdot T_{im}} $$

**Assumptions explicitly stated:**
1. Intake-manifold temperature is quasi-steady.
2. $\eta_{vol}$ is read from a calibration lookup as a function of $(N, P_{im})$.
3. Air behaves as an ideal gas with $R = 287\ \text{J/(kg·K)}$.
4. No residual gas and no EGR contamination (or EGR is compensated separately).

*(7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **closed-loop lambda control** in a gasoline ECM using a PI controller. Derive the incremental form of the controller and state the anti-windup limits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a flowchart, describe the **operating modes (states) of the ECM** from key-on to key-off. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Closed-Loop Lambda Control — PI Incremental Form (7 marks)**

The wide-band **UEGO** sensor outputs a voltage proportional to the **lambda value** $\lambda_{measured}$. The error is:

$$ e_k = \lambda_{target} - \lambda_{measured,\,k} $$

with $\lambda_{target} = 1.000$.

A digital **PI** controller updates a fuel trim $\mu_k$ each event. The continuous PI is:

$$ u(t) = K_P e(t) + K_I \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau $$

Discretising with sampling time $T_s$ using a **backward-Euler integrator**:

$$ \mu_k = K_P e_k + K_I T_s \sum_{i=0}^{k} e_i $$

Differentiating once (i.e., subtracting $\mu_{k-1}$ from $\mu_k$) yields the **velocity / incremental form** preferred on the KTU board:

$$ \mu_k = \mu_{k-1} + K_P \bigl(e_k - e_{k-1}\bigr) + K_I T_s e_k $$

**Anti-windup clamp (1 mark):**
$$ 0.7 \le \mu_k \le 1.3 $$

The lower and upper bounds prevent the integrator from accumulating stale error during transient enrichment or fuel cut. Beyond this range, the ECM either opens the throttle more (lean limit) or cuts fuel (rich limit).

*(7 marks)*

**(b) Operating-Mode State Diagram (7 marks)**

The ECM transitions through the following states (see Section 4.4 for the Mermaid state diagram):

1. **Init (1 mark)** — Power-on self-test, RAM/ROM/ADC checks, watchdog enabled.
2. **Cranking (1 mark)** — Starter engages; CKP events trigger cranking fuel; **fixed timing**; no closed-loop.
3. **Warm-Up (2 marks)** — Engine fires; **open-loop** fueling scaled by $\text{ECT}$ enrichment table; ignition timing advanced via the **warm-up map**.
4. **Closed-Loop (1 mark)** — Activated once **ECT > 60 °C** AND UEGO sensor is **hot**; PI lambda controller engages.
5. **Open-Loop Transient (1 mark)** — During hard acceleration, fuel cut, or wide-open throttle.
6. **Shutdown (1 mark)** — Key-off; fuel pump relay de-energises after **3 s** timer; diagnostic freeze-frame written to non-volatile memory.

*(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **ECM** = the real-time embedded brain of engine management; **NOT** the same as a generic ECU.
- [ ] Three simultaneous closed loops: **air-charge**, **fuel metering**, **ignition timing**.
- [ ] **Speed-Density:** $m_a = \dfrac{\eta_{vol}\cdot V_d\cdot P_{im}\cdot N}{2 R T_{im}}$.
- [ ] **MAF** is a direct measurement; **Speed-Density** is an indirect calculation.
- [ ] Stoichiometric A/F for gasoline = **14.67** (rounded to 14.7).
- [ ] $\lambda = 1.000 \pm 0.01$ is the closed-loop target.
- [ ] Injector pulse width: $t_{inj} = \tau_d + \dfrac{m_a}{Q_{inj}\cdot (A/F)_{stoich}}$.
- [ ] Lambda PI controller in **velocity form** with **anti-windup clamp** $\mu \in [0.7, 1.3]$.
- [ ] Hardware sections: **Power, MCU, AFE, Digital I/O, Actuator Drivers, Comms, Watchdog**.
- [ ] Typical MCU: **AURIX TC3xx**, **MPC57xx**, **RH850** — all **ASIL-D capable**.
- [ ] Crank-cycle budget @ 6000 rpm ≈ **10 ms**; tooth period ≈ **833 µs** — never violate these deadlines.
- [ ] Diagnostics: **OBD-II P-codes** are stored in the ECM.
- [ ] Functional safety: **ISO 26262** ASIL-D → dual-core lockstep + ECC memory + window watchdog.
- [ ] Communication: **CAN FD** (powertrain), **LIN** (low-speed sensors / actuators).
- [ ] Operating voltage range **9–16 V**, cranking **6 V** minimum, load-dump protected per **ISO 7637-2**.
- [ ] State machine: **Init → Cranking → Warm-Up → Closed-Loop → Shutdown**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Control Module (TCM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT632 - AUTOMOTIVE EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transmission Control Module (TCM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Transmission Control Module (TCM)** is a specialized, real-time, hard-real-time **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** within the modern automotive **Powertrain Domain** that autonomously governs the electro-hydraulic / electro-mechanical actuation of an Automatic Transmission (AT), Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT), or Dual-Clutch Transmission (DCT). It executes closed-loop control algorithms over clutch pressures, torque converter lock-up, and shift solenoids to deliver optimal gear ratios in synchronization with the Engine Control Module (ECM) over the **CAN (Controller Area Network)** bus.

In strict NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education language, the TCM is an *embedded cyber-physical system* that maps the driver's intent (throttle position, brake pedal, gear selector position) and vehicle dynamics (vehicle speed, engine speed, turbine speed, oil temperature) into deterministic actuation commands.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Intelligent Gear-Shifting Brain"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine you are driving a manual-gear car in dense city traffic. Your brain continuously watches: *(i)* the speedometer, *(ii)* the engine RPM sound, *(iii)* the road slope, and *(iv)* your urgency to overtake. Based on this, your left foot modulates the clutch, your right foot controls the throttle, and your right hand shifts the lever — all happening **in a fraction of a second**. Now, replace *your brain* with a **32-bit microcontroller running a real-time RTOS**, replace your *senses* with **sensors**, and replace your *limbs* with **solenoids and hydraulic valves** — that is precisely what the TCM does, but with **microsecond precision, no fatigue, and adaptive learning**.

## 1.3 Position in the Modern Vehicle Network

The TCM is one node among many on the high-speed powertrain CAN bus. It does **not** operate in isolation; it shares torque requests, engine speed, and brake torque signals with peer ECUs.

| ECU Peer (Communicating Node) | Data Exchanged With TCM | Bus |
|---|---|---|
| Engine Control Module (ECM) | Engine Torque, RPM, Throttle Position | CAN High-Speed (500 kbps) |
| Brake Control Module (ABS/ESC) | Brake Torque, Wheel Speed | CAN High-Speed |
| Body Control Module (BCM) | Gear Selector Position, Ignition State | CAN / LIN |
| Battery Management System (BMS) | Battery Voltage for Solenoid Actuation | CAN |

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Bold Physical Constants (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **CAN Bus Speed (Powertrain):** **500 kbps**
> - **CAN Bus Speed (Chassis):** **250 kbps**
> - **TCM Operating Voltage:** **12 V ± 0.5 V** (nominal automotive battery rail)
> - **Oil Operating Temperature Window:** **−40 °C to +150 °C**
> - **Typical Solenoid Actuation Current:** **0.8 A to 1.8 A** (PWM-driven at **~100 Hz to 1 kHz**)
> - **Shift Execution Time (Modern AT/DCT):** **100 ms to 400 ms**
> - **TCM Microcontroller Clock Frequency:** typically **150 MHz to 300 MHz** (e.g., Infineon Aurix TC2xx/TC3xx)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a TCM

The TCM is architecturally layered. The KTU examiner expects students to recognize the four classical layers:

### Layer 1 — Input / Signal Conditioning Stage
- **Analog Inputs (AI):** Throttle Position Sensor (TPS, dual-track potentiometer), Transmission Oil Temperature (TOT, NTC thermistor), Turbine Speed Sensor (Hall-effect).
- **Digital / Frequency Inputs:** Output Shaft Speed Sensor (variable reluctance), Crankshaft Position (via CAN).
- **Switch Inputs:** Park/Neutral/Drive/Low (PRNDL) selector switch, brake pedal switch.

### Layer 2 — Microcontroller Core (Decision Engine)
- Multi-core 32-bit MCU (e.g., **Infineon Aurix TC3xx**, **NXP MPC574x**, **Renesas RH850**).
- Runs a deterministic **AUTOSAR Classic** software stack.
- Hosts the **Shift Scheduler FSM (Finite State Machine)**.

### Layer 3 — Driver Stage (Power Electronics)
- **Low-Side Drivers (LSD):** N-channel MOSFETs driving shift solenoids.
- **High-Side Drivers (HSD):** P-channel or smart high-side switches.
- **H-Bridge Drivers:** For lock-up clutch and pressure regulation valves.

### Layer 4 — Actuators
- **Shift Solenoids (SS1, SS2, SS3, SS4):** Spool valves routing hydraulic pressure.
- **Pressure Control Solenoids (PCS):** PWM-modulated proportional valves.
- **Torque Converter Clutch (TCC) Solenoid.**

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Shift Control — Operational Logic Steps

1. **Sample sensors** (TPS, vehicle speed, engine RPM, oil temp) every **10 ms** (control loop period).
2. **Determine Driver Intent** by reading the PRNDL switch and brake pedal.
3. **Compute Desired Gear** using a pre-calibrated **Shift Map** (lookup table indexed by TPS% and vehicle speed).
4. **Request Engine Torque Reduction** to ECM via CAN message `EngTorqueReq` to reduce driveline shock during the shift.
5. **Energize Shift Solenoids** in the precise hydraulic sequence (e.g., 1-2 upshift: SS1 OFF → SS2 ON).
6. **Ramp Line Pressure** via PWM duty-cycle modulation of the PCS to avoid clutch slip.
7. **Lock TCC** (torque converter lockup) at steady cruise for fuel economy.
8. **Synchronize** with ABS during downhill shifts to prevent wheel lock.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table (Must Memorize for KTU Board Exams):**

| # | Formula / Relation | Variable Definitions | Typical Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle i_{gear} = \frac{N_{engine}}{N_{output\_shaft}}$ | Gear Ratio, Engine Speed, Output Speed | dimensionless | Defines mechanical advantage of each gear |
| 2 | $\displaystyle T_{output} = T_{engine} \cdot i_{gear} \cdot \eta_{trans}$ | Output Torque, Engine Torque, Transmission Efficiency | N·m | Computes torque delivered to driveshaft |
| 3 | $\displaystyle \tau_{shift} = J_{eq} \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt}$ | Shift Inertia Torque, Equivalent Inertia, Angular Acceleration | N·m | Determines torque-hole magnitude |
| 4 | $\displaystyle v_{vehicle} = \frac{2\pi \cdot r_{wheel} \cdot N_{output}}{60 \cdot i_{final}}$ | Vehicle Velocity, Wheel Radius, Output RPM, Final Drive Ratio | m/s | Speed-to-RPM conversion |
| 5 | $\displaystyle P_{hydraulic} = Q \cdot \Delta P$ | Hydraulic Power, Flow Rate, Pressure Drop | W | Sizing of PCS valve |
| 6 | $\displaystyle D_{PWM} = \frac{V_{avg}}{V_{supply}} \times 100\%$ | PWM Duty Cycle, Average Solenoid Voltage | % | Controls PCS modulation |
| 7 | $\displaystyle f_{loop} = \frac{1}{T_{sample}} = \frac{1}{0.01 \text{ s}} = 100 \text{ Hz}$ | Control Loop Frequency | Hz | TCM inner-loop rate |
| 8 | $\displaystyle \tau_{line\_pressure} = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt$ | PI-Controller Output for Line Pressure | bar | Closed-loop clutch pressure regulation |

> **Syllabus Highlight:** The TCM implements a **PI / PID closed-loop controller** on line pressure to maintain constant clutch torque capacity during a shift.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern vehicles, the TCM directly influences:
- **Fuel Economy:** Up to **15–20 %** improvement via TCC lockup and optimized shift scheduling.
- **Emissions Compliance:** Coordinates with ECM to keep engine in the BS-VI / Euro 6 efficiency window.
- **Drivability:** Reduces shift shock ("torque hole") to < **5 N·m** perceptually.
- **EVT (Electrified Vehicles):** Modern TCMs integrate with hybrid power-split devices (e.g., Toyota THS).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vehicle Velocity from Output Shaft RPM

This is a **guaranteed KTU question** because it links the TCM's input signal (shaft speed) to the controlled output (vehicle speed).

**Given Data (assume typical compact car):**
- Output shaft RPM during 3rd gear: $N_{output} = 2400 \text{ rpm}$
- Tire dynamic radius: $r_{wheel} = 0.31 \text{ m}$
- Final drive ratio: $i_{final} = 3.94$
- Transmission efficiency: $\eta_{trans} = 0.92$

**Step 1 — Convert RPM to angular velocity:**

$$\omega_{output} = \frac{2\pi \cdot N_{output}}{60}$$

$$\omega_{output} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 2400}{60} = 80\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 251.327 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute wheel angular velocity using final drive ratio:**

$$\omega_{wheel} = \frac{\omega_{output}}{i_{final}}$$

$$\omega_{wheel} = \frac{251.327}{3.94} \approx 63.788 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute linear vehicle velocity:**

$$v_{vehicle} = r_{wheel} \cdot \omega_{wheel}$$

$$v_{vehicle} = 0.31 \cdot 63.788 \approx 19.774 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to km/h for engineering readability:**

$$v_{vehicle} = 19.774 \times 3.6 \approx 71.19 \text{ km/h}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** At $v_{vehicle} = 71.19$ km/h in 3rd gear, the TCM would evaluate its shift map; if TPS > **30 %**, it would likely schedule a **3 → 4 upshift** within 200 ms.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Output Torque at the Driveshaft

**Given:**
- Engine torque from ECM (via CAN): $T_{engine} = 180 \text{ N·m}$
- 2nd gear ratio (typical 6-speed AT): $i_{2} = 3.20$
- 1st gear ratio: $i_{1} = 4.58$ (for context)
- $\eta_{trans} = 0.92$

**Step 1 — Apply the gear-ratio torque equation:**

$$T_{output} = T_{engine} \cdot i_{gear} \cdot \eta_{trans}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values for 2nd gear:**

$$T_{output,2} = 180 \cdot 3.20 \cdot 0.92$$

$$T_{output,2} = 576 \cdot 0.92 = 529.92 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with 1st gear:**

$$T_{output,1} = 180 \cdot 4.58 \cdot 0.92 = 758.45 \text{ N·m}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** 1st gear delivers **43 % more torque** than 2nd — this is why TCM holds 1st gear longer on inclines (hill-hold logic).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inertia Torque During a 2 → 3 Upshift (the "Torque Hole")

This is the **most marks-fetching derivation** for KTU because it explains the *torque hole* phenomenon that TCM engineers must minimize.

**Given:**
- Effective driveline inertia at transmission output: $J_{eq} = 0.45 \text{ kg·m}^2$
- Engine speed drop during shift: $\Delta\omega_{engine} = 60 \text{ rad/s}$
- Shift duration (synchronization time): $t_{shift} = 0.25 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Compute angular deceleration:**

$$\alpha = \frac{\Delta\omega_{engine}}{t_{shift}}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{60}{0.25} = 240 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute inertia torque (the torque-hole magnitude):**

$$\tau_{inertia} = J_{eq} \cdot \alpha$$

$$\tau_{inertia} = 0.45 \cdot 240 = 108 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute required ECM torque reduction to compensate:**

$$\Delta T_{ECM} = \tau_{inertia} - T_{losses}$$

$$\Delta T_{ECM} \approx 108 - 30 = 78 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Engine torque request sent on CAN:**

The TCM broadcasts the message `EngTorqueReq = T_{engine} − ΔT_{ECM}` so the ECM throttles back momentarily, smoothing the shift.

$$T_{engine,req} = 180 - 78 = 102 \text{ N·m}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The "torque hole" perceived by the driver is **only 30 N·m** instead of 108 N·m, thanks to TCM-ECM coordination — a textbook example of **cross-ECU real-time cooperation**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When Does the TCM Upshift?

| Throttle Position (TPS) | Vehicle Speed | Current Gear | TCM Action | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 8 % | 35 km/h | 2nd | Hold 2nd | Cruise mode, no urgency |
| 35 % | 38 km/h | 2nd | Upshift 2 → 3 | Normal acceleration |
| 75 % | 30 km/h | 1st | Hold 1st | Kickdown / hill climb |
| 15 % | 80 km/h | 4th | Lock TCC | Fuel economy cruise |
| 0 % | 60 km/h | 4th | Downshift 4 → 3 | Brake-torque fuel cut |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TCM Functional Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENS["INPUT SENSOR LAYER"]
        S1["TPS Throttle Position Sensor"]
        S2["VSS Vehicle Speed Sensor"]
        S3["OSS Output Shaft Speed"]
        S4["TOT Transmission Oil Temp"]
        S5["PRNDL Gear Selector Switch"]
        S6["BPS Brake Pedal Switch"]
    end

    subgraph MCU["TCM MICROCONTROLLER CORE"]
        C1["Signal Conditioning ADC"]

        C5["CAN Protocol Stack"]
    end

    subgraph DRV["POWER DRIVER STAGE"]
        D1["LSD Shift Solenoid 1"]
        D2["LSD Shift Solenoid 2"]

    end

    subgraph ACT["ACTUATOR LAYER"]
        A1["Hydraulic Spool Valves"]

    end

    subgraph NET["VEHICLE CAN BUS"]

    end

    SENS --> C1
    C1 --> C3
    C3 --> C2
    C3 --> C4
    C3 --> C5
    C4 --> DRV
    D1 --> A1
    D2 --> A1
    D3 --> A2

    C5 <--> N3
```

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Shift Control Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Driver Throttle Input"] --> B["TPS Sensor Reading"]
    B --> C["TCM Reads Vehicle Speed"]
    C --> D{"Shift Map Decision"}
    D -->|"Upshift Required"| E["Request Torque Cut to ECM"]
    E --> F["Energize Shift Solenoids"]
    F --> G["Measure Output Shaft Speed"]
    G --> H{"Speed Sync Achieved"}
    H -->|"No"| I["PI Pressure Correction"]
    I --> F
    H -->|"Yes"| J["Restore Engine Torque"]
    J --> K["Shift Complete Ack to BCM"]
```

## 4.3 TCM-to-ECM CAN Message Exchange Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DRV as Driver
    participant TCM as TCM
    participant ECM as ECM
    participant SOL as Shift Solenoid
    DRV->>TCM: Throttle Increase 25 percent
    TCM->>ECM: CAN Msg EngTorqueReq = 180 Nm
    ECM-->>TCM: CAN Msg EngTorqueAct = 180 Nm
    TCM->>SOL: Energize SS1 OFF, SS2 ON
    SOL-->>TCM: Hydraulic Pressure Reached 8 bar
    TCM->>ECM: CAN Msg EngTorqueReq = 102 Nm
    ECM-->>TCM: CAN Msg EngTorqueAct = 102 Nm
    TCM->>TCM: Detect Speed Sync
    TCM->>ECM: CAN Msg EngTorqueReq = 180 Nm
    TCM->>DRV: Smooth 2 to 3 upshift complete
```

---
