# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The V2V Communication Stack (Layered Architecture)

The V2V protocol stack is best understood in **two parallel technologies**:

### A. DSRC / WAVE Stack (IEEE Family)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ IEEE 1609.3   — Networking Services (WSMP, IPv6)│
│ IEEE 1609.4   — Multi-channel Operation         │
│ IEEE 1609.2   — Security Services (Signing)     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ IEEE 802.11p  — MAC + PHY (OFDM, 5.9 GHz)       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ IEEE 802.2    — LLC Sublayer                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### B. C-V2X Stack (3GPP Family)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ ETSI ITS / SAE J2945 — Facilities (Message Gen) │
│ Application Layer (BSM, CAM, DENM)              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 3GPP LTE-V2X / NR-V2X (PC5 Sidelink)            │
│ MAC / PHY (5G New Radio, OFDM, 5.9 GHz)         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ LTE Uu (optional, for non-safety)               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **DSRC vs C-V2X at a glance:**
> - **DSRC (802.11p)** → CSMA/CA, listen-before-talk, no scheduler, contention-based, mature since ~2010.
> - **C-V2X (LTE-V2X, Rel-14+)** → Uses **sidelink** (PC5 interface) for direct V2V, and **Uu interface** for network-assisted. Can operate **without cellular coverage** (mode 4). Better range, better in non-line-of-sight, scalable to 5G NR-V2X.

---

## 2.2 Message Types (KTU Frequently Asked)

### 2.2.1 BSM — Basic Safety Message (SAE J2735 / US Standard)

- **Frequency:** **10 Hz** (every 100 ms)
- **Size:** ~**244 bytes** (Part I mandatory + Part II optional)
- **Content:** GPS lat/lon, speed, heading, acceleration (3-axis), brake status, steering angle, vehicle size, DSRC message ID, time stamp.
- **Use:** Continuous cooperative awareness.

### 2.2.2 CAM — Cooperative Awareness Message (ETSI EN 302 637-2, EU)

- **Frequency:** **1–10 Hz** (event-triggered, default 1 Hz)
- **Size:** ~**100–800 bytes**
- **Content:** Station type, position, speed, heading, activation of lights/path history.

### 2.2.3 DENM — Decentralized Environmental Notification Message (ETSI EN 302 637-3)

- **Frequency:** Event-driven (not periodic)
- **Use:** Hazard warnings (accident, ice, emergency braking, road works).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory aid:**
> **B**SM = **B**eam me your state every 100 ms.
> **C**AM = **C**ontinuous **A**wareness (European BSM).
> **D**ENM = **D**anger event **N**otification (asynchronous alert).

---

## 2.3 IEEE 802.11p PHY — Critical Parameters

| Parameter | Value | Why It Matters |
|-----------|-------|----------------|
| **Frequency Band** | 5.85–5.925 GHz | Licensed, safety-critical |
| **Channel Bandwidth** | **10 MHz** (vs 20 MHz in Wi-Fi) | **Halves the Doppler spread**, doubles symbol duration (8 µs), mitigates high-mobility distortion |
| **Modulation** | BPSK, QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM | Adaptive rate |
| **Data Rates** | **3, 4.5, 6, 9, 12, 18, 24, 27 Mbps** | Lower rates → more robust, longer range |
| **OFDM Subcarriers** | 52 (48 data + 4 pilot) | Same as 802.11a/g |
| **FFT Size** | 64 | Standard OFDM |
| **Symbol Duration** | **8 µs** | Twice Wi-Fi's 4 µs (delay spread tolerance) |
| **Guard Interval** | 1.6 µs | Robustness against multipath |
| **TX Power** | Up to **+33 dBm (2 W) EIRP** | Long range, but regulated |
| **Receiver Sensitivity** | –85 dBm (typ.) | Defines link budget |
| **Max Range** | ~1000 m (LOS), ~300 m (NLOS urban) | Safety-relevant |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **most important** for solving KTU 2024 numerical/model questions on V2V. Memorize the form, the variables, and the units.

### 2.4.1 Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) — V2V LOS

The Friis transmission equation in linear and logarithmic form:

$$
\boxed{\text{FSPL} = \left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}})}
$$

Where:
- $d$ = transmitter–receiver separation (m or km)
- $f$ = carrier frequency (Hz or MHz)
- $\lambda = c/f$ = wavelength
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** (speed of light)

### 2.4.2 Two-Ray Ground Reflection Model (V2V NLOS / Highway)

Used when both antennas are above the ground plane (typical for cars):

$$
\boxed{P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \frac{h_t^{2} h_r^{2}}{d^{4}}}
$$

Path loss in dB:

$$
\boxed{\text{PL}_{2\text{-ray(dB)}} = 40\log_{10}(d) - 10\log_{10}(G_t G_r h_t^{2} h_r^{2})}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** At 5.9 GHz and typical vehicle antenna heights (1.5 m), the **breakpoint distance** is $d_b = \frac{4 h_t h_r}{\lambda} \approx 700$ m. Beyond this, the path-loss exponent jumps from 2 (free-space) to **4** (two-ray), which is critical for V2V range estimation.

### 2.4.3 Doppler Shift in V2V (High-Mobility Effect)

$$
\boxed{f_d = \frac{v}{\lambda} \cos\theta = \frac{v f_0}{c} \cos\theta}
$$

Where:
- $v$ = relative speed between vehicles
- $\theta$ = angle between velocity vector and line connecting vehicles
- $f_0$ = carrier frequency

**Numerical example:** Two cars approaching at 200 km/h = 55.56 m/s at $\theta = 0°$ on 5.9 GHz:

$$
f_d = \frac{55.56 \times 5.9 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}} \approx \mathbf{1092 \, \text{Hz}}
$$

The **coherence time** is then $T_c \approx \frac{0.423}{f_d} \approx 0.39$ ms — extremely short, which is why 802.11p uses **half-bandwidth (10 MHz)** channels.

### 2.4.4 Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity

$$
\boxed{C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = B \log_{2}(1 + \text{SNR})}
$$

Where:
- $C$ = channel capacity (bits/s)
- $B$ = bandwidth (Hz) = **10 MHz** for DSRC
- SNR = signal-to-noise ratio (linear)

### 2.4.5 Link Budget (Receiver Sensitivity Check)

$$
\boxed{P_r \, (\text{dBm}) = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{PL}(d) - L_{\text{extra}}}
$$

The link is feasible if $P_r \geq P_{\text{sensitivity}}$ (e.g., –85 dBm for QPSK at 6 Mbps).

### 2.4.6 Coherence Bandwidth & RMS Delay Spread

$$
\boxed{B_c \approx \frac{1}{2\pi \sigma_\tau} \approx \frac{1}{5\sigma_\tau}}
$$

For a typical **urban V2V** channel, $\sigma_\tau \approx 200$ ns, giving $B_c \approx 1$ MHz — *narrowband* in the DSRC sense, hence **flat fading** is the typical V2V model (Rician or Rayleigh).

### 2.4.7 Maximum Latency Bound for Safety Applications

$$
\boxed{\text{End-to-End Latency} = t_{\text{tx}} + t_{\text{prop}} + t_{\text{queue}} + t_{\text{proc}} \leq L_{\max}}
$$

| Application | $L_{\max}$ |
|-------------|-----------|
| Forward Collision Warning | **100 ms** |
| Emergency Brake Light Warning | **20 ms** |
| Blind Spot Warning | **100 ms** |
| Lane Change Warning | **100 ms** |
| Cooperative Adaptive Cruise Control | **20 ms** |

### 2.4.8 Message Generation Rate (BSM) Capacity Load

For a road with vehicle density $\rho$ (vehicles/km) and 10 Hz BSM rate:

$$
\boxed{\text{Channel Load} = \rho \times 10 \, \text{ BSM/s/vehicle} \times 244 \, \text{bytes}}
$$

**Worst case (1 km highway, $\rho = 100$):** 244 KB/s = ~2 Mbps — fits within 6 Mbps QPSK rate comfortably.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of V2V

> [!NOTE]
> **Why engineers care about V2V math:**
> 1. **Antenna placement** on a vehicle ($h_r$ in two-ray model) is dominated by aesthetics (shark-fin on roof) but directly determines the breakpoint distance and the multipath profile.
> 2. **Power allocation** is regulated — exceeding 33 dBm EIRP is illegal in 5.9 GHz ITS band.
> 3. **Beamforming** at mmWave (future 5G NR-V2X at 30 GHz) is being researched for V2V to combat blockage.
> 4. **Sensor fusion** in the autonomous stack uses V2V data to extend the *electronic horizon* far beyond the camera/radar line-of-sight.

---

## 2.6 Step-by-Step Concept of a BSM Transmission (Why and How)

The "Why" and "How" behind each stage of a V2V BSM round-trip:

1. **Why** does the OBU collect GPS at 10 Hz? → Because vehicle dynamics (acceleration) change at < 100 ms timescales, and BSMs must capture *current* state to be useful for the receiver's collision-avoidance algorithm.

2. **How** is the BSM built? → The OBU's *facilities layer* (SAE J2945) packages data from the CAN bus (speed, brake, steering) + GNSS receiver (lat/lon/heading) into a J2735-formatted BSM.

3. **Why** sign the BSM? → Because attackers could inject false "ghost vehicles" to disable adaptive cruise control. IEEE 1609.2 mandates **PKI-based digital signatures** using ECDSA-256.

4. **How** does it reach other cars? → The MAC layer (1609.4) picks the **SCH (Service Channel)** or **CCH (Control Channel)** in alternating slots of 50 ms. PHY transmits the OFDM burst on a 10 MHz channel.

5. **Why** does the receiver trust it? → It verifies the signature against a **Certificate Authority (CA)** hierarchy (US DOT SCMS — Security Credential Management System).

6. **How** does it become an action? → The receiver's application layer fuses this BSM with onboard sensors (radar, camera) and, if a threat is detected, issues a warning to the driver (HMI) or commands brakes (AEB).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation #1 — Link Budget for a Highway V2V Link

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU-style):**
> A V2V link uses 802.11p with center frequency $f_0 = 5.9$ GHz, TX power $P_t = 20$ dBm, TX antenna gain $G_t = 3$ dBi, RX antenna gain $G_r = 3$ dBi, RX sensitivity $P_{\text{sens}} = -82$ dBm at QPSK 6 Mbps. Compute the maximum reliable communication range assuming free-space propagation. State whether the range is acceptable for a Forward Collision Warning application (≥ 300 m).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the given quantities and units**

| Symbol | Value | Unit |
|--------|-------|------|
| $f_0$ | 5.9 × 10⁹ | Hz |
| $P_t$ | 20 | dBm |
| $G_t$ | 3 | dBi |
| $G_r$ | 3 | dBi |
| $P_{\text{sens}}$ | –82 | dBm |

**Step 2 — Convert sensitivity to dBW (optional but cleaner for math)**

$$
P_{\text{sens,dBW}} = P_{\text{sens,dBm}} - 30 = -82 - 30 = -112 \text{ dBW}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum allowable path loss**

The fundamental link equation in dB:

$$
P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{PL}(d) \geq P_{\text{sens}}
$$

Solving for the maximum path loss:

$$
\text{PL}_{\max} = P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{\text{sens}}
$$

$$
\text{PL}_{\max} = 20 + 3 + 3 - (-82) = 108 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Invert the FSPL equation to find $d_{\max}$**

$$
\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}})
$$

Set $\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = \text{PL}_{\max} = 108$ dB and solve for $d_{\text{km}}$:

$$
108 = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(5900) + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}})
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(5900) = 20 \times 3.7709 = 75.42
$$

$$
108 = 32.45 + 75.42 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}})
$$

$$
108 - 107.87 = 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}})
$$

$$
0.13 = 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}})
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) = \frac{0.13}{20} = 0.0065
$$

$$
d_{\text{km}} = 10^{0.0065} = 1.015 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to metres**

$$
\boxed{d_{\max} \approx 1015 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 6 — Decision for FCW application**

Since 1015 m > 300 m, the link **comfortably meets** the Forward Collision Warning range requirement with 30+ dB of fade margin. The link budget is engineering-feasible for highway use.

**Note on NLOS:** In a real urban canyon with NLoS, the path loss exponent rises to ~3–4, reducing range to 300–500 m — still acceptable, but with smaller fade margin.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation #2 — Doppler Spread for Oncoming V2V Scenario

**Problem Statement:**
> Two cars are approaching each other head-on. Car A is moving at 100 km/h, Car B at 60 km/h. Carrier frequency $f_0 = 5.9$ GHz. Find the **maximum Doppler frequency shift** and the **coherence time** of the channel.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the closing speed**

The relative speed is the *sum* of magnitudes when cars approach head-on:

$$
v_{\text{rel}} = v_A + v_B = 100 + 60 = 160 \text{ km/h}
$$

Convert to m/s:

$$
v_{\text{rel}} = 160 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 44.44 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the wavelength**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{5.9 \times 10^{9}} = 0.05085 \text{ m} \approx 5.08 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum Doppler shift (at $\theta = 0°$)**

$$
f_d = \frac{v_{\text{rel}}}{\lambda} \cos\theta
$$

At maximum (head-on, $\theta = 0°$, $\cos\theta = 1$):

$$
f_d = \frac{44.44}{0.05085} \times 1 = 873.9 \text{ Hz} \approx 874 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Doppler spread (worst-case = 2 × max Doppler)**

For an isotropic scattering environment:

$$
B_D = 2 f_d = 2 \times 874 = 1748 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.75 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the coherence time (Jakes' model, 50% correlation)**

$$
T_c \approx \frac{0.423}{f_d} = \frac{0.423}{874} = 4.84 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 484 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check against OFDM symbol duration**

802.11p symbol duration $T_s = 8$ µs. Since $T_s \ll T_c$ (8 µs vs 484 µs), the channel is **slow-fading** within one OFDM symbol — the 10 MHz channelization choice is verified correct for V2V.

**Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{f_d \approx 874 \text{ Hz}, \quad T_c \approx 484 \text{ µs}, \quad \text{Channel is slow fading}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DSRC vs C-V2X

| Parameter | DSRC (802.11p) | C-V2X (LTE/5G NR) |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Standardization Year** | 2010 | 2016 (3GPP Rel-14) |
| **Origin** | IEEE / Wi-Fi family | 3GPP / Cellular family |
| **PHY Access** | CSMA/CA (contention) | SPSC (semi-persistent scheduling) |
| **Synchronization** | Asynchronous | Network-assisted or GNSS-based |
| **Latency** | 4–20 ms | 5–20 ms (PC5 mode 4) |
| **Range (highway)** | 1000 m LOS | 1200 m LOS |
| **NLOS Performance** | Poor | Better (signal processing gain) |
| **Scalability at high density** | Degrades (CSMA collision) | Better (scheduled resources) |
| **Maturity / Deployment** | Mature, deployed in US/EU (2017–2019) | Emerging, post-2020 |
| **Future Roadmap** | Phasing out in US (2020 FCC ruling) | Migrating to 5G NR-V2X |
| **Maneuvering at 250 km/h** | Tested, marginal | Designed-in support |
| **Bandwidth** | 10 / 20 MHz | 10 / 20 MHz (LTE), 40+ MHz (NR) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks:** *“Compare DSRC and C-V2X and justify which is better for an Indian smart-city deployment.”*
> **Standard answer:** C-V2X is favored because India (along with most of Asia and Europe post-2020) is moving towards 5G NR-V2X due to its superior scalability, cellular reuse, and roadmap to NR sidelink (Rel-16/17).

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation #3 — BSM Channel Load in Dense Traffic

**Problem Statement:**
> A 2-lane urban arterial has a vehicle density of 150 vehicles/km. Each vehicle transmits a 244-byte BSM at 10 Hz on a 6 Mbps QPSK channel. Compute the channel load as a percentage.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Per-vehicle bit rate**

$$
R_{\text{per-veh}} = 244 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} \times 10 \text{ Hz} = 19520 \text{ bps} \approx 19.52 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate channel load from 150 vehicles within 1 km**

$$
R_{\text{total}} = 150 \times 19.52 \text{ kbps} = 2928 \text{ kbps} \approx 2.93 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the channel load fraction**

$$
\text{Load}_{\%} = \frac{R_{\text{total}}}{R_{\text{channel}}} \times 100 = \frac{2.93}{6} \times 100 = 48.8\%
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

Even at 150 veh/km, the **aggregate BSM load is only ~49%** of the QPSK 6 Mbps channel — comfortably within capacity, with ~50% headroom for retransmissions, beacons, and DENM events. The 802.11p channel is **not** the bottleneck for BSM broadcast.

> [!NOTE]
> **This result is a common viva question:** *“Is the V2V channel capacity-limited?”* The answer is **no** for typical periodic BSM traffic, but **yes** for video/LiDAR cooperative perception streams (which require ~50–100 Mbps and are not part of safety-critical 802.11p).

## 4.2 DSRC Channelization (Mermaid — Frequency Allocation)

> The 5.9 GHz ITS band is divided into 7 channels of 10 MHz each, alternating between **CCH** (Control) and **SCH** (Service).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DSRC["DSRC BAND 5.850 - 5.925 GHz (Total 75 MHz)"]
        CH178["CH 178\n5.915 - 5.925 GHz\nSCH High Power"]

        CH174["CH 174\n5.875 - 5.885 GHz\nSCH"]
        CH172["CH 172\n5.855 - 5.865 GHz\nSCH"]
        CH178Crit["CH 178 Critical Safety"]
        CH184["CH 184\n5.855 - 5.865 GHz\nSCH"]
        CH182["CH 182\n5.835 - 5.845 GHz\nSCH"]
        CH180["CH 180\n5.815 - 5.825 GHz\nSCH"]
        CCH["CH CCH 178\n5.885 - 5.895 GHz\nCONTROL CHANNEL\nBSM Broadcast"]
    end

    CCH -.->|"Alternating 50 ms slots"| SCH172[("SCH Operation")]
    SCH172 -.->|"Multi-channel coord"| CH178
```

## 4.3 V2V Message Lifecycle State Machine (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Sensing: GNSS fix acquired
    Sensing --> DataFusion: CAN bus data ready
    DataFusion --> BSMBuild: 100 ms timer expires
    BSMBuild --> Sign: BSM payload ready
    Sign --> Transmit: ECDSA signed
    Transmit --> Idle: TX complete on CCH
    Transmit --> CollisionBackoff: CSMA channel busy
    CollisionBackoff --> Transmit: backoff timer expires
    Sign --> EventTrigger: DENM condition detected
    EventTrigger --> DENMTransmit: Asynchronous DENM
    DENMTransmit --> Idle
```

## 4.5 Security / PKI Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OBU as Vehicle OBU
    participant CA as Certificate Authority
    participant PeerOBU as Peer Vehicle OBU
    Note over OBU,CA: Bootstrap Phase
    OBU->>CA: Request Pseudonym Certificate
    CA-->>OBU: Signed Pseudonym Cert (Valid 5 min)
    Note over OBU,PeerOBU: V2V Runtime Phase
    OBU->>PeerOBU: BSM + Signature + Cert
    PeerOBU->>PeerOBU: Verify ECDSA Signature
    PeerOBU->>PeerOBU: Check Cert against CRL
    PeerOBU-->>OBU: Trust Decision
    Note over CA: CA periodically issues Misbehavior Reports
```

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

> **Q1.** Define Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) communication. List **four** safety applications enabled by V2V.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
V2V communication is a wireless, short-range, peer-to-peer, broadcast-based cooperative driving technology in which OBUs exchange real-time kinematic and operational state (position, speed, heading, brake status) at 10 Hz over a 300–1000 m range, primarily using IEEE 802.11p / IEEE 1609 WAVE in the 5.9 GHz band.

**Four safety applications:**
1. Forward Collision Warning (FCW)
2. Emergency Brake Light Warning (EBLW)
3. Blind Spot Warning (BSW)
4. Lane Change Warning (LCW)
5. (Bonus) Intersection Collision Warning

*(Allocation: 1 mark definition + 2 marks applications — partial marks if 3 apps only.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

> **Q2.** What is a BSM in V2V? Mention its **transmission frequency**, **size**, and **two mandatory fields**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Basic Safety Message (BSM)** is the standardized periodic broadcast message defined by SAE J2735, used in DSRC-based V2V to convey a vehicle's current state to neighbors.

- **Transmission frequency:** 10 Hz (every 100 ms)
- **Size:** ~244 bytes (Part I mandatory + Part II optional up to 1200 bytes)
- **Two mandatory fields:** (i) GPS latitude and longitude, (ii) Vehicle speed and heading (Part I core data element).

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark frequency/size + 1 mark fields.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B pattern: Each module typically carries a 14-mark question with internal choice (either/or). Solve **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question 3 — Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

> **Q3 (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **WAVE (IEEE 1609) protocol stack** for V2V. State the function of each layer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q3 (b)** A DSRC V2V link operates at 5.9 GHz, TX power 20 dBm, TX and RX antenna gains 3 dBi each, and RX sensitivity –82 dBm. Assuming free-space propagation, calculate the **maximum communication range** and comment on its suitability for a Forward Collision Warning (FCW) application requiring 300 m. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(a) — WAVE Stack (7 marks)

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ IEEE 1609.3 — Networking & WSMP             │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ IEEE 1609.2 — Security Services              │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ IEEE 1609.4 — Multi-Channel Operation        │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ IEEE 802.11p — MAC + PHY (OFDM, 10 MHz)     │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ IEEE 802.2 — LLC                            │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Layer Functions (5 marks):**

| Layer | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **IEEE 802.2 LLC** | Provides logical link control and uniform interface to upper layers, multiplexing multiple upper-layer protocols. |
| **IEEE 802.11p MAC** | Defines the contention-based channel access (CSMA/CA), frame format, and timing for the 5.9 GHz PHY. Supports the 10 MHz channelization and EDCA QoS. |
| **IEEE 802.11p PHY** | OFDM-based 10 MHz waveform in 5.85–5.925 GHz; supports rates 3–27 Mbps; designed for high Doppler and short coherence time. |
| **IEEE 1609.4** | Manages multi-channel operation: alternates Control Channel (CCH) and Service Channel (SCH) in 50 ms slots; coordinates channel switching. |
| **IEEE 1609.2** | Provides security: ECDSA-based signing of BSMs, certificate management, and replay protection. |
| **IEEE 1609.3** | Networking services — defines **WAVE Short Message Protocol (WSMP)** for efficient BSM delivery without full IPv6 overhead; supports IPv6 as alternative. |

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(b) — Link Budget (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify parameters.

$$
f_0 = 5.9 \text{ GHz}, \quad P_t = 20 \text{ dBm}, \quad G_t = G_r = 3 \text{ dBi}, \quad P_{\text{sens}} = -82 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute maximum allowable path loss.

$$
\text{PL}_{\max} = P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{\text{sens}} = 20 + 3 + 3 - (-82) = 108 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply FSPL equation.

$$
\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(5900) + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}})
$$

$$
108 = 32.45 + 75.42 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}})
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Solve for $d_{\text{km}}$.

$$
20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) = 108 - 107.87 = 0.13
$$

$$
d_{\text{km}} = 10^{0.0065} = 1.015 \text{ km} \approx 1015 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclusion.

$$
\boxed{d_{\max} \approx 1015 \text{ m} \gg 300 \text{ m FCW requirement}}
$$

The link is **suitable** for FCW with a 700+ m fade margin, ensuring high reliability even under NLOS urban conditions (where path loss exponent rises to ~3–4, reducing effective range to ~500 m, still well above 300 m).

---

### Question 3 — Choice B (14 Marks, Alternative Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

> **Q3 (a)** Compare **DSRC (IEEE 802.11p)** and **C-V2X (LTE/5G NR)** along any **seven** parameters. State which technology is more suitable for future V2V deployment and justify. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q3 (b)** Two cars approach each other head-on. Car A is moving at 80 km/h, Car B at 70 km/h, on a 5.9 GHz DSRC channel. Calculate (i) maximum Doppler shift, (ii) coherence time, and (iii) comment on whether the 8 µs 802.11p symbol duration is robust to this Doppler effect. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(a) — DSRC vs C-V2X (7 marks)

| # | Parameter | DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) | C-V2X (3GPP Rel-14/16) |
|---|-----------|---------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Multiple Access | CSMA/CA (contention) | SPSC (scheduled) |
| 2 | Origin | Wi-Fi family | Cellular family |
| 3 | Latency | 4–20 ms | 5–20 ms |
| 4 | Range | ~1000 m LOS | ~1200 m LOS |
| 5 | Synchronization | Asynchronous | GNSS / Network sync |
| 6 | Scalability | Degrades at high density | Better (scheduled) |
| 7 | Future Roadmap | Phasing out (US FCC 2020) | Migrating to 5G NR-V2X |
| 8 | Spectrum | 5.9 GHz ITS | 5.9 GHz + Uu uplink |

*(5 marks — 0.5 per cell, 7 rows × 0.5 = 3.5 + integration marks 1.5)*

**Conclusion (2 marks):** C-V2X (especially 5G NR-V2X) is more suitable for future deployment because (i) it scales better with vehicle density via scheduled access, (ii) it leverages existing cellular infrastructure and roadmap, and (iii) NR-V2X supports higher data rates and integration with network slicing for safety services.

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(b) — Doppler Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute closing speed.

$$
v_{\text{rel}} = 80 + 70 = 150 \text{ km/h} = 41.67 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute wavelength.

$$
\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{5.9 \times 10^{9}} = 5.085 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute maximum Doppler shift ($\theta = 0°$).

$$
f_d = \frac{v_{\text{rel}}}{\lambda} = \frac{41.67}{0.05085} \approx 819.5 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute coherence time.

$$
T_c = \frac{0.423}{f_d} = \frac{0.423}{819.5} \approx 5.16 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 516 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compare with symbol duration.

$$
T_s = 8 \text{ µs} \ll T_c = 516 \text{ µs}
$$

Since $T_s \ll T_c$, the channel is **slow fading** within one OFDM symbol — the 802.11p design is **robust** to this Doppler effect. The 10 MHz channelization (vs 20 MHz Wi-Fi) is the engineering choice that halves the inter-carrier spacing to 156.25 kHz, providing 1.5× headroom for Doppler-induced ICI (inter-carrier interference).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing BSM and CAM:** BSM = SAE/US (10 Hz, 244 bytes), CAM = ETSI/EU (1–10 Hz, event-triggered). Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using 20 MHz bandwidth for DSRC:** The KTU-expected answer is **10 MHz**. The 20 MHz confusion costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the units in FSPL:** Use $f$ in **MHz** and $d$ in **km** to get the +32.45 constant. Mixing units costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Writing "Wi-Fi" instead of "802.11p":** Examiners want the **standard** number, not the marketing term. Penalized 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in the protocol stack question:** Even a rough ASCII block diagram earns 1.5–2 marks. Skipping it = -2 marks minimum.
> 6. **No justification in comparison questions:** "DSRC is better" without a reason = 0 marks for the conclusion. Always justify with a use-case (e.g., "DSRC is better for low-latency safety-only, but C-V2X is better for cellular integration").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **V2V Communication — Final KTU 2024 Cheat Sheet**

### 📌 Definition & Scope
- V2V = real-time, peer-to-peer, broadcast-based short-range wireless communication between OBUs.
- Subset of **V2X** (umbrella); siblings: V2I, V2P, V2N, V2G.
- Operates in **5.9 GHz ITS band** (75 MHz in US, 70 MHz in EU).

### 📌 Standards
- **PHY/MAC:** IEEE 802.11p (DSRC) or 3GPP C-V2X (PC5 sidelink).
- **Upper layers:** IEEE 1609.1/.2/.3/.4 (WAVE) or ETSI ITS-G5.
- **Messages:** SAE J2735 BSM (US) / ETSI EN 302 637 CAM + DENM (EU).

### 📌 Key PHY Parameters (802.11p)
- **Bandwidth:** 10 MHz (NOT 20 MHz).
- **Modulation:** OFDM with 52 subcarriers.
- **Rates:** 3, 4.5, 6, 9, 12, 18, 24, 27 Mbps.
- **Symbol time:** 8 µs (guard interval 1.6 µs).
- **TX power:** ≤ 33 dBm EIRP.

### 📌 Key Equations (Memorize)
- $\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}})$
- $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$
- $f_d = \dfrac{v f_0}{c}\cos\theta$
- $T_c \approx 0.423 / f_d$
- $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$

### 📌 Message Properties
- **BSM:** 10 Hz, 244 bytes, contains GPS, speed, heading, brake, acceleration.
- **CAM:** 1–10 Hz, event-triggered, EU standard.
- **DENM:** Asynchronous, hazard events.

### 📌 Latency Requirements
- **Safety critical:** < 20 ms (AEB, EBLW).
- **General safety:** < 100 ms (FCW, BSW, LCW).

### 📌 V2V-Specific Challenges
- **High mobility** → Doppler spread handled by 10 MHz channelization.
- **Frequent topology change** → broadcast rather than unicast.
- **No infrastructure** → ad-hoc / sidelink mode (no AP).
- **Security** → ECDSA-256 signing, PKI certificates.
- **Multipath** → Rician LOS-dominant channel model.

### 📌 DSRC vs C-V2X
- DSRC = CSMA/CA, contention, Wi-Fi heritage, mature but being phased out (US FCC 2020).
- C-V2X = SPSC scheduling, cellular heritage, scalable, evolving to 5G NR-V2X.

### 📌 Application Set (Memorize at least 5)
- Forward Collision Warning, Emergency Brake Light Warning, Blind Spot Warning, Lane Change Warning, Do Not Pass Warning, Intersection Collision Warning, Cooperative Adaptive Cruise Control, Platooning.

### 📌 Channel Models
- LOS highway: free-space ($\alpha = 2$).
- Urban NLOS: Rician / Rayleigh, $\alpha = 3$–$4$ beyond breakpoint.
- Breakpoint distance: $d_b = 4 h_t h_r / \lambda$.

### 📌 Historical / Industry Context (Bonus Marks)
- 1999: FCC allocates 5.9 GHz.
- 2010: IEEE 802.11p published.
- 2017: GM Cadillac CTS ships V2V (first US production).
- 2019: Toyota starts deploying V2V in Japan.
- 2020: FCC reallocates lower 5.9 GHz to Wi-Fi, leaving upper 30 MHz for C-V2X.
- 2024+: 5G NR-V2X (Rel-16/17/18) is the future.

> **End of Module 4 — Vehicle to Vehicle Communication Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PEMUT632)**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of V2X Communication

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) Communication** is a sophisticated, latency-critical wireless communication paradigm that enables a vehicle to exchange real-time kinematic, environmental, and safety-critical data with heterogeneous entities in its operational sphere — including other vehicles (V2V), roadside infrastructure (V2I), vulnerable road users such as pedestrians and cyclists (V2P), cellular networks and cloud backends (V2N), and the electrical power grid (V2G). V2X forms the wireless nervous system of **Cooperative Intelligent Transport Systems (C-ITS)** and is the foundational enabler for autonomous driving, predictive collision avoidance, smart traffic management, and energy-efficient transportation ecosystems as defined by the **3GPP Release 14/15/16** and **IEEE 802.11p/1609** family of standards.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context for Automotive Embedded Systems, V2X is studied as a **cyber-physical system (CPS)** layer that augments onboard sensors (RADAR, LIDAR, Camera) by providing **non-line-of-sight (NLOS) situational awareness**, which onboard perception systems fundamentally cannot achieve.

## 1.2 The Five Pillars of V2X — Taxonomy

| V2X Variant | Full Form | Communication Peer | Primary Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **V2V** | Vehicle-to-Vehicle | Other moving vehicles | Forward collision warning, blind-spot alert, platooning |
| **V2I** | Vehicle-to-Infrastructure | Roadside Units (RSU), Traffic signals | Signal phase timing, adaptive cruise via infrastructure |
| **V2P** | Vehicle-to-Pedestrian | Smartphones, wearables of VRUs | Pedestrian crossing alerts, cyclist safety |
| **V2N** | Vehicle-to-Network | Cellular base stations / Cloud | Real-time HD map updates, software OTA, infotainment |
| **V2G** | Vehicle-to-Grid | Smart electrical grid | Bidirectional EV charging, load balancing |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a busy Indian city junction during monsoon.** Your car's camera and RADAR can only "see" what is directly in front of you — perhaps a stationary truck blocking the view. You have no idea that behind that truck, an autorickshaw is hurtling at speed on a collision course. V2X is like giving every vehicle, traffic light, and pedestrian a **magical two-way radio walkie-talkie** that constantly broadcasts their position, speed, and intent. Even though you cannot physically see the autorickshaw (it is in your **non-line-of-sight**), your car **knows** it is there because the autorickshaw has been "talking" about itself over a dedicated safety radio channel. This is **cooperative perception** — vehicles cooperating electronically to see what no single sensor can.

A second analogy: think of V2X as a **group WhatsApp chat** between vehicles and infrastructure. Every participant "types" their status (location, speed, brake status) **10 times per second**, and every other participant reads everyone else's messages. The "chat room" operates on a special reserved frequency (5.9 GHz in most jurisdictions) to ensure **deterministic, low-latency delivery**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Operating Frequency Band:** **5.85 – 5.925 GHz** (Dedicated Short-Range Communications / ITS-G5 band, ~70 MHz bandwidth)
> - **Maximum Achievable Range:** ~**300 m (DSRC)** / ~**500+ m (C-V2X)** line-of-sight
> - **Latency Target:** **< 100 ms** end-to-end (3GPP eV2X target: **< 20 ms** for L3+ automation)
> - **Message Transmission Rate:** **10 Hz** for BSM/CAM (100 ms cycle)
> - **Relative Velocity Support:** up to **500 km/h** (designed for high-speed differentials)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 V2X — The Two Competing Technology Stacks

There are **two principal physical-layer architectures** for V2X defined globally. KTU examiners expect students to know both stacks and their comparative merits.

### 2.1.1 DSRC (Dedicated Short-Range Communications) — IEEE 802.11p

DSRC is a **Wi-Fi derivative** that was the **first commercially deployed V2X standard** (USA, 1999–2020 era). It operates without any cellular network infrastructure, making it suitable for **ad-hoc peer-to-peer vehicular mesh networking**.

**Operational Steps:**
1. The vehicle's **On-Board Unit (OBU)** broadcasts a **Basic Safety Message (BSM)** every **100 ms** on the Control Channel (CCH: **CH 178 @ 5.885 GHz**).
2. Each BSM packet contains: GPS position, timestamp, speed, acceleration, heading, vehicle dimensions, brake status.
3. Nearby OBUs within ~**300 m** receive the BSM via the Roadside Unit (RSU) or directly peer-to-peer.
4. The receiving OBU's safety application layer performs **threat assessment algorithms** (Time-To-Collision, Post-Encroachment-Time).
5. If a collision risk is detected, the Human-Machine Interface (HMI) issues an **audible/visual/haptic warning** to the driver within **< 100 ms**.

**Why DSRC Matters (Engineering Rationale):**
- **No subscription or SIM required** — purely peer-to-peer.
- **Very low latency at short range** (~**4 ms** MAC-layer latency).
- **Privacy-by-design** — no persistent identifiers transmitted.
- **Limitation:** Channel congestion degrades rapidly with vehicle density; no native support for wide-area coordination.

### 2.1.2 C-V2X (Cellular V2X) — 3GPP Release 14+

C-V2X reuses the **4G LTE / 5G NR physical layer** and is championed by the **5G Automotive Association (5GAA)**. It operates in **two modes** simultaneously:

- **PC5 Interface (Sidelink):** Direct device-to-device communication (similar to DSRC's peer-to-peer mode), operating on the **5.9 GHz ITS band**. Works **without network coverage**.
- **Uu Interface (Up/Downlink):** Traditional cellular communication via base stations (eNodeB / gNodeB) for wide-area V2N services.

**Why C-V2X Matters:**
- Leverages the **existing cellular ecosystem** and **economies of scale** of mobile networks.
- **5G NR-V2X** supports **ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC)** with **< 5 ms** one-way latency.
- Superior **spectral efficiency** and **range (~500 m+)** compared to DSRC.
- Supports **network-scheduled resources (Mode 3)** and **autonomous resources (Mode 4)** for sidelink.
- **Limitation:** Requires cellular coverage for Mode 3 scheduling; slightly higher power consumption.

## 2.2 OSI Layer Mapping for V2X Stack

| OSI Layer | DSRC Protocol | C-V2X Protocol | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Layer 7 (Application)** | SAE J2945/1 | SAE J2945, ETSI ITS-G5 | Safety applications (FCW, EEBL) |
| **Layer 6 (Presentation)** | ASN.1 / DER encoding | ASN.1 / DER encoding | Data serialization |
| **Layer 5 (Session)** | WAVE Service Advertisement (WSA) | Service Announcement | Session establishment |
| **Layer 4 (Transport)** | WSMP / UDP | UDP / IP | Reliable/quick transport |
| **Layer 3 (Network)** | WSMP (IPv6 optional) | IP / ProSe | Addressing, routing |
| **Layer 2 (Data Link)** | IEEE 802.11p (CSMA/CA) | LTE/NR Sidelink (PC5) | Channel access |
| **Layer 1 (Physical)** | OFDM @ 10 MHz | SC-FDM / OFDM @ 10/20 MHz | RF modulation |

## 2.3 Message Standards — The "Languages" of V2X

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BSM (Basic Safety Message) — SAE J2735 (USA/DSRC):** Core vehicle state beacon. Size ~**400 bytes**, transmitted at **10 Hz**, contains **Part I (mandatory: position, speed, heading)** and **Part II (optional: brake status, acceleration, path history)**.
>
> **CAM (Cooperative Awareness Message) — ETSI EN 302 637-2 (Europe/ITS-G5):** European counterpart to BSM. Generation triggered by **movement threshold** (Δpos > 4 m or Δv > 0.5 m/s), with frequency between **1 – 10 Hz**.
>
> **DENM (Decentralized Environmental Notification Message) — ETSI EN 302 637-3:** **Event-triggered** message for road hazards (sudden braking, slippery road, accident). Unlike BSM/CAM, DENMs are **asynchronous** and **geographically addressed**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are HIGH-YIELD for KTU ESE. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Free-space path loss (FSPL) | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | dB | d in **km**, f in **MHz** (valid for d > 1 m) |
| 2 | Maximum range (line-of-sight) | $R_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot t_{prop}}{2}$ | m | For DSRC one-way: $t_{prop} \leq 100~\mu s$ gives $R_{max} \approx 15$ km theoretical, but practical **~300 m** due to FCC EIRP limit of **33 dBm** |
| 3 | Received power | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - PL - L_{sys}$ | dBm | $P_t$ = transmit power, $G_t,G_r$ = antenna gains, $PL$ = path loss, $L_{sys}$ = system losses |
| 4 | SNR at receiver | $SNR = P_r - N_0 - N_f - 10\log_{10}(BW)$ | dB | $N_0 = -174$ dBm/Hz (thermal noise floor), $N_f$ = noise figure (typ. **9 dB** for OBU) |
| 5 | Message cycle time | $T_{cycle} = \dfrac{1}{f_{BSM}}$ | s | For BSM: $f_{BSM} = 10~\text{Hz} \Rightarrow T_{cycle} = 100~\text{ms}$ |
| 6 | Information age (latency) | $t_{age} = t_{rx} - t_{tx} + t_{proc}$ | ms | Sum of propagation, queueing, and processing |
| 7 | Time-To-Collision (TTC) | $TTC = \dfrac{\Delta d}{v_{rel}}$ | s | Δd = inter-vehicle gap, $v_{rel}$ = relative velocity |
| 8 | Channel bandwidth allocation | $BW_{total} = N_{channels} \times BW_{channel}$ | MHz | CCH = **1 channel × 10 MHz**, SCH = **6 channels × 10 MHz** in DSRC |
| 9 | Shannon channel capacity | $C = BW \times \log_2(1 + SNR)$ | bps | Theoretical upper bound on data rate |
| 10 | Energy per BSM | $E_{BSM} = P_t \cdot T_{packet}$ | Joules | For $P_t = 100$ mW, $T_{packet} = 500~\mu s$: $E_{BSM} = 50~\mu J$ |
| 11 | Channel busy ratio (CBR) | $CBR = \dfrac{\sum T_{busy}}{T_{observation}}$ | dimensionless | Metric for congestion control; threshold **~0.65** triggers decentralized congestion control (DCC) |
| 12 | Maximum supported relative velocity | $v_{rel,max} = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta f_{Doppler}}{2 f_c}$ | m/s | For $f_c = 5.9$ GHz, $\Delta f_{Doppler} = \pm 750$ Hz: $v_{rel,max} \approx 76$ m/s ≈ **274 km/h** |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

V2X is not merely a research curiosity. It is mandated or strongly incentivized by:
- **US NHTSA** (proposed mandate for V2V in light vehicles, 2023+ era).
- **EU Directive 2010/40/EU** and the **C-ITS Deployment Plan** (cooperative services deployment by 2025).
- **China C-V2X national standard** (YDT 3340-2018), mandatory in new vehicles under **Intelligent Connected Vehicle (ICV) roadmap**.
- **Mercedes-Benz Drive Pilot (Level 3)** uses V2X for autonomous lane changes and intersection negotiation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Reliable Communication Range for DSRC OBU

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A DSRC On-Board Unit (OBU) transmits at **$P_t = 20$ dBm** with antenna gain **$G_t = 3$ dBi**. The receiver OBU has antenna gain **$G_r = 3$ dBi** and noise figure **$N_f = 9$ dB**. System losses **$L_{sys} = 5$ dB**. Operating frequency **$f = 5.9$ GHz**, channel bandwidth **$BW = 10$ MHz**. The minimum SNR required for QPSK-1/2 decoding is **$SNR_{min} = 9.5$ dB**. Determine the **maximum reliable communication range** under free-space LOS conditions.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the thermal noise floor in the channel bandwidth:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_0 &= -174~\text{dBm/Hz} \\
BW &= 10~\text{MHz} = 10^{7}~\text{Hz} \\
10\log_{10}(BW) &= 10\log_{10}(10^{7}) = 70~\text{dB-Hz} \\
\text{Noise power (no NF)} &= N_0 + 10\log_{10}(BW) = -174 + 70 = -104~\text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means the receiver sees a noise floor of **−104 dBm** before considering the noise figure.

**Step 2 — Include the receiver noise figure to get effective noise floor:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{eff} &= \text{Noise power} + N_f \\
N_{eff} &= -104 + 9 = -95~\text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the minimum required received power for reliable decoding:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{r,min} &= N_{eff} + SNR_{min} \\
P_{r,min} &= -95 + 9.5 = -85.5~\text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the link budget equation to find maximum allowed path loss:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{r,min} &= P_t + G_t + G_r - PL_{max} - L_{sys} \\
PL_{max} &= P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{sys} - P_{r,min} \\
PL_{max} &= 20 + 3 + 3 - 5 - (-85.5) \\
PL_{max} &= 21 + 85.5 = 106.5~\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

The radio channel can tolerate up to **106.5 dB** of path loss before the link breaks.

**Step 5 — Use the free-space path loss formula and solve for distance $d$ (in km):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
PL_{max} &= 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44 \\
106.5 &= 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(5900) + 32.44
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\log_{10}(5900) \approx 3.7709$, we get $20 \times 3.7709 = 75.42$ dB.

$$
\begin{aligned}
106.5 &= 20\log_{10}(d) + 75.42 + 32.44 \\
106.5 &= 20\log_{10}(d) + 107.86 \\
20\log_{10}(d) &= 106.5 - 107.86 = -1.36 \\
\log_{10}(d) &= \dfrac{-1.36}{20} = -0.068 \\
d &= 10^{-0.068} \approx 0.854~\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Maximum reliable LOS range ≈ **854 m** (theoretical free-space; in practice with multipath, foliage, and blockage, this drops to **~300 m** as observed in field deployments).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time-To-Collision (TTC) Computation for V2V Warning

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Two vehicles (V_ego and V_target) are approaching an intersection. V_ego is at distance **$d_{ego} = 80$ m** from the intersection, traveling at **$v_{ego} = 15$ m/s** (54 km/h). V_target is at **$d_{tar} = 120$ m** on a perpendicular road, traveling at **$v_{tar} = 20$ m/s** (72 km/h) directly toward the intersection. Both will continue at constant velocity unless warned. Determine: **(a)** the TTC for each vehicle, **(b)** the minimum required latency for a V2V warning system to prevent collision.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (a) — Time-To-Collision for each vehicle:**

The Time-To-Collision (TTC) is the time it will take the vehicle to reach the common conflict point (the intersection center), assuming constant velocity.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TTC_{ego} &= \dfrac{d_{ego}}{v_{ego}} = \dfrac{80}{15} = 5.33~\text{s} \\
TTC_{tar} &= \dfrac{d_{tar}}{v_{tar}} = \dfrac{120}{20} = 6.00~\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **gap between arrivals** at the intersection is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_{arrival} &= \vert TTC_{tar} - TTC_{ego} \vert = \vert 6.00 - 5.33 \vert = 0.67~\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since this gap is **less than 1.0 s**, a collision is highly likely without intervention. **Warning must be issued.**

**Step (b) — Minimum required V2V latency:**

Assume the driver reaction time is **$t_{react} = 1.5$ s** and brake application produces a deceleration of **$a = 3$ m/s²** (mild braking). The safe stopping distance for V_ego at 15 m/s:
$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{stop} &= v_{ego} \cdot t_{react} + \dfrac{v_{ego}^{2}}{2a} \\
d_{stop} &= 15 \times 1.5 + \dfrac{15^{2}}{2 \times 3} \\
d_{stop} &= 22.5 + 37.5 = 60~\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

V_ego must begin braking no later than when it is **$d_{stop} = 60$ m** from the intersection. Therefore, the warning must reach the driver when V_ego is at:
$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{warn} &= d_{ego} - d_{stop} = 80 - 60 = 20~\text{m} \\
\text{Corresponding time to intersection at warning:} \quad t_{warn} &= \dfrac{d_{warn}}{v_{ego}} = \dfrac{20}{15} = 1.33~\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

But the V2V message must be received **even earlier** to allow for:
- Wireless transmission latency
- OBU processing
- HMI rendering
- Driver perception (~**0.5 s** total system overhead)

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{warn,actual} &= 1.33 + 0.5 = 1.83~\text{s before intersection} \\
\text{Therefore, max allowed V2X end-to-end latency} &= 1.83 - t_{react,partial~adjustment} \\
\text{Conservative engineering target:} \quad & \boxed{\,t_{latency} \leq 100~\text{ms}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is why the **3GPP eV2X standard mandates < 100 ms (and < 20 ms for L4 autonomy)**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — BSM Throughput & Channel Load Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A 4-lane urban highway has a vehicle density of **$\rho = 100$ vehicles/km/lane**. Each vehicle broadcasts a BSM of **$L_{BSM} = 400$ bytes** at **$f_{BSM} = 10$ Hz**. Compute: **(a)** total BSM bytes generated per second per km² of roadway, **(b)** total RF load in Mbps for a 4-lane 1-km stretch.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (a) — Total BSM generation rate per vehicle:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{per~vehicle} &= L_{BSM} \times f_{BSM} \\
R_{per~vehicle} &= 400 \times 8 \times 10 = 32{,}000~\text{bits/s} = 32~\text{kbps per vehicle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total vehicles in 1 km × 4 lanes:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{vehicles} &= \rho \times N_{lanes} \times L = 100 \times 4 \times 1 = 400~\text{vehicles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total aggregate data rate on the channel:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{total} &= N_{vehicles} \times R_{per~vehicle} \\
R_{total} &= 400 \times 32{,}000 = 12{,}800{,}000~\text{bps} = 12.8~\text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b) — Channel capacity check using Shannon:**

DSRC uses **10 MHz** channel bandwidth with typical spectral efficiency **~2.7 bps/Hz** (QPSK-3/4). Achievable throughput ≈ **27 Mbps**. Therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Channel utilization} &= \dfrac{R_{total}}{C_{channel}} = \dfrac{12.8}{27} \approx 47.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** At ~47% channel load, the network is heavily congested. Above **~65% load**, the Channel Busy Ratio (CBR) triggers **Decentralized Congestion Control (DCC)**, which reduces BSM rate to maintain link reliability. This is why V2X congestion control algorithms (e.g., **LIMERIC, PULSAR, ETSI DCC**) are critical research topics.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication. List its five sub-categories with one application each.**

**Model Answer:**
V2X is a wireless communication framework enabling a vehicle to exchange real-time safety and operational data with surrounding entities (vehicles, infrastructure, pedestrians, networks, and the grid) over the **5.9 GHz ITS band**.

| Sub-category | Application |
|---|---|
| **V2V** | Forward collision warning, cooperative adaptive cruise control |
| **V2I** | Traffic signal phase timing (SPaT), green-wave speed advisory |
| **V2P** | Smartphone-based pedestrian crossing alert |
| **V2N** | Real-time HD map download for autonomous driving |
| **V2G** | Bidirectional EV charging, demand-response load balancing |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for tabulated list with applications)*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Compare DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) and C-V2X (3GPP) on the basis of (i) underlying technology, (ii) latency, (iii) dependency on cellular network.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) | C-V2X (3GPP Release 14+) |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Underlying technology | Wi-Fi derivative, OFDM, CSMA/CA | LTE/5G NR, SC-FDM, scheduled or autonomous sidelink |
| (ii) Latency | ~4 ms MAC layer, ~50 ms end-to-end | ~5–20 ms (5G URLLC target: <5 ms) |
| (iii) Cellular dependency | None — fully ad-hoc peer-to-peer | Mode 3 requires network; Mode 4 (PC5) is ad-hoc |
| Frequency | 5.85–5.925 GHz (70 MHz) | 5.9 GHz (PC5) + sub-6 GHz (Uu) |

*(3 marks: 1 per parameter)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. You must answer **either Question A or Question B**. Each sub-part is typically **7 marks**. Mark distribution follows **CO-mapping** and **Bloom's Taxonomy L3–L5**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Part (a)** — **7 Marks:** Explain the architecture of a V2X communication system with a neat block diagram. Describe the functions of the **On-Board Unit (OBU)** and **Roadside Unit (RSU)**.

**Model Answer:**

The V2X architecture consists of three major domains: **Vehicle Domain, Wireless Domain, and Infrastructure Domain**.

**On-Board Unit (OBU) Functions:**
1. **Data acquisition** from in-vehicle CAN bus: vehicle speed, brake status, steering angle, GPS coordinates, heading.
2. **BSM generation** every **100 ms** containing kinematic state.
3. **Wireless transmission/reception** on the 5.9 GHz ITS band via dedicated antenna.
4. **Security:** Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm (ECDSA) signing of each BSM for authentication.
5. **Threat assessment** by running safety applications (e.g., TTC computation).
6. **HMI actuation** — warning the driver via display, buzzer, or seat vibration.
7. **Privacy management** — rotating pseudonymous certificates every 5 minutes.

**Roadside Unit (RSU) Functions:**
1. Fixed infrastructure mounted on traffic signals, lamp posts, or overhead gantries.
2. **Relays V2X messages** between vehicles and the traffic management center.
3. Broadcasts **SPaT (Signal Phase and Timing)** and **MAP (road topology)** messages continuously.
4. Acts as an **intermediate node** for V2I services and internet gateway.
5. Powered by mains electricity or solar with battery backup.

**Block Diagram (described in text):** Vehicle Domain {OBU, GPS, CAN bus, HMI} → Wireless Domain {5.9 GHz RF channel} → Infrastructure Domain {RSU, Traffic Controller, Cloud Server, V2G Grid}.

*(7 marks: 2 for architecture description + 2 for OBU functions + 2 for RSU functions + 1 for diagram)*

---

**Part (b)** — **7 Marks:** With reference to **C-V2X**, explain the **PC5 (sidelink)** and **Uu (uplink/downlink)** interfaces. Discuss two key advantages of C-V2X over DSRC.

**Model Answer:**

**PC5 Interface (Sidelink):**
- Direct **device-to-device** communication between vehicles **without requiring cellular network coverage**.
- Operates on the **5.9 GHz ITS band** (same as DSRC) for backward compatibility.
- Supports **two resource allocation modes:**
  - **Mode 3 (Network-scheduled):** Base station (eNodeB/gNodeB) allocates sidelink resources centrally.
  - **Mode 4 (Autonomous):** Vehicle autonomously selects radio resources using **sensing-based semi-persistent scheduling (SPS)** with no network dependency.
- Designed for **latency-critical safety messages** (BSM, CAM, DENM).

**Uu Interface (Up/Downlink):**
- Traditional **cellular communication** between vehicle (UE) and base station.
- Used for **non-safety V2N services**: HD map streaming, software OTA updates, fleet management, infotainment.
- Leverages existing **4G/5G infrastructure** for wide-area coverage.
- Higher latency than PC5 but supports **high-bandwidth applications**.

**Two Advantages of C-V2X over DSRC:**

1. **Superior spectral efficiency and range:** C-V2X uses **SC-FDM** with advanced receiver algorithms (Turbo decoding) achieving **~500 m reliable range** vs DSRC's ~300 m. With **5G NR-V2X**, range can exceed **1 km** using beamforming.

2. **Evolution path to 5G:** C-V2X has a **clear roadmap from LTE-V2X → 5G NR-V2X**, gaining **URLLC** (<5 ms latency), **eMBB** (high throughput for sensor sharing), and **mMTC** (massive vehicle density). DSRC is a **mature but stagnant** standard with no roadmap beyond IEEE 802.11p.

*(7 marks: 2 for PC5 + 2 for Uu + 1.5 per advantage)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Alternate]**

**Part (a)** — **7 Marks:** Describe the **Basic Safety Message (BSM)** as defined in **SAE J2735**. What are the mandatory (Part I) and optional (Part II) fields? Why is a **10 Hz transmission rate** chosen?

**Model Answer:**

The **BSM (Basic Safety Message)** is the core periodic broadcast message in SAE J2735 (US DSRC standard). It is generated and transmitted by every vehicle's OBU to enable cooperative awareness.

**Part I — Mandatory Fields (always transmitted):**
1. **MessageID** — identifies the message as BSM (value = 2).
2. **MsgCount** — 3-bit counter (0–127) for duplicate detection.
3. **TemporaryID** — randomly rotating pseudonymous certificate for privacy.
4. **DSecond** — timestamp in seconds within the current minute.
5. **Latitude, Longitude, Elevation** — WGS-84 GPS coordinates (1/10 micro-degree resolution).
6. **Speed** — vehicle speed in 0.02 m/s units.
7. **Heading** — direction of travel in 0.0125° units.
8. **Acceleration (4-way)** — longitudinal and lateral acceleration.
9. **Brake System Status** — applied, available, parking brake.
10. **Vehicle Size** — length and width for path prediction.

**Part II — Optional Fields (transmitted less frequently or when triggered):**
- Path history (recent GPS trace).
- Path prediction (future predicted path).
- Exterior lights status (turn signals, hazard).
- Yaw rate, steering angle.
- Event flags (hard braking, airbag deployed).

**Why 10 Hz (100 ms cycle)?**
- **Nyquist sampling** of vehicle dynamics: at highway speeds (~30 m/s), a vehicle moves **3 m per 100 ms**. This is the minimum granularity needed for accurate **path prediction** and **collision avoidance**.
- **Human reaction time budget:** The driver needs the message **at least every 100 ms** for the safety application's threat assessment to remain valid (TTC computation refresh).
- **Channel capacity:** At 10 Hz × 400 bytes, the load is manageable (32 kbps per vehicle) without saturating the 10 MHz channel.
- **Standard consensus:** Empirical studies by USDOT CAMP consortium showed that 10 Hz offers the **optimal trade-off** between situational awareness and channel congestion.

*(7 marks: 2 for mandatory fields + 2 for optional fields + 3 for 10 Hz justification)*

---

**Part (b)** — **7 Marks:** A DSRC OBU transmits at **20 dBm** with antenna gain **2 dBi**. The receiver OBU has antenna gain **2 dBi** and noise figure **10 dB**. Channel bandwidth is **10 MHz**, operating frequency **5.9 GHz**, system losses **4 dB**, and minimum required SNR **9 dB**. Calculate the **maximum reliable line-of-sight range** using the free-space path loss model.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Noise floor in 10 MHz bandwidth:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{floor} &= -174~\text{dBm/Hz} + 10\log_{10}(10^{7}) \\
&= -174 + 70 = -104~\text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective noise with receiver noise figure:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{eff} &= -104 + 10 = -94~\text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum required received power:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{r,min} &= N_{eff} + SNR_{min} = -94 + 9 = -85~\text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum tolerable path loss (link budget):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
PL_{max} &= P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{sys} - P_{r,min} \\
&= 20 + 2 + 2 - 4 - (-85) = 105~\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve free-space path loss for distance (d in km, f in MHz):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
PL_{max} &= 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44 \\
20\log_{10}(5900) &= 20 \times 3.7709 = 75.42~\text{dB} \\
105 &= 20\log_{10}(d) + 75.42 + 32.44 \\
20\log_{10}(d) &= 105 - 107.86 = -2.86 \\
\log_{10}(d) &= -0.143 \\
d &= 10^{-0.143} \approx 0.719~\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $d_{max} \approx 719~\text{m} \approx 0.72~\text{km}$

*(7 marks: 1 per step, 2 marks for final numerical answer with units)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in V2X Questions — Read Before Writing Your Exam:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mistake in FSPL Formula:** The free-space path loss formula $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ **requires d in km and f in MHz**. Students frequently plug in meters or GHz and lose **2–3 marks** silently.
>
> 2. **Confusing BSM, CAM, and DENM:** BSM is **SAE (USA/DSRC)** terminology; CAM is **ETSI (Europe/ITS-G5)**. Do not use them interchangeably. DENMs are **event-triggered**, not periodic — this distinction is worth **2 marks** in any comparative question.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to add Noise Figure:** When computing receiver sensitivity, students often compute $-174 + 10\log_{10}(BW)$ but forget to add the **noise figure $N_f$**, resulting in an overestimated range by **~3 dB** (≈ factor of 1.4× error in distance).
>
> 4. **Mixing PC5 and Uu:** PC5 is **sidelink (direct V2V/V2I)**, Uu is **cellular up/downlink**. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 5. **Wrong Frequency Band:** V2X uses **5.9 GHz ITS band** in almost all regions. Writing "2.4 GHz Wi-Fi" or "5 GHz Wi-Fi" loses **2 marks** immediately as it indicates fundamental misunderstanding.
>
> 6. **Ignoring units in final answer:** Always quote **distance in meters**, **latency in milliseconds**, and **power in dBm/dBW**. Naked numbers without units = **0.5 mark deduction**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision — Memorize These 12 Bullet Points Before Entering the Exam Hall:**
>
> - V2X operates on the **5.9 GHz** ITS band, with **~70 MHz bandwidth** (5.85–5.925 GHz).
> - V2X has **5 sub-categories**: V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N, V2G.
> - **DSRC** = IEEE 802.11p / IEEE 1609 stack, **Wi-Fi derivative**, fully ad-hoc.
> - **C-V2X** = 3GPP LTE/5G, has **PC5 (sidelink)** and **Uu (uplink/downlink)** interfaces.
> - **BSM** (SAE J2735) is transmitted at **10 Hz**, size **~400 bytes**, contains Part I (mandatory) + Part II (optional).
> - **CAM** (ETSI EN 302 637-2) is the European counterpart of BSM, triggered by movement thresholds (1–10 Hz).
> - **DENM** (ETSI EN 302 637-3) is **event-triggered** (asynchronous), used for hazards like sudden braking or road accidents.
> - End-to-end V2X latency target is **< 100 ms** (3GPP eV2X: **< 20 ms** for Level 3+ autonomy).
> - Free-space path loss: $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44$ dB.
> - Practical DSRC range **~300 m**; C-V2X range **~500 m+**; 5G NR-V2X can exceed **1 km**.
> - **Channel Busy Ratio (CBR) threshold** for congestion control: **~0.65**.
> - **Time-To-Collision (TTC)** is the core safety metric: $TTC = \Delta d / v_{rel}$.
